topics.title: Watershed - Introduction and Characteristics. Watershed Development - Problems and Prospects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET741 - WATERSHED DEVELOPMENT AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Watershed - Introduction and Characteristics. Watershed Development - Problems and Prospects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a Watershed

A **Watershed** (also called a **Catchment**, **Drainage Basin**, or **River Basin**) is a **geo-hydrological unit** of land that captures precipitation, surface runoff, and subsurface flow, and discharges it through a common outlet such as a river, stream, lake, or reservoir. Every parcel of land on Earth belongs to some watershed, no matter how small.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (FAO / ISRO Standard):**
> *"A watershed is a topographically delineated area bounded by a ridge line (water divide) such that all precipitation falling within it drains through a single outlet."*

The boundary of a watershed is called the **Watershed Divide**, **Catchment Boundary**, or **Ridge Line**. It separates one drainage system from another and is identifiable on a topo-sheet as the line connecting the highest elevations around the basin.

### Sub-Types of Watersheds (Hierarchical Classification)

| Hierarchical Level | Local Name | Typical Area |
|---|---|---|
| Micro-watershed | Sub-micro | < 100 ha |
| Mini-watershed | Nala basin | 100 – 1000 ha |
| Milli-watershed | Small river basin | 10 – 100 km² |
| Sub-watershed | Tributary basin | 100 – 1000 km² |
| Macro-watershed | Major river basin | > 1000 km² |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For Module 1, the focus is on understanding the **watershed as a basic planning unit** for soil and water conservation, NOT on the engineering hydraulics of dams.

## 1.2 Watershed Characteristics — Introduction

The **behaviour** of a watershed (how much runoff it produces, how fast, how much sediment it carries) is governed by three inter-related families of characteristics:

1. **Drainage / Morphometric Characteristics** — size, shape, slope, drainage network.
2. **Climatic Characteristics** — rainfall (intensity, duration, frequency), temperature, evapotranspiration.
3. **Physiographic / Land Characteristics** — soil type, geology, vegetation cover, land use.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Funnel of a Bath-Tub"

Imagine your bathroom as a mini-watershed. The **ridge line** is the edge of the bath-tub where water would spill over into the next room. The **drain hole** is the **outlet**. Whatever water (rainfall) you pour inside stays inside and exits ONLY through the drain — it cannot leave sideways because the rim is higher.

- A **steep-sided funnel** = a **steep, compact watershed** → water exits fast, less infiltration, more floods.
- A **wide, shallow basin** = a **flat, elongated watershed** → water exits slowly, more infiltration, more recharge.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Watershed characteristics are essentially the "geographical fingerprint" that determines how a piece of land responds to rainfall — its hydrologic personality.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical / Morphometric Characteristics of a Watershed

These are measurable geometric parameters that completely describe the **shape, size, and drainage fabric** of a watershed. They are the **primary topic** for KTU Module 1 calculations.

### (a) Watershed Area ($A$)

The total horizontal projection of the land surface enclosed by the watershed boundary. **Largest single property** that controls runoff volume.

- **Unit:** hectares (ha) or km²
- **Influences:** Peak runoff, total water yield, sediment load.

### (b) Watershed Shape

Shape controls the **time of concentration** (how fast runoff reaches the outlet). Two extreme shapes and a real-world formula:

- **Fan-shaped (compact)** → fast response → sharp flood peaks.
- **Elongated (fern-leaf)** → slow response → flatter, sustained flow.

The **Shape Factor** and **Compactness Coefficient** quantify this.

### (c) Watershed Slope ($S$)

Slope governs **runoff velocity, infiltration opportunity, and erosion intensity**. Measured from the topo-sheet using contour spacings.

- **Steep slope** → high runoff, low infiltration, more soil erosion.
- **Mild slope** → more infiltration, more groundwater recharge.

### (d) Drainage Density ($D_d$)

The **total length of streams** per unit area of the watershed. Higher $D_d$ implies a well-drained, impermeable, fine-textured terrain.

### (e) Stream Order ($u$)

Horton's stream ordering: the smallest unbranched fingertip stream is Order 1; when two of the same order meet, the order increments by 1. The highest order stream defines the **order of the watershed**.

### (f) Bifurcation Ratio ($R_b$)

Ratio of the number of streams of one order to the number of streams of the next higher order. Indicates the degree of **dendritic branching**.

### (g) Length of the Main Channel ($L$)

The longest continuous watercourse from the headwater to the outlet. Used in time-of-concentration formulas.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU Module 1 — memorize the symbols, units, and the **physical meaning** of each.

| Parameter | Formula (LaTeX) | Definition of Terms | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Drainage Density** $D_d$ | $D_d \;=\; \dfrac{\Sigma L_u}{A}$ | $\Sigma L_u$ = total stream length of all orders; $A$ = area | km/km² |
| **Stream Frequency** $F_s$ | $F_s \;=\; \dfrac{\Sigma N_u}{A}$ | $\Sigma N_u$ = total number of streams | per km² |
| **Bifurcation Ratio** $R_b$ | $R_b \;=\; \dfrac{N_u}{N_{u+1}}$ | $N_u$ = no. of streams of order $u$ | dimensionless |
| **Compactness Coefficient** $C_c$ | $C_c \;=\; \dfrac{P}{2\sqrt{\pi A}}$ | $P$ = perimeter; $A$ = area | dimensionless |
| **Form Factor** $F_f$ | $F_f \;=\; \dfrac{A}{L_b^{\,2}}$ | $L_b$ = basin length | dimensionless |
| **Circularity Ratio** $R_c$ | $R_c \;=\; \dfrac{4\pi A}{P^{2}}$ | $P$ = perimeter; $A$ = area | dimensionless |
| **Elongation Ratio** $R_e$ | $R_e \;=\; \dfrac{2\sqrt{A/\pi}}{L_b}$ | $L_b$ = basin length | dimensionless |
| **Relief Ratio** $R_r$ | $R_r \;=\; \dfrac{H}{L_b}$ | $H$ = relief (max−min elevation) | dimensionless |
| **Ruggedness Number** $R_n$ | $R_n \;=\; D_d \times H$ | product of drainage density and relief | dimensionless |
| **Length of Overland Flow** $L_o$ | $L_o \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2 D_d}$ | inverse of twice the drainage density | km |
| **Horton's Law (Stream Number)** | $N_u \;=\; R_b^{\,(\Omega - u)}$ | $\Omega$ = basin order; $u$ = order | dimensionless |
| **Horton's Law (Stream Length)** | $\bar{L}_u \;=\; \bar{L}_1 \, R_L^{\,(u-1)}$ | $R_L$ = length ratio | km |
| **Time of Concentration** $T_c$ (Kirpich) | $T_c \;=\; 0.01947 \, L^{0.77} \, S^{-0.385}$ | $L$ in m; $S$ = slope (m/m); $T_c$ in min | min |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These characteristics are not academic curiosities — they drive every watershed project:

- **Urban Hydrology** — $T_c$ determines the design rainfall duration for storm drains.
- **Dam / Spillway Design** — drainage density and relief ratio feed into the **Rational Method** and **SCS-CN** runoff estimation.
- **Soil Conservation** — areas with high ruggedness numbers ($R_n > 0.4$) require **bench terraces** or **contour bunds**.
- **Flood Management** — compact (low $F_f$) watersheds need **detention basins**.
- **Groundwater Recharge** — low $D_d$, high infiltration soils are preferred for **check-dams** and **recharge shafts**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Drainage Density, Bifurcation Ratio & Horton's Law

### Problem Statement

A 4th-order ($\Omega = 4$) dendritic watershed has the following morphometric data extracted from a Survey of India topo-sheet:

| Stream Order ($u$) | No. of Streams ($N_u$) | Avg. Length per Stream ($\bar{L}_u$) in km |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 60 | 1.20 |
| 2 | 14 | 3.50 |
| 3 | 4 | 8.00 |
| 4 | 1 | 14.00 |

The total watershed area $A$ = **42 km²**. Compute:
1. Drainage Density $D_d$
2. Bifurcation Ratio $R_b$ (for each successive pair)
3. Stream Frequency $F_s$
4. Average Bifurcation Ratio $\overline{R_b}$
5. Length Ratio $R_L$
6. Horton's predicted $N_4$ and compare with observed.

---

### Step 1 — Total Stream Length

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma L_u &= N_1 \bar{L}_1 + N_2 \bar{L}_2 + N_3 \bar{L}_3 + N_4 \bar{L}_4 \\
&= (60)(1.20) + (14)(3.50) + (4)(8.00) + (1)(14.00) \\
&= 72.00 + 49.00 + 32.00 + 14.00 \\
&= 167.00 \;\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Total length = (count of streams) × (mean length) summed over every order.

---

### Step 2 — Drainage Density $D_d$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_d &= \frac{\Sigma L_u}{A} = \frac{167.00 \;\text{km}}{42 \;\text{km}^2} \\
&\approx 3.976 \;\text{km/km}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** A $D_d$ of ~4 km/km² is **moderately high**, indicating moderately permeable terrain with well-developed drainage — typical of peninsular India.

---

### Step 3 — Bifurcation Ratios (each pair)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{b\,(1\!-\!2)} &= \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{60}{14} \approx 4.286 \\
R_{b\,(2\!-\!3)} &= \frac{N_2}{N_3} = \frac{14}{4} = 3.500 \\
R_{b\,(3\!-\!4)} &= \frac{N_3}{N_4} = \frac{4}{1} = 4.000
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Average Bifurcation Ratio $\overline{R_b}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{R_b} &= \frac{R_{b(1-2)} + R_{b(2-3)} + R_{b(3-4)}}{3} \\
&= \frac{4.286 + 3.500 + 4.000}{3} \\
&= \frac{11.786}{3} \\
&\approx 3.929
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Average $R_b \approx 3.93$ lies in the **normal dendritic range (3–5)** per Strahler. No structural or geological distortion is suspected.

---

### Step 5 — Stream Frequency $F_s$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma N_u &= 60 + 14 + 4 + 1 = 79 \\
F_s &= \frac{\Sigma N_u}{A} = \frac{79}{42} \\
&\approx 1.881 \;\text{streams/km}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 6 — Horton's Law Verification

**Horton's Law of Stream Numbers:**
$$
N_u \;=\; R_b^{\,(\Omega - u)}
$$

Take $R_b = \overline{R_b} \approx 3.929$ and $\Omega = 4$.

**Predicted** $N_4$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_4^{\,(\text{pred})} &= R_b^{\,(\Omega - u)} = (3.929)^{\,(4-4)} = (3.929)^{0} \\
&= 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Observed** $N_4 = 1$. Perfect agreement with Horton — confirms a **geologically homogeneous, undisturbed** watershed.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation of Key Ratios (Exhaustive Reference)

| Parameter | Symbol | Range | Interpretation | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bifurcation Ratio** | $R_b$ | 2 – 3 | Flat / rolling terrain | Low peak flow |
| | | 3 – 5 | Normal dendritic (typical) | Standard design assumptions valid |
| | | > 5 | Steep, elongated, structurally controlled | Use elongated-basin runoff formulae |
| **Drainage Density** | $D_d$ | < 1 | Highly permeable / resistant rock | High infiltration, low runoff |
| | | 1 – 3 | Moderate permeability | Mixed response |
| | | > 5 | Impervious, weak rock | Flashy runoff, high erosion risk |
| **Form Factor** | $F_f$ | < 0.30 | Elongated basin | Lower peak discharge |
| | | > 0.45 | Compact / fan-shaped | Higher peak discharge |
| **Compactness Coefficient** | $C_c$ | 1.0 | Perfect circle | Fastest concentration time |
| | | 1.0 – 1.5 | Near-circular | Short lag time |
| | | > 1.5 | Highly irregular | Long, dispersed flow paths |
| **Relief Ratio** | $R_r$ | < 0.05 | Low relief | Low erosion potential |
| | | 0.05 – 0.30 | Moderate relief | Standard erosion control |
| | | > 0.30 | Steep terrain | High erosion, prioritise terracing |
| **Ruggedness Number** | $R_n$ | < 0.10 | Low relief & drainage | Stable landscape |
| | | 0.10 – 0.40 | Moderate | Manageable with standard BMPs |
| | | > 0.40 | Highly rugged | Needs intensive structural measures |
| **Elongation Ratio** | $R_e$ | 0.6 – 1.0 | Circular | Higher peak flow |
| | | < 0.6 | Elongated | Lower peak, longer $T_c$ |
| **Circularity Ratio** | $R_c$ | 0.4 – 0.5 | Normal mature basin | Balanced hydrology |
| | | > 0.7 | Nearly circular | Highly flood-prone |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Watershed as a Hydrological System — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rainfall Input P] --> B[Canopy Interception Ic]
    B --> C[Throughfall + Stemflow]
    C --> D[Surface Depression Storage Dp]
    D --> E[Infiltration I]
    E --> F[Soil Moisture Storage S]
    F --> G[Groundwater Recharge R]
    G --> H[Baseflow to Stream]
    A --> I[Direct Runoff Qd]
    D --> I
    I --> J[Main Channel Flow]
    H --> J
    J --> K[Outlet / Catchment Yield]
    F --> L[Evapotranspiration ET]
    A --> L
    K --> M[Watershed Boundary controlled by Ridge Line]
    L --> M
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Every arrow leaving the watershed boundary would represent a hydrological loss (ET, deep percolation outside basin). The only net **outflow** at the outlet is what remains after interception, infiltration, ET, and storage.

---

## 4.2 Hierarchical Ordering of Streams (Horton-Strahler)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1A[Order 1 stream tip] --> M1[Meeting of two order 1]
    O1B[Order 1 stream tip] --> M1
    O1C[Order 1 stream tip] --> M2[Meeting of two order 1]
    O1D[Order 1 stream tip] --> M2
    M1 --> P1[Junction Order 1 + Order 1 gives Order 2]
    M2 --> P1
    O1E[Order 1 stream tip] --> M3[Two order 1 streams]
    O1F[Order 1 stream tip] --> M3
    M3 --> P2[Junction Order 2 + Order 1 stays Order 2]
    P1 --> P2
    O2A[Order 2 stream] --> P3[Junction Order 2 + Order 2 gives Order 3]
    O2B[Order 2 stream] --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> O3[Order 3 main channel outlet]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Note carefully — a junction between Order 2 and Order 1 remains Order 2. Only **same-order** junctions increment the order.

---

## 4.3 Watershed Development — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Problem Identification] --> P2[Baseline Survey]
    P2 --> P3[Morphometric Analysis]
    P3 --> P4[Hydrological Assessment]
    P4 --> P5[Land Capability Classification]
    P5 --> P6[Plan Formulation]
    P6 --> P7[Entry Point Activities EPA]
    P7 --> P8[Soil and Moisture Conservation SMC]
    P8 --> P9[Afforestation and Pasture Development]
    P9 --> P10[Agro-Horti Forestry]
    P10 --> P11[Monitoring and Evaluation]
    P11 --> P12[Sustainability and Maintenance]
    P12 --> P1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Watershed development is **cyclical and adaptive**. After evaluation, learnings feed back into the next planning cycle.

---

## 4.4 Watershed Problems vs Prospects — Classification Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ProbBlock[Problems]
        PR1[Soil Erosion]
        PR2[Flash Floods]
        PR3[Drought]
        PR4[Sedimentation of Tanks]
        PR5[Land Degradation]
        PR6[Deforestation]
    end
    subgraph ProsBlock[Prospects Solutions]
        SO1[Contour Bunding and Terracing]
        SO2[Check Dams and Recharge Shafts]
        SO3[Water Harvesting Structures]
        SO4[Desilting of Tanks]
        SO5[Composting and Soil Amendments]
        SO6[Social Forestry Plantations]
    end
    PR1 --> SO1
    PR2 --> SO2
    PR3 --> SO3
    PR4 --> SO4
    PR5 --> SO5
    PR6 --> SO6
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
Define a watershed. Explain the concept of a water divide with a neat sketch description.

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**

A **watershed** is a topographically delineated land area within which all precipitation that falls drains through a single, common outlet such as a river, stream, lake, or reservoir. It is bounded on all sides by a ridge line called the **water divide** or **watershed boundary**, which separates one drainage system from another.

The water divide can be visualized on a contour map as the line connecting the highest elevations surrounding the basin — analogous to the rim of a bowl. Water falling on either side of this line flows into **different** watersheds. The **outlet** (lowest point on the boundary) is where the entire basin's runoff converges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key terms to use:** *catchment, drainage basin, water divide, ridge line, outlet.*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
Differentiate between **Drainage Density** and **Stream Frequency**. State their units and typical significance.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Drainage Density $D_d$ | Stream Frequency $F_s$ |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $\Sigma L_u / A$ | $\Sigma N_u / A$ |
| Quantity measured | Total **length** of streams | Total **number** of streams |
| Unit | km/km² | streams/km² |
| Indicates | Texture of dissection, infiltration capacity | Stream spacing and terrain dissection |
| High value means | Impermeable terrain, flashy runoff | Closely spaced streams, fine drainage |

> Both are **morphometric indices** and together describe the drainage texture of a watershed.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** Define and explain the morphological characteristics of a watershed. List any **six** characteristics with formulae. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3rd-order watershed has the following data. Compute Drainage Density, Bifurcation Ratio, Stream Frequency and Form Factor. Comment on the results. **(7 marks)**

| Order | No. of Streams $N_u$ | Avg Length $\bar{L}_u$ (km) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 40 | 1.0 |
| 2 | 10 | 3.5 |
| 3 | 2 | 8.0 |

Given: Area $A = 20 \;\text{km}^2$, Basin Length $L_b = 7.5 \;\text{km}$, Perimeter $P = 22 \;\text{km}$.

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Morphological characteristics** are measurable geometric properties that describe the **shape, drainage network, and surface form** of a watershed. Six key characteristics:

1. **Watershed Area ($A$)** — governs runoff volume.
2. **Watershed Shape** — Form Factor ($F_f = A / L_b^2$).
3. **Watershed Slope ($S$)** — affects runoff velocity.
4. **Drainage Density ($D_d = \Sigma L_u / A$)** — texture of dissection.
5. **Stream Order ($u$)** — Horton's classification of stream hierarchy.
6. **Bifurcation Ratio ($R_b = N_u / N_{u+1}$)** — branching pattern.

Other commonly cited: Relief, Relief Ratio, Ruggedness Number, Length of overland flow, Compactness Coefficient.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Total Stream Length:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma L_u &= (40)(1.0) + (10)(3.5) + (2)(8.0) \\
&= 40 + 35 + 16 = 91 \;\text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Drainage Density:**
$$
D_d = \frac{91}{20} = 4.55 \;\text{km/km}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Bifurcation Ratios:**
$$
R_{b(1-2)} = \frac{40}{10} = 4.0, \quad R_{b(2-3)} = \frac{10}{2} = 5.0
$$

$$
\overline{R_b} = \frac{4.0 + 5.0}{2} = 4.5
$$

**Step 4 — Stream Frequency:**
$$
F_s = \frac{40+10+2}{20} = \frac{52}{20} = 2.6 \;\text{streams/km}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Form Factor:**
$$
F_f = \frac{A}{L_b^{\,2}} = \frac{20}{(7.5)^2} = \frac{20}{56.25} \approx 0.356
$$

**Comments:**
- $D_d = 4.55$ → moderately high → moderately impermeable terrain.
- $\overline{R_b} = 4.5$ → in the standard dendritic range (3–5) → no structural distortion.
- $F_f = 0.356 < 0.45$ → **elongated basin** → lower peak discharge, longer time of concentration.

---

### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** Discuss the major **problems** associated with watershed degradation in India. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the major **prospects / opportunities** of watershed development programmes with examples. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a): Problems**

1. **Soil Erosion** — ~5334 million tonnes of soil lost annually (NBSS&LUP estimate). Gullies, sheet erosion reduce cultivable depth.
2. **Flash Floods & Droughts** — Loss of vegetation reduces infiltration; runoff arrives too fast → floods in monsoon, drought in summer.
3. **Reservoir Siltation** — Reduced live storage of dams; costlier desilting.
4. **Groundwater Depletion** — Recharge zone damage lowers water table.
5. **Deforestation & Biodiversity Loss** — Removal of canopy alters microclimate.
6. **Land Degradation** — Salinity, waterlogging, alkalinity in irrigated zones.
7. **Socio-economic Loss** — Displacement, rural poverty, migration.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b): Prospects / Opportunities**

1. **Soil and Moisture Conservation** — contour bunding (e.g., **Sukhomajri**, Haryana), terracing in the Western Ghats.
2. **Rainwater Harvesting** — check dams in Rajasthan (**Johlka**), percolation tanks in Telangana.
3. **Groundwater Recharge** — Recharge shafts doubling yield (example: **Marvatlge, Maharashtra**).
4. **Agro-horti-forestry** — Integrated farming systems under **MGNREGA + Watershed Development**.
5. **Drought Proofing** — Watershed programmes in **Anantapur, AP** raised crop yields by 30–60 %.
6. **Climate Resilience** — Carbon sequestration through afforestation.
7. **Livelihood Generation** — Wage employment, women's SHGs, dairying.
8. **Sustainable Rural Economy** — Convergence of MGNREGA, IWMP, PMKSY.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Drainage Density with Stream Frequency** — both use the same denominator $A$, but numerators differ (length vs. count). Examiners deduct 1 mark if formulae are swapped.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in morphometric answers — always write km, km², km/km².
> 3. **Skipping the "comment" / "interpretation" step** in numerical problems — KTU board examiners expect a one-line hydrologic interpretation of every computed ratio.
> 4. **Confusing Horton's Law of Stream Numbers** ($N_u = R_b^{\Omega - u}$) with Stream Length Law ($\bar{L}_u = \bar{L}_1 R_L^{\,u-1}$). They are different.
> 5. **In Part B, failing to draw a labelled sketch** of a watershed boundary when asked "explain with a sketch" — students lose 2 marks.
> 6. **Writing "watershed management" and "watershed development" interchangeably** — they differ: management = ongoing operation; development = one-time structural + vegetative upgrade.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Watershed** = topographically bounded land draining to a single outlet; bounded by a **water divide**.
- A watershed has three characteristic families: **Drainage / Morphometric**, **Climatic**, and **Physiographic**.
- The **six most-tested morphometric parameters**: Area, Shape (Form Factor), Slope, Drainage Density, Stream Order, Bifurcation Ratio.
- **Drainage Density** $D_d = \Sigma L_u / A$ — high $D_d$ means impermeable soil, flashy runoff, more erosion.
- **Stream Frequency** $F_s = \Sigma N_u / A$ — describes stream spacing.
- **Bifurcation Ratio** $R_b = N_u / N_{u+1}$ — typical range 3–5 for undisturbed dendritic basins.
- **Form Factor** $F_f = A / L_b^2$ — values > 0.45 = fan-shaped (flood-prone); < 0.30 = elongated.
- **Compactness Coefficient** $C_c = P / (2\sqrt{\pi A})$ — equals 1.0 for a circle.
- **Relief Ratio** $R_r = H / L_b$ — high $R_r$ (>0.30) → steep terrain → high erosion risk.
- **Ruggedness Number** $R_n = D_d \times H$ — values > 0.40 indicate need for intensive structural conservation.
- **Horton's Law of Stream Numbers:** $N_u = R_b^{\,(\Omega - u)}$.
- **Horton's Law of Stream Lengths:** $\bar{L}_u = \bar{L}_1 \cdot R_L^{\,(u-1)}$.
- **Kirpich Formula** for time of concentration: $T_c = 0.01947 \, L^{0.77} S^{-0.385}$ minutes.
- Watershed **development** is cyclical: **Planning → SMC works → Vegetative works → Monitoring → Maintenance**.
- Major problems of watershed degradation: **erosion, flash floods, siltation, groundwater depletion, deforestation**.
- Major prospects: **soil & moisture conservation, rainwater harvesting, groundwater recharge, agro-horti-forestry, drought proofing, livelihood generation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Memorize the **KTU Formula Sheet table** in Section 2 by heart. Almost every 14-mark question in Module 1 is built around morphometric calculations plus a one-line interpretation. Draw **at least one sketch** (watershed boundary or stream ordering) in any 14-mark answer — examiners reward it with **2 grace marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Investigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET741 - WATERSHED DEVELOPMENT AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Investigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Investigation" in Watershed Development?

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Investigation** in watershed development and management refers to the **systematic, multi-disciplinary process of reconnaissance, data collection, survey, and analysis** of the physical, hydrological, biological, geological, and socio-economic characteristics of a watershed to generate the technical baseline required for planning, designing, implementing, and monitoring watershed treatment structures and conservation programs.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PGAET741 - Module 1), investigation is the **first and most critical phase** of any watershed project. The quality of investigation directly determines the success or failure of all subsequent engineering interventions such as check dams, percolation tanks, contour bunds, terracing, and afforestation works.

### Key Components of Watershed Investigation

A complete watershed investigation comprises the following sub-investigations:

1. **Reconnaissance Survey** — Preliminary walk-over and visual assessment
2. **Topographical Survey** — Mapping of terrain, slopes, and elevations
3. **Soil Survey** — Classification of soil types, depth, texture, and erodibility
4. **Geological & Geomorphological Investigation** — Rock types, structural features, landforms
5. **Hydrological Investigation** — Rainfall, runoff, stream flow, and groundwater data
6. **Vegetation and Land Use Survey** — Existing flora, crop patterns, forest cover
7. **Socio-Economic Investigation** — Demographics, livelihoods, community needs
8. **Remote Sensing and GIS-based Investigation** — Satellite data and spatial analysis

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of watershed investigation like a doctor diagnosing a patient before prescribing treatment.**
> 
> Just as a doctor examines blood reports, X-rays, medical history, and lifestyle before recommending medication, a watershed engineer must "examine" the land's slopes, soils, rainfall, drainage, vegetation, and people before designing check dams, terraces, or afforestation plans.
> 
> **Skipping investigation = Prescribing medicine without diagnosis = Project failure.**

### Physical and Standard Metrics Used in Investigation

The following standard metrics form the backbone of any watershed investigation:

- **Catchment Area (A)**: Expressed in **hectares (ha)** or **square kilometres (km²)**
- **Annual Rainfall (P)**: Expressed in **millimetres (mm)** or **centimetres (cm)**
- **Slope Gradient (S)**: Expressed as **percentage (%)** or **degrees (°)**
- **Soil Erodibility Factor (K)**: Dimensionless, ranges from **0.02 to 0.69**
- **Curve Number (CN)**: Dimensionless, ranges from **30 (sandy soil, dense cover) to 98 (impervious urban)**
- **Runoff Depth (R)**: Expressed in **mm**
- **Sediment Yield**: Expressed in **tonnes/km²/year** or **ha·m/100 km²/year**

### Why Investigation is the Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Note:** Examiners frequently frame questions around the statement *"No watershed project can succeed without a thorough pre-investigation."* Students must be able to list the types of investigations and justify their importance in the context of planning, design, and sustainability.

The investigation phase serves **four primary engineering purposes**:

| Purpose | Engineering Outcome |
|---------|---------------------|
| **Baseline Data Generation** | Establishes pre-project conditions for impact assessment |
| **Design Parameter Determination** | Provides values for spillway, dam, and bund design |
| **Problem Identification** | Locates erosion hotspots, degraded lands, water scarcity zones |
| **Stakeholder Mapping** | Identifies beneficiaries, conflicts, and institutional needs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Steps of Watershed Investigation

The investigation process follows a **logical sequence** from broad to specific, often described as the **"Funnel Approach"** — starting with regional overview and progressively zooming into site-specific detail.

### Step 1: Reconnaissance Survey (Preliminary Visit)

This is the **first physical visit** to the watershed. The objective is to gain a general understanding and identify problem areas.

- Conducted by walking through the watershed
- Identify broad land use zones (forest, agricultural, barren, built-up)
- Note obvious signs of erosion (gullies, rills, sheet wash)
- Identify water bodies (streams, ponds, wells, springs)
- Assess accessibility and infrastructure
- Hold informal discussions with villagers

> [!NOTE]
> **Reconnaissance is qualitative, not quantitative.** No precise measurements are taken at this stage.

### Step 2: Collection of Secondary Data

Before detailed field work, existing data is gathered from government agencies:

- **Rainfall Data**: From IMD (India Meteorological Department) stations
- **Toposheets**: From Survey of India (SOI) on 1:50,000 or 1:25,000 scale
- **Soil Maps**: From NBSS&LUP (National Bureau of Soil Survey & Land Use Planning)
- **Census Data**: From District Census Handbook
- **Groundwater Data**: From Central Ground Water Board (CGWB)
- **Forest Cover Data**: From Forest Survey of India (FSI)

### Step 3: Topographical Survey

The watershed boundaries and terrain features are precisely mapped using:

- **Theodolite** for angular measurements
- **Total Station** for combined angle and distance
- **Dumpy Level** for elevation differences
- **GPS / DGPS** for geo-referencing
- **Drones / UAVs** for aerial photogrammetry
- **LiDAR** for high-resolution Digital Elevation Models (DEM)

Key outputs:

- **Contour Map** with contour interval of **0.5 m to 1.0 m** for small watersheds, **2 m to 5 m** for larger ones
- **Watershed Boundary Delineation** (ridgeline identification)
- **Drainage Network Map** showing stream order (Strahler or Horton classification)

### Step 4: Soil Survey and Classification

Soil properties directly govern infiltration, runoff, and erosion rates.

**Standard soil survey procedure:**

- Auger boreholes at a grid spacing of **100 m to 250 m** depending on variability
- Profile pits of **1.5 m × 1.5 m × 1.5 m** for detailed horizon study
- Soil samples collected from each horizon for laboratory analysis
- Laboratory tests include: **Particle size analysis (sieve + hydrometer), Atterberg limits, Organic carbon, pH, EC, Infiltration rate (double ring infiltrometer)**

### Step 5: Hydrological Investigation

This is arguably the **most data-intensive** part of watershed investigation.

- Install **rain gauges** (standard IMD pattern, 127.6 cm² collecting area)
- Install **stream gauging stations** with staff gauges
- Measure **discharge** using current meter, float method, or stage-discharge rating curve
- Monitor **groundwater levels** in observation wells
- Compute **runoff coefficient (C)**, **time of concentration (tc)**, and **runoff depth**

### Step 6: Vegetation and Land Use Survey

- Identify existing tree species, crop types, and grass cover
- Estimate canopy density (%)
- Classify land use following **NRSC / USGS Anderson Classification**
- Map degraded lands (wastelands, mining sites, overgrazed areas)

### Step 7: Socio-Economic Investigation

- Household survey using structured questionnaires
- Demographic data: family size, age, education, occupation
- Economic data: income sources, land holdings, livestock
- Institutional data: presence of Gram Panchayat, SHGs, NGOs
- Water use pattern: drinking, irrigation, livestock

### Step 8: Remote Sensing and GIS Investigation

- Acquire **multispectral satellite imagery** (e.g., Landsat 8/9, Sentinel-2, Resourcesat)
- Generate **LULC (Land Use / Land Cover) maps** through supervised classification
- Derive **DEM (Digital Elevation Model)** from SRTM (30 m) or Cartosat (10 m)
- Compute **morphometric parameters** (drainage density, stream frequency, relief ratio, etc.)

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This formula sheet is the most-asked reference for KTU exams.** Memorise the units, ranges, and typical values.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units / Range | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Watershed Area (A)** | From contour planimeter / GIS polygon area | ha or km² | Base parameter for all hydrological design |
| 2 | **Average Slope (S)** | $S = \dfrac{H \times \text{Contour Interval}}{L \times 1000}$ | % or m/m | Terrace spacing, bund design |
| 3 | **Rational Runoff Formula** | $Q = C \times I \times A$ | m³/s | Peak runoff for small watersheds <50 km² |
| 4 | **Runoff Coefficient (C)** | $C = \dfrac{R}{P}$ | Dimensionless, 0.0–1.0 | Stormwater design |
| 5 | **Time of Concentration (Kirpich)** | $t_c = 0.01947 \times L^{0.77} / S^{0.385}$ | minutes | Lag time, peak flow |
| 6 | **SCS Curve Number Runoff** | $Q = \dfrac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S}$, where $S = \dfrac{25400}{CN} - 254$ | mm | Rainfall-runoff transformation |
| 7 | **Drainage Density (Dd)** | $D_d = \dfrac{\Sigma L_i}{A}$ | km/km² | Morphometric analysis |
| 8 | **Stream Frequency (Fs)** | $F_s = \dfrac{N_u}{A}$ | number/km² | Drainage texture |
| 9 | **Bifurcation Ratio (Rb)** | $R_b = \dfrac{N_u}{N_{u+1}}$ | Dimensionless | Drainage pattern maturity |
| 10 | **Relief Ratio (Rr)** | $R_r = \dfrac{H}{\text{Length of main stream}}$ | Dimensionless | Erosion potential indicator |
| 11 | **Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE)** | $A = R \times K \times LS \times C \times P$ | tonnes/ha/year | Annual soil loss estimation |
| 12 | **Sediment Delivery Ratio (SDR)** | $SDR = \dfrac{\text{Sediment yield}}{\text{Gross erosion}}$ | % | Reservoir sedimentation |
| 13 | **Infiltration (Horton's)** | $f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) \times e^{-kt}$ | mm/hr | Infiltration modelling |
| 14 | **Peak Discharge (SCS Triangular)** | $q_p = 0.208 \times A \times Q / t_p$ | m³/s | Ungauged watershed design |
| 15 | **Soil Erodibility Factor (K)** | From nomograph or regression | Dimensionless | USLE input |

### Real-World Utility of These Formulas

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter in actual watershed projects:**
> 
> - The **Rational formula** is used by PWD and NREGA engineers to size **earthen check dams and farm ponds** for a 10-year or 25-year return period storm.
> - The **USLE** is used by the **Integrated Watershed Management Programme (IWMP)** and **PMKSY** to prioritise sub-watersheds for treatment.
> - **Morphometric parameters** computed in GIS help identify **erosion-prone micro-watersheds** without expensive field surveys.

---

## 2.3 Standard Scales of Maps Used in Investigation

| Map Type | Scale | Source | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Toposheet | 1:50,000 / 1:25,000 | Survey of India | Reconnaissance, base map |
| Cadastral Map | 1:4,000 to 1:8,000 | State Revenue Dept | Land ownership, plot boundaries |
| Soil Map | 1:250,000 to 1:50,000 | NBSS&LUP | Soil classification |
| LULC Map | 1:50,000 to 1:10,000 | NRSC / Remote sensing | Land use planning |
| Contour Map | 1:5,000 to 1:10,000 | Field survey | Design of structures |
| Geological Map | 1:50,000 to 1:250,000 | GSI | Geological hazards |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Time of Concentration (Kirpich Equation)

The Kirpich equation is one of the most commonly used empirical formulas in watershed investigation. The complete derivation from physical principles is as follows:

### Starting Concept

The **time of concentration (tc)** is defined as the time required for runoff to travel from the **hydraulically most distant point** of the watershed to the **outlet**. Kirpich (1940) developed an empirical relation based on data from seven small rural watersheds in Tennessee, USA.

### Physical Form

The basic kinematic form relating travel time to length and slope is:

$$
t_c \propto \frac{L^n}{S^m}
$$

where $L$ is the length of the longest flow path, $S$ is the average slope, and $n$, $m$ are empirical exponents.

### Dimensional Analysis

Using the Buckingham Pi theorem with the fundamental variables [L, T]:

$$
t_c = K \cdot L^a \cdot S^b
$$

where $K$ is a dimensional constant. Substituting dimensions:

$$
[T] = [L]^a \cdot [L]^b = [L]^{a+b} \quad \text{(incorrect, slope is dimensionless)}
$$

Since slope $S$ is dimensionless ($L/L$), only $L$ carries dimension, and to balance dimensions we need $a = 0$ in the strict sense, but Kirpich's empirical form was calibrated with units embedded in $K$.

### Calibrated Form (Kirpich 1940)

$$
t_c = 0.01947 \times L^{0.77} \times S^{-0.385}
$$

where:
- $t_c$ = time of concentration in **minutes**
- $L$ = length of longest flow path in **metres**
- $S$ = average slope in **m/m** (dimensionless)

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A watershed has the longest flow path $L = 1500$ m and an average slope of $S = 0.025$ m/m. Calculate the time of concentration using Kirpich's equation.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

$$
L = 1500 \text{ m}, \quad S = 0.025
$$

$$
L^{0.77} = 1500^{0.77}
$$

Computing using logarithms:

$$
\log(1500) = 3.1761
$$

$$
0.77 \times 3.1761 = 2.4456
$$

$$
1500^{0.77} = 10^{2.4456} = 279.20
$$

$$
S^{-0.385} = (0.025)^{-0.385}
$$

$$
\log(0.025) = -1.6021
$$

$$
-0.385 \times -1.6021 = 0.6168
$$

$$
(0.025)^{-0.385} = 10^{0.6168} = 4.139
$$

$$
t_c = 0.01947 \times 279.20 \times 4.139
$$

$$
t_c = 0.01947 \times 1155.66
$$

$$
t_c \approx 22.5 \text{ minutes}
$$

### Verification by Alternative Method

Using the simplified Kirpich form for kilometres:

$$
t_c = 0.0667 \times (L/\sqrt{S})^{0.77} \quad \text{(in minutes, L in km)}
$$

Converting $L = 1.5$ km, $S = 0.025$:

$$
L / \sqrt{S} = 1.5 / \sqrt{0.025} = 1.5 / 0.1581 = 9.487
$$

$$
9.487^{0.77} = ?
$$

$$
\log(9.487) = 0.9772, \quad 0.77 \times 0.9772 = 0.7524, \quad 10^{0.7524} = 5.654
$$

$$
t_c = 0.0667 \times 5.654 = 0.377 \text{ hours} = 22.6 \text{ minutes}
$$

Both methods agree. **The time of concentration is approximately 22.5 minutes.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation of SCS Curve Number Runoff Equation

The **Soil Conservation Service (SCS)** method (USDA, 1972) is the most widely used rainfall-runoff transformation in KTU-affiliated watershed design problems.

### Starting Concept

The total rainfall $P$ is partitioned into three components:

1. **Initial abstraction (Ia)** — water lost to interception, evaporation, and depression storage
2. **Actual runoff (Q)** — water that becomes direct runoff
3. **Continuing abstraction (F)** — water that infiltrates after initial abstraction

$$
P = I_a + Q + F
$$

### Empirical Assumption

The SCS method assumes proportionality between the ratios of actual to potential abstractions:

$$
\frac{F}{S} = \frac{Q}{P - I_a}
$$

where $S$ is the **maximum potential retention** of the soil.

### Solving for Q

Rearranging:

$$
F = \frac{S \cdot Q}{P - I_a}
$$

Substituting into the water balance:

$$
P = I_a + Q + \frac{S \cdot Q}{P - I_a}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $(P - I_a)$:

$$
P(P - I_a) = I_a(P - I_a) + Q(P - I_a) + S \cdot Q
$$

Expanding and simplifying:

$$
P^2 - P \cdot I_a = P \cdot I_a - I_a^2 + Q \cdot P - Q \cdot I_a + S \cdot Q
$$

$$
P^2 - 2 P \cdot I_a + I_a^2 = Q(P - I_a + S)
$$

$$
(P - I_a)^2 = Q(P - I_a + S)
$$

$$
Q = \frac{(P - I_a)^2}{P - I_a + S}
$$

### Empirical Relation for Initial Abstraction

The SCS recommends:

$$
I_a = 0.2 \cdot S
$$

Substituting back:

$$
Q = \frac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P - 0.2S + S} = \frac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S}
$$

This is the **canonical SCS-CN runoff equation**.

### Maximum Retention in Terms of Curve Number

The Curve Number $CN$ is defined as:

$$
CN = \frac{25400}{S + 254} \quad \Rightarrow \quad S = \frac{25400}{CN} - 254
$$

where $S$ is in **mm**.

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A watershed has $CN = 80$. A storm of depth $P = 100$ mm occurs. Compute the direct runoff depth $Q$.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

Step 1: Compute $S$.

$$
S = \frac{25400}{80} - 254 = 317.5 - 254 = 63.5 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 2: Compute initial abstraction.

$$
I_a = 0.2 \times 63.5 = 12.7 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 3: Compute effective rainfall.

$$
P - I_a = 100 - 12.7 = 87.3 \text{ mm}
$$

Step 4: Apply the SCS-CN equation.

$$
Q = \frac{(87.3)^2}{100 + 0.8 \times 63.5}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{7621.29}{100 + 50.8} = \frac{7621.29}{150.8}
$$

$$
Q \approx 50.54 \text{ mm}
$$

**Direct runoff depth = 50.5 mm**, which is **50.5%** of the total rainfall — a reasonable value for $CN = 80$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE)

The **USLE** by Wischmeier & Smith (1978) is the standard tool for estimating **average annual soil loss** in watershed investigation.

### Structure

$$
A = R \times K \times LS \times C \times P
$$

| Factor | Description | Symbol | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | Average annual soil loss | — | tonnes/ha/year |
| $R$ | Rainfall erosivity index | $R$ | MJ·mm/ha·hr·year |
| $K$ | Soil erodibility factor | $K$ | tonnes·hr/MJ·mm |
| $LS$ | Slope length-gradient factor | $LS$ | dimensionless |
| $C$ | Cover management factor | $C$ | dimensionless |
| $P$ | Conservation practice factor | $P$ | dimensionless |

### Physical Derivation Logic

The equation is **not derived from first principles** but is a **multiparametric regression model** built from thousands of plot experiments at **52 USDA research stations** across the US. However, the logical structure is as follows:

**Step 1: Rainfall Erosivity (R)**

The rainfall erosivity factor combines:
- **Kinetic energy (KE)** of the rainstorm
- **Maximum 30-minute intensity (I30)**

$$
R = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (KE)_i \times (I_{30})_i
$$

For India, the modified Fournier index is often used as a proxy:

$$
F = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{12} p_i^2}{P}
$$

where $p_i$ is monthly rainfall and $P$ is annual rainfall.

**Step 2: Slope-Length-Gradient Factor (LS)**

For a uniform slope:

$$
LS = \left( \frac{L}{22.13} \right)^m \times \left( \frac{0.065 + 0.045 S + 0.0065 S^2}{0.065 + 0.045 \times 9 + 0.0065 \times 81} \right)
$$

where:
- $L$ = slope length in metres
- $S$ = slope in %
- $m$ = slope-length exponent, typically **0.5 for slopes > 9%, 0.4 for 3–9%, 0.3 for 1–3%, 0.2 for <1%**

**Step 3: Soil Erodibility (K)**

For Indian soils, the following nomograph-based equation by Singh et al. is used:

$$
K = 0.0017 \times \frac{OM}{Silt + Sand} + \text{adjustment terms}
$$

Typical K values:
- **Sandy soil**: 0.05 – 0.15
- **Loam**: 0.20 – 0.35
- **Clay**: 0.10 – 0.25
- **Silt loam**: 0.30 – 0.50

**Step 4: Cover Management Factor (C)**

C represents the ratio of soil loss from land with cover to that from bare fallow. Typical values:

| Land Use | C Value |
|---|---|
| Dense forest | 0.001 – 0.01 |
| Grass cover | 0.01 – 0.10 |
| Agricultural crop | 0.20 – 0.50 |
| Bare soil | 1.00 |

**Step 5: Conservation Practice Factor (P)**

| Practice | P Value |
|---|---|
| Contour bunding | 0.50 – 0.80 |
| Strip cropping | 0.30 – 0.60 |
| Terracing | 0.10 – 0.50 |
| No practice | 1.00 |

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A watershed has the following USLE parameters: $R = 350$ MJ·mm/ha·hr·year, $K = 0.30$ t·hr/MJ·mm, $LS = 1.5$, $C = 0.20$, $P = 0.80$. Compute the average annual soil loss.

**Solution:**

$$
A = 350 \times 0.30 \times 1.5 \times 0.20 \times 0.80
$$

$$
A = 350 \times 0.30 = 105
$$

$$
A = 105 \times 1.5 = 157.5
$$

$$
A = 157.5 \times 0.20 = 31.5
$$

$$
A = 31.5 \times 0.80 = 25.2 \text{ tonnes/ha/year}
$$

**Average annual soil loss = 25.2 t/ha/year**, which is **moderately high** and warrants treatment interventions.

---

## 3.4 Drainage Density Derivation (Horton 1945)

Drainage density is the **most important morphometric parameter** in watershed investigation, indicating how well a watershed is drained.

### Definition

$$
D_d = \frac{\Sigma L_i}{A}
$$

where:
- $\Sigma L_i$ = total length of all streams in the watershed (km)
- $A$ = total area of the watershed (km²)

### Step-by-Step Derivation Logic

**Step 1:** Identify all stream segments (1st, 2nd, 3rd, ... order) from a toposheet or DEM.

**Step 2:** Measure the length of each segment using a **map wheel** or **GIS polyline length tool**.

**Step 3:** Sum the lengths:

$$
L_{total} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3 + \ldots + L_n
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the area of the watershed using a **planimeter** or **GIS area tool**.

**Step 5:** Apply the formula.

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A watershed has the following data: 12 first-order streams with total length 8.5 km, 5 second-order streams of total length 4.2 km, 2 third-order streams of total length 2.1 km, and 1 fourth-order stream of length 1.0 km. The total area is 6.0 km². Compute the drainage density.

**Solution:**

$$
\Sigma L_i = 8.5 + 4.2 + 2.1 + 1.0 = 15.8 \text{ km}
$$

$$
D_d = \frac{15.8}{6.0} = 2.633 \text{ km/km}^2
$$

### Interpretation Table

| Drainage Density | Infiltration | Terrain | Erosion Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **< 1.0 km/km²** | Very high | Permeable, flat | Low |
| **1.0 – 2.0 km/km²** | Moderate | Moderate slope | Moderate |
| **2.0 – 5.0 km/km²** | Low | Steep, impermeable | High |
| **> 5.0 km/km²** | Very low | Steep, rocky | Very High |

Our computed value of **2.63 km/km²** indicates a **moderately erosion-prone watershed**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Watershed Investigation

The following block diagram maps the complete interaction between the various investigation activities, data sources, and engineering outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Project Initiation] --> B1[Reconnaissance Survey]
    B1 --> C1[Secondary Data Collection]
    C1 --> D1[Topographical Survey]
    D1 --> E1[Soil Survey]
    D1 --> F1[Geological Survey]
    D1 --> G1[Hydrological Investigation]
    E1 --> H1[Field Laboratory Testing]
    F1 --> H1
    G1 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Data Compilation and Analysis]
    C1 --> I1
    I1 --> J1[Remote Sensing and GIS Overlay]
    J1 --> K1[Design Parameter Generation]
    K1 --> L1[Soil Loss Estimation USLE]
    K1 --> M1[Runoff Estimation SCS CN]
    K1 --> N1[Morphometric Analysis]
    L1 --> O1[Final Investigation Report]
    M1 --> O1
    N1 --> O1
    O1 --> P1[Planning and Design Phase]
    P1 --> Q1[Implementation of Structures]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram of Investigation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1: Reconnaissance] --> P2[Phase 2: Secondary Data]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Detailed Field Survey]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Laboratory Analysis]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Data Integration]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6: Remote Sensing Overlay]
    P6 --> P7[Phase 7: Report Generation]
    P1 --- N1[Visual inspection, villager interaction]
    P2 --- N2[IMD, SOI, NBSS, GSI data]
    P3 --- N3[Total station, GPS, auger]
    P4 --- N4[Sieve, hydrometer, infiltrometer]
    P5 --- N5[GIS database creation]
    P6 --- N6[Landsat, Sentinel, SRTM DEM]
    P7 --- N7[Final investigation report]
```

## 4.3 Data Integration Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTS[Input Data Sources]
        I1[IMD Rainfall Data]
        I2[Survey of India Toposheets]
        I3[NBSS and LUP Soil Maps]
        I4[GSI Geological Maps]
        I5[FSI Forest Cover Data]
        I6[CGWB Groundwater Data]
        I7[NRSC Satellite Imagery]
    end
    subgraph PROCESSING[GIS Processing Hub]
        G1[Geo-referencing]
        G2[Layer Overlay]
        G3[Spatial Analysis]
        G4[Morphometric Tools]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS[Engineering Outputs]
        O1[Contour Map]
        O2[Soil Distribution Map]
        O3[Watershed Boundary]
        O4[Drainage Network]
        O5[LULC Map]
        O6[Slope Map]
    end
    I1 --> G2
    I2 --> G1
    I3 --> G2
    I4 --> G2
    I5 --> G2
    I6 --> G2
    I7 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> G4
    G4 --> O1
    G4 --> O2
    G4 --> O3
    G4 --> O4
    G4 --> O5
    G4 --> O6
```

## 4.4 USLE Factor Dependency Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Rainfall Erosivity Factor R] --> A[Annual Soil Loss A]
    K[Soil Erodibility K] --> A
    LS[Slope Length Gradient LS] --> A
    C[Cover Management C] --> A
    P[Conservation Practice P] --> A
    A --> Z[Final USLE Output in tonnes per ha per year]
    R --- R1[From IMD storm data]
    K --- K1[From soil texture and OM]
    LS --- LS1[From contour map]
    C --- C1[From LULC map]
    P --- P1[From field assessment]
```

## 4.5 SCS Curve Number Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Start: Input P and CN] --> B[Compute S = 25400 by CN minus 254]
    B --> C[Compute Ia = 0.2 times S]
    C --> D{Is P greater than Ia}
    D -- No --> E[Output Q = 0, no runoff]
    D -- Yes --> F[Compute Q = P minus 0.2S squared divided by P plus 0.8S]
    F --> G[Output Direct Runoff Depth Q in mm]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
> **Define watershed investigation. List any four types of investigations carried out before planning a watershed project.**

**Model Answer:**

Watershed investigation is the systematic process of collecting, analysing, and interpreting physical, hydrological, biological, and socio-economic data of a watershed to provide the technical basis for planning, designing, and implementing watershed treatment measures.

The four major types of investigations are:

1. **Topographical investigation** — to determine the watershed boundary, slope, and contour map.
2. **Soil investigation** — to classify soils, determine erodibility, and identify problem soils.
3. **Hydrological investigation** — to collect rainfall, runoff, and streamflow data.
4. **Socio-economic investigation** — to assess demographic and economic conditions of the watershed inhabitants.

---

> **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
> **What is meant by reconnaissance survey? Why is it considered essential in watershed investigation?**

**Model Answer:**

Reconnaissance survey is a **preliminary, qualitative field visit** to the watershed conducted to gain a general understanding of the terrain, drainage, land use, vegetation, erosion status, and socio-economic conditions without taking detailed measurements.

It is considered essential because:

- It provides a **first-hand overview** before committing resources to detailed surveys.
- It helps in **identifying problem areas** such as severe gully erosion, landslides, or water scarcity zones.
- It enables **preliminary selection** of sites for check dams, percolation tanks, and other structures.
- It allows interaction with **local communities** to understand their needs and ensure participation.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain in detail the various steps involved in the **topographical investigation of a watershed**. List the instruments used and the outputs produced. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A watershed has a longest flow path of $L = 2.0$ km and an average slope of $S = 0.020$ m/m. Using **Kirpich's equation**, compute the time of concentration in minutes. Also compute the time of concentration using the **Rational Method** for verification, given that the overland flow length is 800 m, the slope is 1.5%, and the overland flow is over cultivated soil. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Topographical investigation** involves the precise mapping of the watershed's terrain features. The steps are:

1. **Establishment of base control points** using DGPS tied to SOI benchmarks.
2. **Traverse survey** using theodolite or total station along the watershed boundary and major ridgelines.
3. **Levelling** to obtain reduced levels (RLs) of all control points using a dumpy level.
4. **Cross-section survey** of the main stream at 100 m to 200 m intervals to compute storage capacity of proposed structures.
5. **Plotting of contours** at intervals of 0.5 m to 2 m, depending on terrain ruggedness.
6. **Delineation of watershed boundary** by connecting the highest elevations (ridgeline).

**Instruments used:**
- Total station (angle + distance)
- Dumpy level (elevation difference)
- GPS / DGPS (geo-referencing)
- Drone with photogrammetry (modern approach)

**Outputs:**
- Contour map
- Watershed boundary map
- Drainage network map
- Cross-section profiles
- Slope map (in %)

### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Given:**
- $L = 2.0 \text{ km} = 2000 \text{ m}$
- $S = 0.020$

**Step 1: Apply Kirpich equation**

$$
t_c = 0.01947 \times L^{0.77} \times S^{-0.385}
$$

Compute $L^{0.77}$:

$$
\log(2000) = 3.3010, \quad 0.77 \times 3.3010 = 2.5418, \quad 2000^{0.77} = 348.07
$$

Compute $S^{-0.385}$:

$$
\log(0.020) = -1.6990, \quad -0.385 \times -1.6990 = 0.6541, \quad 0.020^{-0.385} = 4.508
$$

$$
t_c = 0.01947 \times 348.07 \times 4.508 = 30.55 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 2: Verification using Rational Method overland flow**

For cultivated soil, roughness coefficient $n = 0.20$ (Manning's), overland flow length $L_0 = 800$ m, slope = 1.5% = 0.015.

Kirpich overland form (minutes, L in feet) — adapted to SI as:

$$
t_c = 0.01947 \times \left( \frac{L_0}{\sqrt{S_0}} \right)^{0.77}
$$

$$
\frac{L_0}{\sqrt{S_0}} = \frac{800}{\sqrt{0.015}} = \frac{800}{0.1225} = 6530.6
$$

$$
6530.6^{0.77}: \quad \log(6530.6) = 3.8150, \quad 0.77 \times 3.8150 = 2.9376, \quad 10^{2.9376} = 866.0
$$

$$
t_c = 0.01947 \times 866.0 = 16.86 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Total time of concentration** (overland + channel) ≈ **30.5 minutes (Kirpich direct method)**, with overland component contributing 16.9 minutes and channel flow contributing the remainder.

**Result: $t_c \approx 30.6$ minutes.**

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Alternative Choice]

**(a)** What is the **Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE)**? Explain each factor with its significance. A watershed has $R = 400$ MJ·mm/ha·hr·year, $K = 0.25$ t·hr/MJ·mm, $LS = 2.0$, $C = 0.30$, and $P = 0.80$. Compute the average annual soil loss. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the **morphometric analysis of a watershed**. Define drainage density, stream frequency, bifurcation ratio, and relief ratio. Compute drainage density for a watershed with total stream length 18.5 km and area 7.4 km². **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Definition:**

The **Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE)** is an empirical model developed by Wischmeier & Smith (1978) for estimating the **average annual soil loss** from a slope due to sheet and rill erosion under specific conditions of rainfall, soil, slope, cover, and management.

**Structure:**

$$
A = R \times K \times LS \times C \times P
$$

**Factor explanation:**

- **R (Rainfall erosivity)** — Combines the kinetic energy of rainfall with its maximum 30-minute intensity. Higher in regions of intense storms (e.g., Western Ghats).
- **K (Soil erodibility)** — Inherent susceptibility of soil to erosion. Depends on texture, organic matter, structure, and permeability.
- **LS (Slope length-gradient factor)** — Represents the effect of slope length and steepness on erosion. Longer, steeper slopes → higher LS.
- **C (Cover management factor)** — Ratio of soil loss from cropped land to that from a clean-tilled fallow. Dense forest ≈ 0.001, bare soil = 1.0.
- **P (Conservation practice factor)** — Ratio of soil loss with conservation practice to that with straight-row farming.

**Numerical Solution:**

Given: $R = 400$, $K = 0.25$, $LS = 2.0$, $C = 0.30$, $P = 0.80$

$$
A = 400 \times 0.25 \times 2.0 \times 0.30 \times 0.80
$$

$$
A = 400 \times 0.25 = 100
$$

$$
A = 100 \times 2.0 = 200
$$

$$
A = 200 \times 0.30 = 60
$$

$$
A = 60 \times 0.80 = 48 \text{ t/ha/year}
$$

**Result: Average annual soil loss = 48 t/ha/year**, indicating a **high erosion-prone watershed** that requires urgent treatment.

### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Morphometric analysis** is the quantitative measurement and analysis of the **configuration of the earth's surface**, the shape and dimensions of its landforms, and the geometry of its drainage network. It is essential in watershed investigation for understanding terrain behaviour.

**Key parameters:**

1. **Drainage Density ($D_d$)**: Total stream length per unit area.
2. **Stream Frequency ($F_s$)**: Number of streams per unit area.
3. **Bifurcation Ratio ($R_b$)**: Ratio of streams of a given order to those of the next higher order.
4. **Relief Ratio ($R_r$)**: Ratio of total relief to the length of the longest stream.
5. **Elongation Ratio ($R_e$)**: Ratio of diameter of a circle of equal area to the maximum watershed length.
6. **Form Factor ($R_f$)**: Ratio of basin area to the square of basin length.

**Numerical Solution:**

Given: Total stream length = 18.5 km, Area = 7.4 km²

$$
D_d = \frac{18.5}{7.4} = 2.50 \text{ km/km}^2
$$

**Interpretation:** A drainage density of **2.50 km/km²** indicates a **moderately well-drained watershed with medium infiltration capacity** and **moderate erosion risk**. Such watersheds typically respond well to soil and water conservation measures.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in Kirpich Equation**: Using $L$ in km instead of metres (or vice versa) without the proper coefficient. Kirpich's standard form requires **$L$ in metres** and gives $t_c$ in **minutes**.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $S$ as % instead of m/m**: A 2% slope is **0.02 m/m**, not **2.0**. Students frequently forget to divide by 100.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the $0.2S$ in SCS-CN**: Students often write the SCS-CN equation as $Q = P^2 / (P + S)$, missing the $0.2S$ initial abstraction term.
> 
> 4. **Incorrect USLE Multiplication Order**: USLE is a product, not a sum. Students sometimes add factors instead of multiplying.
> 
> 5. **Missing Units in Final Answer**: Always mention **t/ha/year, mm, km/km², etc.** Units are compulsory in KTU Board valuation.
> 
> 6. **No Mention of Secondary Data**: When asked about investigation, students list only field surveys. **Always mention secondary data sources** (IMD, SOI, NBSS, GSI) as part of investigation.
> 
> 7. **Skipping the Reconnaissance Phase**: Many students jump directly to detailed survey. Reconnaissance is the **mandatory first step** of investigation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Key Definitions (Rapid Revision):**
> - **Investigation** — Systematic pre-project data collection and analysis.
> - **Reconnaissance** — First preliminary field visit, qualitative only.
> - **Watershed Boundary** — Ridge line connecting highest points.
> - **Contour** — Line joining points of equal elevation.
> - **Drainage Density** — Total stream length per unit area.
> - **Time of Concentration** — Time for water from the most distant point to reach the outlet.
> - **CN** — Curve Number representing runoff potential.
> - **USLE** — Universal Soil Loss Equation (R × K × LS × C × P).

> **Key Formulae (Must Memorise):**
> - Rational Runoff: $Q = C \times I \times A$
> - Kirpich: $t_c = 0.01947 \times L^{0.77} \times S^{-0.385}$
> - SCS-CN: $Q = (P - 0.2S)^2 / (P + 0.8S)$, where $S = 25400/CN - 254$
> - Drainage Density: $D_d = \Sigma L_i / A$
> - USLE: $A = R \times K \times LS \times C \times P$

> **Key Data Sources (Memory Tip: "ISN-F-CG"):**
> - **I**MD — Rainfall
> - **S**OI — Toposheets
> - **N**BSS&LUP — Soil maps
> - **F**SI — Forest cover
> - **C**GWB — Groundwater
> - **G**SI — Geological maps
> - **N**RSC — Satellite imagery and LULC

> **Numerical Range Checks (Quick Sanity):**
> - Curve Number: 30 to 98
> - K factor: 0.02 to 0.69
> - C factor: 0.001 to 1.0
> - Drainage density: 0.5 to 5.0 km/km²
> - Time of concentration: 10 to 120 minutes for small watersheds
> - USLE soil loss: 5 to 200 t/ha/year

> **Investigations Order (Memory Tip: "R-S-T-S-H-L-S-R-G"):**
> **R**econnaissance → **S**econdary data → **T**opographical → **S**oil → **H**ydrological → **L**and use → **S**ocio-economic → **R**emote sensing → **G**IS analysis

> **Critical Engineering Insight:**
> The investigation phase consumes only **5–10%** of the total project budget but determines **80–90%** of the project's success or failure. KTU examiners love this statistic in viva voce.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Topographical Survey
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET741 - WATERSHED DEVELOPMENT AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Topographical Survey

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Topographical Survey** is a detailed, large-scale spatial reconnaissance procedure executed to determine the precise three-dimensional configuration (planimetry `X`, `Y` and altimetry `Z`) of the natural and artificial features of a tract of land. As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PGAET741**, it is the foundational geomatic activity upon which the entire watershed characterization, drainage delineation, and storage structure design rest.

Mathematically, the objective is to determine the reduced level (RL) of numerous points and represent them through:

$$ Z = f(X, Y) $$

where `Z` represents the orthometric height above the Mean Sea Level (MSL), while `X` and `Y` represent the horizontal positional coordinates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** A topographical survey is the first technical step in any watershed project. Without accurate topographic data, calculations of **runoff**, **catchment area**, **slope gradient**, and **storage capacity** of check dams become engineering guesswork rather than scientific design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a sculptor tasked with carving a small replica of a mountain on a table. Before you touch a chisel, you would need a "skin" stretched over the mountain showing every bump, depression, and ridge. A topographical survey is exactly that process of creating a "mathematical skin" (the Digital Terrain Model) of the land.

**The Coffee Cup Analogy:**
Think of a coffee cup with complex bumps of coffee grounds on the surface. If you were to slice it horizontally at uniform heights (say every 1 cm from the top), each slice would draw a closed loop. Stack these slices in order, and you have a perfect 3D model. The horizontal slices are called **contours**, and the height between slices is the **contour interval**.

## 1.3 Key Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics in Topographical Survey:**
> - **Contour Interval (CI):** Typically **1 m, 2 m, 5 m, or 10 m** for watershed projects
> - **Horizontal Equivalent (HE):** The horizontal distance between adjacent contour lines on the map
> - **Scale of Survey:** 1:1000 to 1:10,000 for watershed-level surveys
> - **Grid Size:** Commonly **10 m × 10 m, 20 m × 20 m, or 50 m × 50 m** based on terrain roughness
> - **Reference Datum:** Mean Sea Level (MSL) at **Chennai (0.000 m)** for India (Survey of India)

## 1.4 Objectives of Topographical Survey in Watershed Projects

1. **Preparation of base maps** showing natural and cultural features.
2. **Determination of slopes and gradients** for channel design.
3. **Delineation of watershed boundary** and sub-watershed divisions.
4. **Computation of cross-sectional areas** for earthen dams and check dams.
5. **Estimation of storage volumes** using contour data.
6. **Planning of conservation structures** (terraces, bunds, gully plugs).
7. **Identification of natural drainage lines** and watercourses.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Methods of Topographical Survey

The selection of a method depends on the **scale of the map**, **terrain complexity**, **accuracy required**, and **available time/funds**. The hierarchy of methods, from classical to modern, is as follows:

### 2.1.1 Classical Methods

**A. Chain and Cross-Staff Survey (Preliminary)**
- Used for small, flat watersheds.
- The chain (20 m) measures horizontal distances, and the cross-staff sets out perpendicular offsets.
- Limitation: cannot handle steep, undulating terrain.

**B. Compass Survey (Traversing)**
- Uses a prismatic compass to measure bearings.
- The closed traverse forms a polygon enclosing the watershed.
- Suitable for reconnaissance surveys of medium-sized areas.

**C. Plane Table Survey (Radiation and Intersection)**
- A direct graphical method where measurements are plotted in the field itself.
- Best suited for small to medium watersheds (up to 250 ha).
- **Radiation Method:** Instrument station is the center; rays are drawn to each object.
- **Intersection Method:** Two instrument stations plot the position of objects via intersecting rays.

**D. Theodolite Survey (Most Accurate Classical Method)**
- Measures horizontal angles, vertical angles, and distances.
- Combined with **trigonometric levelling**, it determines RLs of distant points.
- Capable of handling large watersheds with high relief.

### 2.1.2 Modern Methods

**E. Total Station Survey (Integrated Digital Survey)**
- Combines **Electronic Distance Measurement (EDM)**, **angle encoder**, and **microprocessor**.
- Calculates `(X, Y, Z)` coordinates of every observed point in real-time.
- Accuracy: ±(2 mm + 2 ppm × D).
- **Reflectorless mode** allows measurement to inaccessible cliff faces and gully walls.

**F. Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS/GPS)**
- Uses satellite trilateration (minimum 4 satellites for 3D fix).
- Provides `(Latitude, Longitude, Ellipsoidal Height)`.
- Transforms to `(X, Y, Z)` on MSL via **geoid undulation** correction `N`:
$$ h = H + N $$
where `h` = ellipsoidal height, `H` = orthometric height (above MSL), `N` = geoid undulation.

**G. Aerial Photogrammetry & LiDAR**
- Used for very large watersheds (>1000 ha).
- **LiDAR** (Light Detection and Ranging) provides dense point clouds (±15 cm vertical accuracy).

## 2.2 Contours and Contour Lines

**Contour Line:** An imaginary line on the ground joining points of equal reduced level (elevation).

**Contour Interval (CI):** The vertical distance between two successive contour lines. Selection rules:
- For **flat terrain**: large CI (1–2 m).
- For **steep terrain**: small CI (5–10 m) to capture relief detail.
- For **watershed master plans**: CI = 1 m to 5 m.

**Horizontal Equivalent (HE):** The horizontal ground distance between two consecutive contour lines.

## 2.3 Characteristics of Contour Lines (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **10 Golden Rules of Contours for KTU Exams:**
> 1. Contours are **closed curves** (may extend beyond map boundary).
> 2. **Widely spaced contours** = gentle slope; **closely spaced** = steep slope.
> 3. **Equally spaced contours** = uniform slope.
> 4. Contours **never intersect** (except at a vertical cliff/overhang, where they coincide).
> 5. Contours **never cross** a watershed/ridge line perpendicularly.
> 6. **Convex contours (towards higher elevation)** indicate a **valley**.
> 7. **Concave contours (towards higher elevation)** indicate a **ridge/saddle**.
> 8. The **steepest direction** at any point is along the **shortest distance** to the next contour.
> 9. Spot heights and bench marks are marked with **X** and **B.M.**.
> 10. Every fifth contour is called an **index contour** and drawn thicker with elevation labels.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $V = \int_{A} Z \, dA$ | Volume under topographic surface | m³ |
| $V = \frac{h}{3} \left( A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 A_2} \right)$ | Prismoidal formula for volume between two sections | m³ |
| $V = \frac{h}{2} (A_1 + A_2)$ | Trapezoidal (average end area) formula | m³ |
| $S = \frac{CI}{HE} \times 100$ | Slope of ground (%) | percent |
| $S = \tan^{-1} \left( \frac{CI}{HE} \right)$ | Slope angle (degrees) | degrees |
| $h = H + N$ | Orthometric vs Ellipsoidal height relation | m |
| $D = 100 \times \frac{CI}{HE}$ | Grade in percent (mapping) | % |
| $A = \text{grid area} \times N_{contour}$ | Area count method using planimeter/grid | m² |
| $RL_{new} = RL_{BM} \pm h_{is} \pm h_{fs}$ | Height of Instrument method of levelling | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table:** All vertical pipe symbols are replaced with `\vert` or omitted for markdown compatibility. Use `D` for horizontal distance.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Watershed Engineering

- **Slope Analysis:** Determines whether terracing, bunding, or contour farming is needed.
- **Drainage Network Design:** Contour-crunching algorithms extract stream networks from DEMs.
- **Storage Capacity Estimation:** Contour area method estimates reservoir volume:
$$ V = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{CI}{3} \left( A_i + A_{i+1} + \sqrt{A_i \cdot A_{i+1}} \right) $$

- **Cut-and-Fill Calculations:** Earthwork estimation for farm ponds and check dams.
- **Erosion Hazard Zoning:** Steep-slope zones are flagged for afforestation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Volume of Earthwork Between Two Contours (Prismoidal Formula)

The classical derivation of the **Prismoidal Formula** for computing volume between two parallel cross-sections separated by a horizontal distance `h`:

**Step 1 — Definition of a Prismoid:**
A prismoid is a solid bounded by two parallel polygonal end-areas `A_1` and `A_2`, separated by perpendicular distance `h`, with intermediate cross-sections varying linearly.

**Step 2 — Consider a Single Elemental Slice:**
Take a thin slice at distance `x` from the first section. The cross-sectional area varies linearly between `A_1` and `A_2`:

$$
A(x) = A_1 + \frac{A_2 - A_1}{h} \cdot x
$$

**Step 3 — Set Up the Volume Integral:**
The elementary volume `dV = A(x) dx`. Integrating from `0` to `h`:

$$
V = \int_{0}^{h} A(x) \, dx
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute A(x) and Integrate:**

$$
V = \int_{0}^{h} \left[ A_1 + \left( \frac{A_2 - A_1}{h} \right) x \right] dx
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate:**

$$
V = A_1 h + \frac{A_2 - A_1}{h} \cdot \frac{x^2}{2} \Big|_{0}^{h}
$$

$$
V = A_1 h + \frac{A_2 - A_1}{h} \cdot \frac{h^2}{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify:**

$$
V = A_1 h + \frac{(A_2 - A_1) h}{2} = \frac{2 A_1 h + (A_2 - A_1) h}{2}
$$

$$
V = \frac{h (A_1 + A_2)}{2}
$$

This is the **Trapezoidal Rule** (average end area).

**Step 7 — Prismoidal Correction (Simpson's Rule):**
For a more accurate volume when the cross-section varies parabolically, include a middle section `A_m`:

$$
V = \frac{h}{6} (A_1 + 4 A_m + A_2)
$$

**Step 8 — Express in Final Form (Contour Method):**
When the two areas are bounding contours of a reservoir at vertical interval `CI = h`:

$$
\boxed{ V = \frac{h}{3} \left( A_1 + A_2 + \sqrt{A_1 \cdot A_2} \right) }
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the $\sqrt{A_1 A_2}$ term?** It comes from integrating the geometric mean of the linearly varying cross-section, which is a precise estimate of the mid-section area under linear-variation assumption.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Slope from Contour Spacing

**Step 1 — Define the Geometric Setup:**
Two consecutive contours at RL `Z_1` and `Z_2` are separated vertically by `CI = Z_2 - Z_1` and horizontally on the map by `HE`.

**Step 2 — Slope Definition:**
Slope is rise over run:

$$
S = \frac{\text{rise}}{\text{run}} = \frac{CI}{HE}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Percentage:**

$$
\boxed{ S (\%) = \frac{CI}{HE} \times 100 }
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Angle:**

$$
\boxed{ \theta = \tan^{-1} \left( \frac{CI}{HE} \right) }
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A topographic survey of a farm pond site yields the following contour areas at 1 m vertical interval:

| Contour RL (m) | Area (m²) |
|---|---|
| 100.0 | 0 |
| 101.0 | 1,200 |
| 102.0 | 2,800 |
| 103.0 | 4,500 |
| 104.0 | 6,000 |
| 105.0 | 7,200 |

**Find the storage capacity of the proposed farm pond up to RL 105 m using the Prismoidal formula.**

**Step 1 — Pair the Contours for Prismoidal Application:**
Prismoidal rule requires a **mid-section**, so we work in pairs of two intervals (2 m total):

- Pair 1: 100 → 102 m (A₁ = 0, A₂ = 2,800 m², h = 2 m)
- Pair 2: 102 → 104 m (A₁ = 2,800, A₂ = 6,000 m², h = 2 m)
- Remaining: 104 → 105 m (use Trapezoidal rule, A₁ = 6,000, A₂ = 7,200, h = 1 m)

**Step 2 — Compute Pair 1 Volume:**

$$
V_1 = \frac{2}{3} \left( 0 + 2800 + \sqrt{0 \times 2800} \right) = \frac{2}{3} (2800) = 1866.67 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Pair 2 Volume:**

$$
V_2 = \frac{2}{3} \left( 2800 + 6000 + \sqrt{2800 \times 6000} \right)
$$

$$
\sqrt{2800 \times 6000} = \sqrt{16,800,000} = 4098.78 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
V_2 = \frac{2}{3} (2800 + 6000 + 4098.78) = \frac{2}{3} (12898.78) = 8599.19 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Remaining Volume (Trapezoidal):**

$$
V_3 = \frac{1}{2} (6000 + 7200) = 6600 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Total Storage Capacity:**

$$
V_{total} = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 = 1866.67 + 8599.19 + 6600
$$

$$
\boxed{ V_{total} = 17,065.86 \text{ m}^3 \approx 17,066 \text{ m}^3 }
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Litres:**

$$
V_{total} = 17,066 \times 1000 = 1.7066 \times 10^7 \text{ litres} \approx 17.07 \text{ ML (megalitres)}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Grid Method for Area Calculation

**Step 1 — Overlay a Transparent Grid:**
A square grid of side `a` is overlaid on the contour map. The total plan area is `A_total = n \times a^2`, where `n` is the number of complete squares.

**Step 2 — Count Squares Inside Each Contour:**
Number of grid squares enclosed by each contour `i` is `n_i`.

**Step 3 — Compute Area Enclosed by Contour `i`:**

$$
A_i = n_i \times a^2
$$

**Step 4 — Volume Between Two Contours:**

$$
V_{i, i+1} = \frac{CI}{3} \left( A_i + A_{i+1} + \sqrt{A_i \cdot A_{i+1}} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Total Volume:**

$$
\boxed{ V_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \frac{CI}{3} \left( A_i + A_{i+1} + \sqrt{A_i \cdot A_{i+1}} \right) }
$$

## 3.5 Decision Matrix for Method Selection

| Terrain Feature | Recommended Method | Accuracy | Relative Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flat, small area (< 50 ha) | Chain + cross-staff | Low | Very Low |
| Rolling, medium area (50–250 ha) | Plane table (rad./int.) | Medium | Low |
| Hilly, large area (250–1000 ha) | Theodolite + Dumpy level | High | Medium |
| Rugged, large area (> 1000 ha) | Total station / RTK GPS | Very High | High |
| Macro-watersheds (> 5000 ha) | LiDAR / Photogrammetry | Very High | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
