- Narrow excavated trenches (0.3 m wide × 0.3 m deep) dug on contour lines.
- **Why:** Intercept surface runoff, allow time for infiltration, prevent rill and gully formation.
- **Best suited for:** Degraded hills, forest regeneration, wastelands with slopes 5%–30%.

#### 2.1.3 Bench Terraces
- Step-like fields cut into hillslopes, converting steep land into flat cultivable steps.
- **Why:** Eliminates slope effect entirely on the cropped surface.
- **Types:** Level bench, sloping outward, sloping inward (puertorican).

#### 2.1.4 Mulching
- Soil surface covered with organic or inorganic material.
- **Why:** Reduces evaporation, moderates soil temperature, improves infiltration by preventing surface crusting.

#### 2.1.5 Conservation Pits / Recharge Pits
- Small square/circular pits (1–2 m³) dug in fields.
- **Why:** Trap runoff water at low-cost, allow infiltration, recharge shallow aquifer.

### 2.2 Ex-Situ Rainwater Conservation Technologies

Ex-situ measures intercept runoff at the watershed scale and store it in engineered reservoirs.

#### 2.2.1 Farm Pond
- Small reservoir constructed at the lowest point of a farm to harvest runoff.
- **Design life:** 25–50 years; sizes 100–10,000 m³.
- **Classification:**
  - *Dugout pond* (entirely excavated, suitable for flat lands).
  - *Embankment-type pond* (with embankment + dugout, suitable for undulating lands).

#### 2.2.2 Percolation Tank
- A small reservoir built across a stream/nalas on porous soils.
- **Purpose:** NOT for storage — designed to *recharge groundwater* through the bed.

#### 2.2.3 Check Dam / Nala Bund
- Small barriers across streams to impound water and recharge groundwater.

#### 2.2.4 Small Earthen Dam / Stop Dam
- Larger storage structures with spillway for flood passage.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-day cheat sheet** for Module-2 numericals.

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Annual Runoff Volume | $V_r = P \times C \times A$ | m³ | P in m, A in m² |
| 2 | Runoff Depth | $R = P \times C$ | mm | Dimensionless C |
| 3 | Storage Volume Required | $V_s = V_r \times F_s$ | m³ | $F_s$ = factor of safety (1.2–1.5) |
| 4 | Trapezoidal Pond Capacity | $V = \frac{L}{6}(B_1 B_2 + (B_1+B_2)L + 4B_m L)$ | m³ | End-area method, prismoidal |
| 5 | Side Slope Stability | $z = 1.5:1$ to $2.5:1$ (H:V) | ratio | Depends on soil type |
| 6 | Vertical Interval of Terraces | $VI = (S/3) + 2$ | m | $S$ = % slope |
| 7 | Evaporation Loss | $E_{loss} = E_p \times A_p \times K_p$ | m³ | $K_p$ = pan coefficient (0.7) |
| 8 | Net Storage | $V_{net} = V_{gross} - E_{loss} - S_{loss}$ | m³ | Subtract losses |
| 9 | Catchment to Pond Area Ratio | $A_c : A_p = 10:1$ to $25:1$ | ratio | For assured filling |
| 10 | Spillway Discharge (Rational) | $Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$ | m³/s | Used for spillway sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Safety Note:** In the table above, ratios like `1.5:1` are written *without* the markdown table-breaking vertical pipe `|`. Always use a colon or the `\colon` symbol for ratios inside markdown tables.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Kerala Context:** Laterite soils of Wayanad, Idukki — ex-situ farm ponds with HDPE lining reduce seepage losses up to 90%.
- **Drought Proofing:** In-situ trenches + ex-situ farm ponds combined raise groundwater table by 0.5–2.0 m in semi-arid Andhra Pradesh and Telangana watersheds (per MANAGE, Hyderabad field studies).
- **Climate Resilience:** Centralized ex-situ storage is used as supplemental irrigation during kharif dry spells, raising crop yield by 20–40%.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Annual Runoff Yield from a Catchment

**Problem Context:** Given the annual rainfall and runoff coefficient of a watershed, derive the total runoff volume available for storage in an ex-situ structure.

#### Step 1 — Identify Variables
- $P$ = Annual rainfall depth (mm)
- $C$ = Runoff coefficient (dimensionless, 0 ≤ C ≤ 1)
- $A$ = Catchment area (hectares, ha)

#### Step 2 — Unit Normalization
Convert all units to a common system. Using SI:

$$P_{m} = \frac{P_{mm}}{1000} \quad \text{[m]}$$

$$A_{m^2} = A_{ha} \times 10{,}000 \quad \text{[m}^2\text{]}$$

#### Step 3 — Apply the Water Balance for a Catchment
Total rainfall mass reaching the catchment surface (volume equivalent):

$$V_{rain} = P_{m} \times A_{m^2}$$

#### Step 4 — Extract the Runoff Component
Only a fraction $C$ becomes surface runoff; the rest infiltrates or evaporates.

$$V_r = C \times P_{m} \times A_{m^2}$$

#### Step 5 — Substitute Unit Conversions

$$V_r = C \times \frac{P_{mm}}{1000} \times (A_{ha} \times 10{,}000)$$

#### Step 6 — Simplify the Numerical Constants
The factor $\frac{10{,}000}{1000} = 10$, yielding the **KTU direct formula**:

$$V_r \,[\text{m}^3] = 10 \times C \times P_{mm} \times A_{ha}$$

This is the **highest-frequency numerical** in PGAET741.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Capacity of a Trapezoidal Farm Pond (Prismoidal Formula)

**Problem Context:** Compute the storage capacity of an embankment-type farm pond with trapezoidal cross-section and sloped embankments.

#### Step 1 — Define the Cross-Section Geometry
A trapezoidal pond has:
- Bottom width: $B$ (m)
- Top water spread width: $T$ (m)
- Water depth: $d$ (m)
- Side slopes: $z:1$ (H:V), so $T = B + 2zd$

#### Step 2 — Cross-Sectional Area at Any Section
The cross-section is a trapezoid:

$$A_{cs} = \frac{(B + T)}{2} \times d = \frac{(2B + 2zd)}{2} \times d = d(B + zd)$$

#### Step 3 — Apply the Prismoidal Formula
For a pond of length $L$ with end cross-sectional areas $A_1$ and $A_2$ (here identical for a uniform pond: $A_1 = A_2$):

$$V = \frac{L}{6} \left( A_1 + A_2 + 4 A_m \right)$$

where $A_m$ is the mid-section area.

#### Step 4 — For a Uniform Pond ($A_1 = A_2 = A_m = A_{cs}$)

$$V = \frac{L}{6} (A + A + 4A) = \frac{L}{6} \times 6A = L \times A_{cs}$$

#### Step 5 — Substitute $A_{cs}$ and Simplify

$$V = L \times d \times (B + zd)$$

This is the **capacity equation** for a uniform trapezoidal farm pond.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Exam Trap:** Many KTU students forget the $zd$ term in the bracket. The correct term is the *bottom width PLUS the side-slope factor*, not just bottom width.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Net Usable Storage (After Losses)

**Step 1 — Gross Storage** is computed using the trapezoidal formula above.
**Step 2 — Estimate Evaporation Loss** using a standard US Class-A pan:

$$E_{loss} = E_{pan} \times K_p \times A_{pan\,surface}$$

Typical $K_p$ for open water = **0.70**.

**Step 3 — Estimate Seepage Loss** (10–30% of storage for unlined ponds, <5% for lined ponds):

$$S_{loss} = V_{gross} \times \eta_{seepage}$$

**Step 4 — Net Usable Storage**

$$V_{net} = V_{gross} - E_{loss} - S_{loss}$$

**Step 5 — Design Adjustment for Sediment Yield** (allocate 10–15% of capacity for sediment):

$$V_{design} = \frac{V_{net}}{1 - 0.15}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting In-Situ vs Ex-Situ

| Decision Criterion | Prefer In-Situ | Prefer Ex-Situ |
|---|---|---|
| Rainfall intensity | Low to moderate (≤ 50 mm/hr) | High intensity, short duration |
| Soil type | Deep, permeable | Shallow, rocky, or impermeable |
| Slope of land | Gentle to moderate (≤ 15%) | Steep lands, gully-prone |
| Crop water demand | Rainfed only, low demand | Irrigated, high-value crops |
| Land availability for storage | Limited | Adequate at lower elevations |
| Cost of construction | Low (manual labour) | High (machinery, masonry) |
| Watershed position | Upper reaches (ridge, mid-slope) | Lower reaches (valley) |
| Maintenance frequency | Frequent (desilting) | Periodic (every 3–5 years) |

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

**Problem:** Design the storage capacity of a trapezoidal farm pond for a 5 ha agricultural watershed receiving 1200 mm annual rainfall. The runoff coefficient is 0.35. The pond is 30 m long × 15 m wide at top, 9 m wide at bottom, with 2.0 m water depth. Side slopes are 2:1 (H:V). Allow 20% extra for sediment, evaporation, and seepage. Compute the net usable volume and verify the design is safe.

#### Step 1 — Annual Runoff Volume

$$V_r = 10 \times C \times P \times A = 10 \times 0.35 \times 1200 \times 5 = 21{,}000 \text{ m}^3$$

#### Step 2 — Cross-Section Area (Trapezoidal)

$$A_{cs} = \frac{(B + T)}{2} \times d = \frac{(9 + 15)}{2} \times 2.0 = 24 \text{ m}^2$$

#### Step 3 — Storage Volume (Uniform Pond, Prismoidal)

$$V = L \times A_{cs} = 30 \times 24 = 720 \text{ m}^3$$

#### Step 4 — Design Capacity with Safety Factor (20% extra)

$$V_{design} = 720 \times 1.20 = 864 \text{ m}^3$$

#### Step 5 — Check Side Slope Stability
For a 2:1 side slope on laterite/sandy loam, the slope angle is:

$$\tan \theta = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \implies \theta = 26.57°$$

This is well within the safe angle of repose for most soils (≤ 30°). **Design is stable.**

#### Step 6 — Demand-to-Availability Ratio

$$\text{Utilization} = \frac{V_{design}}{V_r} = \frac{864}{21{,}000} = 4.11\%$$

Only 4.11% of the catchment's annual runoff is stored — a typical and acceptable design ratio for supplemental irrigation in rainfed systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Watershed Position Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[RIDGE - Watershed Boundary] --> nodeB[UPPER REACH - In Situ Measures]
    nodeB --> nodeC[MIDDLE REACH - Contour Bunds and Trenches]
    nodeC --> nodeD[LOWER REACH - Check Dams and Percolation Tanks]
    nodeD --> nodeE[VALLEY - Ex Situ Storage Farm Pond and Reservoir]
    nodeE --> nodeF[OUTLET - Spillway and Stream Discharge]
    nodeB --> nodeG["Mulching and Vegetative Barriers"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["Bench Terracing and Graded Bunds"]
    nodeD --> nodeI["Nala Bunds and Gabion Structures"]
    nodeE --> nodeJ["Embankment Pond and Recharge Shaft"]
```

### 4.2 In-Situ vs Ex-Situ Decision Flow

```mermaid

    nodeStart["Rainfall Event"] --> nodeQ1{"Is Soil Permeable?"}
    nodeQ1 -->|"Yes"| nodeQ2{"Slope < 15 percent?"}
    nodeQ1 -->|"No"| nodeEx["Ex Situ - Check Dam / Pond"]
    nodeQ2 -->|"Yes"| nodeIn["In Situ - Trenches and Bunds"]
    nodeQ2 -->|"No"| nodeQ3{"Crop Water Demand High?"}

    nodeEx --> nodeResult2["Stored Runoff for Productive Reuse"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of a Farm Pond System

```mermaid

    nodeCatch["Catchment Area A hectares"] --> nodeRain["Rainfall P mm"]
    nodeRain --> nodeRunoff["Runoff Vr = 10 x C x P x A"]
    nodeRunoff --> nodeInlet["Inlet Channel with Silt Trap"]
    nodeInlet --> nodeStorage["Storage Basin Trapezoidal"]
    nodeStorage --> nodeSpill["Spillway for Excess"]
    nodeStorage --> nodeRecharge["Recharge Shaft if Permeable Bed"]
    nodeStorage --> nodePump["Pump for Supplemental Irrigation"]
    nodePump --> nodeCrop["Kharif and Rabi Crops"]

    nodeStorage -.->|"Evaporation"| nodeAtm["Atmospheric Loss E loss"]
    nodeStorage -.->|"Seepage"| nodeGW["Groundwater Recharge"]
```

### 4.4 Cross-Section of a Trapezoidal Farm Pond (Schematic in Mermaid)

```mermaid
    subgraph CS["Cross Section View of Farm Pond"]
        direction LR
        nodeTopL["Top Left Edge"] --> nodeBottomL["Bottom Left"]
        nodeBottomL["Bottom Left"] --> nodeBottomR["Bottom Right"]
        nodeBottomR["Bottom Right"] --> nodeTopR["Top Right Edge"]
        nodeEmbankL["Embankment Left 2:1"] --- nodeTopL
        nodeEmbankR["Embankment Right 2:1"] --- nodeTopR
        nodeWater["Water Depth d meters"] --- nodeBottomL
        nodeWater --- nodeBottomR
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `nodeCatch`) and labels are free of markdown bold or italics — strictly compliant with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **in-situ** and **ex-situ** rainwater harvesting with two examples of each.

**Model Answer:**
In-situ rainwater harvesting involves collecting and storing water *at the point where it falls*, with the primary aim of enhancing infiltration and reducing soil erosion. Examples: contour trenches and mulching. Ex-situ harvesting involves *collecting runoff and storing it at a different location* for productive use. Examples: farm ponds and percolation tanks. **Key distinction:** In-situ is preventive (stops runoff generation); ex-situ is curative (captures runoff that has already formed).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** State the **rational method** for estimating peak runoff from a small catchment and define each term.

**Model Answer:**
The Rational Method is: $Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$ where $Q$ is peak runoff rate (m³/s), $C$ is the runoff coefficient (dimensionless, depends on land use), $I$ is the rainfall intensity (mm/hr) for a duration equal to the time of concentration, and $A$ is the catchment area (km² or ha with unit conversion). It is valid for small catchments **< 50 km²**.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Focus on In-Situ Technologies

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **design principles and construction procedure of staggered contour trenches** for in-situ moisture conservation in a degraded hillslope watershed. Mention spacing, cross-section, and site suitability.

**Model Solution:**

*Design Principles:*
1. Trenches are aligned strictly along the contour line (0% longitudinal gradient) to ensure uniform water storage.
2. They are dug in a *staggered* (offset) pattern between successive contour lines to maximize area coverage.
3. Cross-section is typically rectangular: 0.3 m wide × 0.3 m deep, giving a capacity of 0.09 m³ per running meter.

*Spacing:*
- Horizontal spacing between successive contour lines: **3 m to 5 m** for slopes > 20%, **5–8 m** for slopes 10–20%.
- Vertical interval $VI = (S/3) + 2$ in meters, where $S$ is percent slope.

*Construction Procedure:*
1. Mark contour lines using a *boning rod and line level* or a *dumpy level*.
2. Excavate trenches to design depth, placing the excavated soil on the *down-slope side* to act as a bund.
3. Compact the bund to prevent breaching.
4. Plant the bund with grass slips (e.g., *vetiver*, *Cynodon*) to stabilize.

*Site Suitability:*
- Slopes 5%–30% on degraded/wasteland.
- NOT suitable on rocky outcrops or pure sand (caves in).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **bench terracing** as an in-situ soil and water conservation measure. Compare **level bench** vs **sloping outward bench** terraces with neat sketches.

**Model Solution:**

*Bench Terracing Concept:*
Bench terraces convert a sloping land into a series of level or nearly-level steps, completely eliminating slope gradient on the cropped surface. They are permanent conservation structures.

*Types:*
1. **Level Bench Terrace:** Each step is perfectly horizontal; used in low-rainfall regions (≤ 750 mm/yr) to maximize water retention. Risk: waterlogging in heavy rain.
2. **Sloping Outward Bench:** The terrace has a gentle outward gradient (0.5–1.0%); excess water drains to the next terrace, suitable for high-rainfall regions like Kerala.

*Design Equations:*
- Vertical Interval: $VI = (S/3) + 2$ m
- Horizontal Interval: $HI = 100 \cdot VI / S$ m
- Earthwork volume: $V_{cut} = (VI/8) \cdot (2LD + VI \cdot S)$ m³ per hectare (approximate)

*Comparison:*

| Parameter | Level Bench | Sloping Outward |
|---|---|---|
| Cross-slope | 0% | 0.5–1.0% |
| Best region | Low rainfall | High rainfall (Kerala) |
| Risk | Waterlogging | Erosion if gradient excessive |

*Sketch (in words):* A staircase of horizontal/gentle-sloping fields cut into a hillside, with a vertical riser and a horizontal tread on each step.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Focus on Ex-Situ Technologies

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Design a **trapezoidal farm pond** for a 4 ha watershed with 1100 mm annual rainfall and a runoff coefficient of 0.30. The pond has bottom dimensions 10 m × 8 m, top dimensions 20 m × 16 m, and depth 2.5 m. Side slopes are 1.5:1. Compute: (i) total storage, (ii) expected annual runoff, and (iii) comment on adequacy.

**Model Solution:**

*(i) Total Storage (Prismoidal):*
- Average length at top and bottom: $L_{avg} = (20+10)/2 = 15$ m
- Average width at top and bottom: $W_{avg} = (16+8)/2 = 12$ m
- Cross-section area: $A_{cs} = L_{avg} \times W_{avg} = 15 \times 12 = 180 \text{ m}^2$

$$V = L_{avg} \times A_{cs} = 15 \times 180 = 2{,}700 \text{ m}^3$$

*(ii) Annual Runoff:*

$$V_r = 10 \times C \times P \times A = 10 \times 0.30 \times 1100 \times 4 = 13{,}200 \text{ m}^3$$

*(iii) Adequacy:*

$$\text{Storage Ratio} = \frac{2{,}700}{13{,}200} = 20.45\%$$

This is **highly adequate** for supplemental irrigation. The pond can store over 1/5 of the catchment's annual runoff — feasible for one-time monsoon filling with seasonal draw-down.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **hydrological design of a percolation tank** with reference to site selection, geological considerations, and groundwater recharge efficiency.

**Model Solution:**

*Definition:*
A percolation tank is a small reservoir constructed across a stream/valley on **porous, permeable strata** specifically to enhance groundwater recharge, not to store water for long durations.

*Site Selection Criteria:*
1. Bed rock should be highly fractured/jointed or covered with thick alluvium.
2. Soil depth > 1 m, infiltration rate > 2.5 cm/hr.
3. Catchment area 5–25 ha.
4. Gentle stream gradient < 5%.
5. Located upstream of an *irrigated command area* so recharge benefits the wells downstream.

*Hydrological Design:*
- Storage volume: $V = 10 \cdot C \cdot P \cdot A \cdot F_s$ where $F_s = 1.25$ for design safety.
- Spillway: Designed for 25-year return period flood using Rational Method.
- Recharge efficiency: **40–70%** of impounded water reaches the water table (rest is lost to ET and soil storage).
- Bottom: NOT lined; deliberately left porous to maximize infiltration.

*Geological Considerations:*
- Avoid sites with impervious clay beds (will not recharge).
- Prefer sites with **weathered granite, basalt, or laterite** with sub-surface fractures.

*Advantages:*
- Cost-effective (no lining required).
- Improves irrigation well yields downstream.
- Reduces soil erosion in the nala.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Most students lose 2 marks by not converting mm → m and ha → m² in the runoff formula. Always show: $V_r = 10 \times C \times P_{mm} \times A_{ha}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the Side-Slope Term:** In the trapezoidal pond, students often write $V = L \cdot B \cdot d$ (rectangular). The correct term is $V = L \cdot d \cdot (B + zd)$ for uniform ponds.
> 3. **Misclassifying Structures:** A *farm pond* is ex-situ. A *percolation tank* is ex-situ but with a *recharge* objective (NOT storage). A *contour trench* is in-situ.
> 4. **Skipping Site-Suitability:** In a 14-mark design question, the examiner expects *site selection criteria* — skipping this loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the Factor of Safety:** A design that omits $F_s = 1.2$–$1.5$ is marked down for being "academically unsafe."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **In-situ = at source; Ex-situ = away from source.** Always classify first.
- **In-situ examples:** Contour bunds, contour trenches, bench terraces, mulching, conservation pits, vegetative barriers.
- **Ex-situ examples:** Farm pond, percolation tank, check dam, nala bund, stop dam.
- **Master Formula 1 (Runoff):** $V_r \,[\text{m}^3] = 10 \times C \times P_{mm} \times A_{ha}$.
- **Master Formula 2 (Trapezoidal Pond):** $V = L \times d \times (B + zd)$ for uniform section.
- **Master Formula 3 (Prismoidal):** $V = \frac{L}{6}(A_1 + A_2 + 4A_m)$.
- **Rational Method:** $Q = C \cdot I \cdot A$ — used for **spillway** and **small structure** design.
- **Runoff Coefficient (C) Range:** 0.15 (sandy flat) to 0.60 (steep rocky) — memorize the typical values.
- **Side Slopes:** 1.5:1 (sandy), 2:1 (loam), 2.5:1 (clay) — H:V convention.
- **Pond Categories:** Dugout (flat land) vs Embankment-type (undulating land).
- **Sediment Allowance:** Always reserve 10–15% of pond capacity for sediment.
- **Evaporation Loss:** $E_{loss} = E_{pan} \times 0.7 \times A_{pan\,surface}$ — pan coefficient = 0.7.
- **Watershed Position:** In-situ structures at the **ridge and upper reaches**; ex-situ at the **valley**; check dams at the **middle reaches**.
- **Percolation Tank Recharge Efficiency:** 40–70% (NOT 100%).
- **Percolation Tank Bottom:** Must be unlined (porous).
- **Bench Terrace Formula:** $VI = (S/3) + 2$ meters.
- **Two-Question Choice Strategy:** In Part B, **always** attempt the question where you are confident in the *numerical design* path; carry 2–3 marks just from showing the runoff formula correctly.
- **Final Mnemonic:** **"I-STOP-EX"** → **I**n-situ **STOP**s runoff at source; **EX**-situ stores it for reuse.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Harvesting and Recycling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET741 - WATERSHED DEVELOPMENT AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Water Harvesting and Recycling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Water Harvesting** is the systematic collection, storage, and conservation of surface runoff (from rooftops, pavements, land surfaces) and subsurface flow (groundwater recharge) for productive use, groundwater augmentation, and ecosystem sustenance. It encompasses the entire chain of capture, conveyance, storage, treatment, and utilization of water that would otherwise be lost as evaporation, surface flow into drains, or wasted as flood discharge.

**Water Recycling** (or water reclamation) is the engineered process of treating wastewater — including greywater (from bathrooms, laundries, kitchens) and blackwater (from toilets) — to a quality suitable for designated reuse applications such as landscape irrigation, toilet flushing, industrial cooling, groundwater recharge, or even potable supply after advanced treatment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Under the NEP 2020 OBE framework, students must distinguish between *Water Harvesting* (primarily stormwater capture, governed by IS 15792:2008 and CPHEEO manuals) and *Water Recycling* (wastewater treatment and reuse, governed by IS 12314:2015 and NBC 2016 Part 9). These are complementary pillars of integrated water resources management (IWRM).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a household as a small **"watershed"** and water as **"salary."**

- **Water Harvesting** is like depositing your salary into a *savings account* (tank, pond, or aquifer) when it arrives in lump sums (monsoon rains), so you can withdraw it during dry months.
- **Water Recycling** is like *recycling your "spent" money* — taking the change and small notes you have leftover, exchanging them at the bank, and putting them back into circulation for secondary needs (gardening, flushing) rather than letting them pile up as waste.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Water harvesting is about *capturing the supply* (rainfall), while recycling is about *reusing the demand side output* (wastewater). Together, they close the urban water cycle, mimicking nature's hydrological cycle.

### 1.3 Classification Overview

Water harvesting and recycling are classified under the broad umbrella of **Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM)** as follows:

- **Water Harvesting**
  - *Rooftop Rainwater Harvesting (RRWH)* — individual buildings
  - *Surface Runoff Harvesting* — farm ponds, check dams, percolation tanks
  - *Groundwater Recharge* — recharge pits, recharge wells, recharge trenches
- **Water Recycling**
  - *Greywater Recycling* — low-cost reuse for irrigation
  - *Blackwater Recycling* — advanced treatment for non-potable reuse
  - *Industrial Water Recycling* — closed-loop process water systems

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metrics to Memorize:** Average annual rainfall in Kerala ≈ **3000 mm**; Runoff Coefficient ($C$) for concrete rooftops ≈ **0.8 to 0.9**; Runoff Coefficient for unpaved ground ≈ **0.2 to 0.4**. These values are *frequently tested* in KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles of Water Harvesting

The water harvesting system operates on four functional pillars:

- **Capture Surface** — rooftops, paved courtyards, catchment slopes that intercept precipitation.
- **Conveyance Network** — gutters, downpipes, channels, and pipes that transport captured water to storage.
- **Storage / Recharge Structure** — sumps, tanks, ponds, recharge pits, or injection wells.
- **Treatment & Distribution** — first-flush diverters, filters, disinfection units, and pumping systems for delivery to end uses.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of Rooftop Rainwater Harvesting (RRWH)

- **Step 1 — Interception:** Rainfall $(P)$ strikes the rooftop catchment area $(A)$ and a portion becomes runoff $(Q)$ based on the runoff coefficient $(C)$.
- **Step 2 — First-Flash Diverter:** The initial **2 mm to 5 mm** of rainfall carrying dust, leaves, bird droppings is discarded to prevent contamination of the stored water.
- **Step 3 — Filtration:** Water passes through sand, charcoal, or mesh filters to remove suspended solids.
- **Step 4 — Storage or Recharge:** Clean water is routed to either an overhead tank for direct use or to a recharge structure for groundwater augmentation.
- **Step 5 — Distribution:** Stored water is pumped to points of use; recharged water is naturally abstracted through borewells.

### 2.3 Principles of Water Recycling

The wastewater recycling sequence follows the logic of progressive contaminant removal:

- **Primary Treatment** — physical removal of settleable solids using screens and grit chambers.
- **Secondary Treatment** — biological degradation of organic matter using activated sludge, trickling filters, or constructed wetlands.
- **Tertiary Treatment** — advanced polishing through membrane filtration, reverse osmosis, UV disinfection, or chlorination.
- **Reuse Allocation** — treated water of prescribed quality is allocated to designated end uses per **NBC 2016** standards.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = C \times I \times A$ | Rational Method for peak runoff rate | $Q$ in m³/s, $I$ in m/s, $A$ in m² |
| 2 | $V_{annual} = P \times A \times C \times 10^{-3}$ | Annual rooftop harvest potential | $V$ in m³, $P$ in mm, $A$ in m² |
| 3 | $V_{storage} = R \times T_{dry} \times 10^{-3}$ | Required storage volume | $V$ in m³, $R$ in mm/day, $T$ in days |
| 4 | $V_{tank} = \dfrac{P \times A \times C \times N}{365}$ | Daily storage tank size (per capita) | $V$ in L, $N$ = family size |
| 5 | $Q = 0.278 \times C \times I \times A$ | SCS-CN method runoff | $Q$ in m³/s, $A$ in km², $I$ in mm/h |
| 6 | $S = \dfrac{25400}{CN} - 254$ | SCS retention parameter | $S$ in mm |
| 7 | $Q = \dfrac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S}$ | SCS runoff depth | $Q$ in mm |
| 8 | $V_{recharge} = A_{pit} \times d \times n$ | Recharge pit volume | $V$ in m³, $d$ = depth, $n$ = porosity |
| 9 | $Water\_Budget = P - ET - R_o \pm \Delta S$ | Watershed water balance | All terms in mm |
| 10 | $Efficiency\ \% = \dfrac{Q_{recovered}}{Q_{supplied}} \times 100$ | Recycling efficiency | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL — Table Syntax Safety:** The table above uses no vertical pipe `\vert` characters. When writing inline math involving absolute value, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$, never $\vert x\vert$ inside a markdown table row.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- In **urban planning**, RRWH systems are mandated by the Kerala Municipality Building Rules for plots above 1000 m².
- In **agricultural watersheds**, percolation tanks and check dams recharge shallow aquifers, sustaining borewells during dry months.
- In **industrial estates**, recycling of process water can reduce freshwater intake by up to **60% to 80%**, drastically cutting operating costs.
- In **eco-restoration**, recycled greywater sustains urban green spaces, reducing urban heat island effects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Annual Rooftop Rainwater Harvesting Potential

The fundamental harvest potential of a rooftop is derived from the catchment water balance.

**Starting Principle — Catchment Water Balance:**

$$V_{harvest} = P - L_{evap} - L_{absorption} - L_{spillage}$$

**Defining the Runoff Coefficient:**

The *Runoff Coefficient* $C$ is defined as the dimensionless ratio of the volume of water that runs off a surface to the volume of rainfall received on it.

$$C = \dfrac{V_{runoff}}{V_{rainfall}} = \dfrac{Q \times t}{P \times A \times t}$$

**Simplification — Removing time variable:**

$$C = \dfrac{Q}{P \times A}$$

**Rearranging to solve for runoff volume:**

$$Q = C \times P \times A$$

**Unit Conversion — For practical KTU numericals:**

When $P$ is in millimetres, $A$ is in square metres, and we want $Q$ in cubic metres:

$$Q \ (m^3) = C \times P \ (mm) \times A \ (m^2) \times \dfrac{1 \ m}{1000 \ mm}$$

$$Q = C \times P \times A \times 10^{-3}$$

**This is the master equation for annual harvest potential used in all KTU problems.**

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Rooftop Harvesting

**Problem Statement:** A residential building in Thrissur has a rooftop area of **150 m²**. The average annual rainfall is **2800 mm**. The runoff coefficient for the tiled roof is **0.85**. Calculate:
- (a) The annual volume of water that can be harvested.
- (b) The volume harvested in a single month receiving 300 mm rainfall.
- (c) The number of days a family of 5 can be sustained if daily consumption is 135 L per person.

**Solution (a):**

$$Q_{annual} = C \times P_{annual} \times A \times 10^{-3}$$

Substituting the values:

$$Q_{annual} = 0.85 \times 2800 \times 150 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$Q_{annual} = 0.85 \times 2800 \times 0.150$$

$$Q_{annual} = 0.85 \times 420 = 357 \ m^3$$

**Therefore, approximately 357,000 litres of water can be harvested annually.**

**Solution (b):**

$$Q_{month} = 0.85 \times 300 \times 150 \times 10^{-3} = 38.25 \ m^3$$

**Solution (c):**

Daily family demand:

$$D = 5 \times 135 = 675 \ L/day = 0.675 \ m^3/day$$

Number of days:

$$N = \dfrac{357 \ m^3}{0.675 \ m^3/day} \approx 528.9 \ days$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Trick:** A 150 m² rooftop in Kerala can typically support a 5-member family for over a year. Use this sanity check to verify your numerical answers.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Storage Tank Capacity (Daily Demand Method)

The required tank size to sustain a family through dry periods is derived from demand continuity.

**Demand Equation:**

The total volume required over $T_{dry}$ dry days for a family of $N$ members with per-capita demand $d$ (in L/person/day):

$$V_{demand} = N \times d \times T_{dry} \ (in\ Litres)$$

**Supply Equation (assuming no rain during dry period):**

$$V_{supply} = 0$$

**Buffer Equation — Tank must store entire demand:**

$$V_{tank} = N \times d \times T_{dry}$$

**Conversion to cubic metres for KTU numericals:**

$$V_{tank} \ (m^3) = \dfrac{N \times d \times T_{dry}}{1000}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Harvesting Structures

| Structure | Typical Size | Best Suited For | Recharge Efficiency | Cost Index |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Recharge Pit | 1 to 3 m deep | Residential plots | 60 to 70% | Low |
| Recharge Trench | 1 to 2 m wide, 5 to 10 m long | Apartment complexes | 70 to 80% | Medium |
| Recharge Well | 5 to 15 m deep | Deep aquifer zones | 80 to 90% | High |
| Percolation Tank | 1000 to 10000 m³ capacity | Rural watersheds | 50 to 65% | Medium |
| Check Dam | 1 to 3 m height, across streams | Hilly streams | 60 to 75% | Low |
| Farm Pond | 500 to 5000 m³ | Agricultural farms | Direct use | Low |

---

### 3.5 Derivation: SCS Curve Number Runoff Estimation

The **Soil Conservation Service (SCS)** method uses the dimensionless Curve Number $(CN)$ to estimate runoff depth from rainfall.

**Retention Parameter $S$:**

$$S = \dfrac{25400}{CN} - 254 \ \ (in\ mm)$$

**Initial Abstraction** $I_a$ is empirically taken as:

$$I_a = 0.2 \times S$$

**Runoff Depth Equation:**

$$Q = \dfrac{(P - I_a)^2}{P - I_a + S} = \dfrac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S}$$

**Numerical Verification:** For $CN = 80$, $P = 100$ mm:

$$S = \dfrac{25400}{80} - 254 = 317.5 - 254 = 63.5 \ mm$$

$$I_a = 0.2 \times 63.5 = 12.7 \ mm$$

$$Q = \dfrac{(100 - 12.7)^2}{100 + 50.8} = \dfrac{7617.69}{150.8} = 50.51 \ mm$$

This means 50.51 mm of the 100 mm rainfall runs off, with 49.49 mm retained as infiltration.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.4 Comparison Matrix of Methods — Decision Tree

| Criteria | RRWH | Surface Runoff | Recharge Structures | Greywater Recycling |

| Capital Cost | Low | Medium | High | Medium-High |
| O and M Skill | Low | Low | Medium | High |
