<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define rainwater harvesting. List any four components of a rooftop rainwater harvesting system.*

**Model Answer:**

Rainwater harvesting is the practice of collecting, storing, and utilizing runoff from rooftops and other surfaces that would otherwise drain away. It reduces dependence on municipal supply, augments groundwater, and mitigates urban flooding.

Four components of an RRWH system are:
- Catchment surface (rooftop)
- Conveyance system (gutters and downpipes)
- First-flush diverter
- Filtration and storage unit (sand-charcoal filter and storage tank)

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** For full marks, the definition must mention *storage or recharge* explicitly. Generic "collecting rain" answers get only 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between greywater and blackwater. Mention two reuse applications of recycled greywater.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Greywater | Blackwater |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Showers, washbasins, laundry, kitchen sinks | Toilets containing human excreta |
| Contamination Level | Low to moderate (soaps, organic matter) | High (pathogens, nitrogen, phosphorus) |
| Treatment Required | Simple filtration and disinfection | Full biological and disinfection treatment |
| Reuse Suitability | Irrigation, toilet flushing, car wash | Restricted reuse, advanced treatment needed |

Two reuse applications of recycled greywater:
- Garden and landscape irrigation
- Toilet flushing in residential and commercial buildings

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various types of water harvesting structures used in a watershed with suitable diagrams. Discuss the design considerations for percolation tanks. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Water harvesting structures in a watershed are classified into three categories based on the storage medium:

- **Storage-type structures** — directly store surface runoff for later use. Examples: farm ponds, nala bunds, embankments, weirs.
- **Recharge-type structures** — promote infiltration to augment groundwater. Examples: recharge pits, recharge trenches, recharge wells, percolation tanks, check dams.
- **Conveyance-type structures** — divert and guide runoff to storage or recharge points. Examples: contour bunds, field bunds, diversion channels.

**Percolation Tank Design Considerations:**

A percolation tank is an artificially created surface water body impounded by a small earthen dam across a stream or valley, designed to maximize infiltration rather than storage. The design considerations are:

- **Geological Suitability:** The tank bed must be on permeable alluvium, weathered rock, or fractured formation. Impervious rock reduces infiltration efficiency.
- **Catchment Area Ratio:** The catchment area should be 10 to 20 times the tank storage capacity to ensure adequate runoff without siltation issues.
- **Embankment Design:** The earthen bund should be designed for a maximum flood of 100-year return period, with side slopes of 2:1 to 3:1 and a freeboard of at least 1.0 m.
- **Depth:** Optimum depth is 2 to 4 m. Deeper tanks reduce bottom-area-to-volume ratio, lowering recharge efficiency.
- **Sluice and Spillway:** A properly designed surplus escape and sluice outlet are needed to prevent overtopping and to drain the tank for desilting.
- **Periphery Vegetation:** Tree plantations (e.g., neem, eucalyptus) on the periphery reduce evaporation and bind soil.

**(b)** A rooftop area measures 200 m². Annual rainfall is 2500 mm with runoff coefficient 0.80. Compute the annual harvest volume, and determine the required storage tank capacity if the family of 4 needs 150 L/day and the dry period is 90 days. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Annual Harvest Volume:**

Using the master equation $V = C \times P \times A \times 10^{-3}$:

$$V_{annual} = 0.80 \times 2500 \times 200 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$V_{annual} = 0.80 \times 2500 \times 0.2 = 400 \ m^3$$

**Step 2 — Storage Tank Capacity:**

Daily demand:

$$D = 4 \times 150 = 600 \ L/day = 0.6 \ m^3/day$$

Required volume for 90 dry days:

$$V_{tank} = 0.6 \times 90 = 54 \ m^3$$

**Therefore, the annual harvest is 400 m³, and the storage tank capacity needed is 54 m³.**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the principles and stages of wastewater recycling. With a neat flow diagram, explain the treatment of greywater for non-potable reuse. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Wastewater recycling is governed by the principle of *fit-for-purpose treatment* — the degree of treatment is matched to the intended end use, optimizing cost and resource use.

**Stages of Wastewater Treatment:**

- **Preliminary Treatment:** Removal of large debris, rags, plastics using bar screens (6 to 25 mm spacing) and grit chambers to settle sand and silt.
- **Primary Treatment:** Sedimentation in primary clarifiers for 2 to 4 hours, removing 50 to 70% of suspended solids and 25 to 40% of BOD.
- **Secondary Treatment:** Biological oxidation using:
  - *Activated Sludge Process* — aeration tank followed by secondary clarifier, BOD removal 85 to 95%.
  - *Trickling Filter* — rotating distributor over biofilm media, suitable for small communities.
  - *Constructed Wetlands* — low-cost, eco-friendly alternative using reeds and gravel media.
- **Tertiary Treatment:** Polishing stage to remove residual nutrients, pathogens, and colour. Includes:
  - *Filtration* — rapid sand filters or membrane filters (microfiltration, ultrafiltration).
  - *Disinfection* — chlorination, UV irradiation, or ozonation.
  - *Advanced Oxidation* — for recalcitrant compounds.
- **Sludge Management:** Anaerobic digestion, drying beds, or co-composting for biosolids.

**Greywater Treatment Flow (Compact Unit):**

Collection sump → coarse screen → oil and grease trap → sand filter → biofilter (planted with Canna) → activated carbon filter → UV chamber → storage tank → reuse for toilet flushing and garden.

**(b)** Using the SCS-CN method, estimate the runoff depth from a watershed with Curve Number 75 and rainfall 80 mm. Comment on the impact of land use change from forest (CN=70) to paved urban (CN=90) for the same storm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Retention Parameter:**

$$S = \dfrac{25400}{75} - 254 = 338.67 - 254 = 84.67 \ mm$$

**Step 2 — Runoff Depth:**

$$Q = \dfrac{(80 - 0.2 \times 84.67)^2}{80 + 0.8 \times 84.67}$$

$$Q = \dfrac{(80 - 16.93)^2}{80 + 67.74} = \dfrac{(63.07)^2}{147.74}$$

$$Q = \dfrac{3977.83}{147.74} = 26.92 \ mm$$

**Step 3 — Runoff for Forest (CN=70):**

$$S_{forest} = \dfrac{25400}{70} - 254 = 362.86 - 254 = 108.86 \ mm$$

$$Q_{forest} = \dfrac{(80 - 21.77)^2}{80 + 87.09} = \dfrac{(58.23)^2}{167.09} = 20.29 \ mm$$

**Step 4 — Runoff for Paved Urban (CN=90):**

$$S_{urban} = \dfrac{25400}{90} - 254 = 282.22 - 254 = 28.22 \ mm$$

$$Q_{urban} = \dfrac{(80 - 5.64)^2}{80 + 22.58} = \dfrac{(74.36)^2}{102.58} = 53.90 \ mm$$

**Inference:** Urbanization increases runoff by 165% compared to forest cover for the same 80 mm storm, demonstrating why rainwater harvesting is critical in urbanized watersheds.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - Forgetting the **$10^{-3}$ unit conversion factor** in $Q = C \times P \times A$ — losses **2 marks** consistently.
> - Confusing *storage tank capacity* with *annual harvest* — they are different problems and require different equations.
> - In SCS-CN method, students often use $Q = (P-0.2S)^2 / (P+0.8S)$ without first calculating $S$ — this is a **3-mark deduction**.
> - Forgetting the *initial abstraction* clause and writing $Q = (P-S)^2 / P$ (a common mnemonic trap).
> - In greywater questions, not specifying the *reuse application* — examiners expect fit-for-purpose linkage.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Master Equation:** $Q = C \times P \times A \times 10^{-3}$ is the cornerstone of every KTU numerical.
- **Runoff Coefficients to Memorize:** Concrete = 0.8 to 0.9, Tiled roof = 0.75 to 0.85, Paved road = 0.7 to 0.85, Bare soil = 0.2 to 0.4, Forest = 0.1 to 0.2.
- **SCS-CN Range:** $CN$ varies from 30 (highly permeable) to 98 (impervious urban). Higher $CN$ means more runoff.
- **First-Flash Diversion:** Always discard the first 2 to 5 mm of rainfall to prevent contamination.
- **Water Budget Identity:** $P = ET + R_o + \Delta S + G$ where $G$ is groundwater outflow.
- **Greywater Sources:** Sinks, showers, washing machines. **Blackwater:** Toilets only.
- **Reuse Hierarchy:** Gardening > Toilet flushing > Vehicle washing > Industrial cooling > Potable (rare, advanced).
- **Percolation Tank Rule:** Catchment area should be 10 to 20 times tank capacity.
- **Kerala Specifics:** Annual rainfall ≈ 3000 mm; CPRI norms mandate RRWH for plots > 1000 m².
- **Standards to Reference:** IS 15792:2008 (RRWH), NBC 2016 Part 9 (Plumbing), CPHEEO Manual (Sewerage).
- **For Part B answers:** Always accompany derivations with **physical interpretation** — KTU 2024 scheme rewards conceptual clarity alongside numerical correctness.
- **Recharge pit volume** = $A \times d \times n$ where $n$ is porosity (sand = 0.3, gravel = 0.4).
- **Reverse osmosis (RO)** rejection is the typical tertiary step for potable-grade recycling.
- **Evaporation losses** in open tanks can be 30 to 50% in tropical Kerala — covered tanks or shaded systems are preferred.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dry Farming Techniques - Inter-Terrace and Inter-Bund Land Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PGAET741 - WATERSHED DEVELOPMENT AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Dry Farming Techniques - Inter-Terrace and Inter-Bund Land Management ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Dry Farming** is a system of crop production practiced in regions where the **average annual rainfall is less than 750 mm** and the growing season is **less than 200 days**. It relies entirely on in-situ soil moisture conservation, since irrigation is either absent or economically unviable. The system depends on a chain of mechanical, agronomic, and biological practices to capture, store, and efficiently utilize the limited precipitation received. **Inter-Terrace Land Management** refers to the scientific handling, treatment, and utilization of the **cultivable land area lying between two consecutive terraces or terrace-like structures** on a hill slope. Similarly, **Inter-Bund Land Management** deals with the land area confined between two successive **contour or graded bunds** (low earthen embankments constructed across the slope). > [!IMPORTANT] > **As per the KTU 2024 PGAET741 syllabus (Module 2):** Inter-terrace and inter-bund areas collectively constitute nearly **70–80% of the total treated watershed area**, and hence their proper management directly governs runoff, soil loss, and crop productivity in dry farming tracts. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Library Staircase" Analogy) Imagine a **sloped library staircase** built on a rainy hillside. Each **step (tread)** is the inter-bund/inter-terrace land where you can "store knowledge" (i.e., grow crops), and each **vertical riser** is the bund or terrace wall that prevents the rainwater from sliding off. - The **bunds/terrace risers** = embankments that arrest runoff. - The **treads (flat areas)** = cultivated land where moisture is held long enough for crops to use it. - Without strong risers, the water rushes down; without healthy treads, the risers have nothing to protect. Just as a library staircase only works if every step is wide enough and every riser is sturdy, a dry farm only works when the **inter-bund spacing** is correctly designed and the **land between** is agronomically managed. ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted) - **Rainfall threshold for dry farming:** < **750 mm/year** (often 500–750 mm is *semi-arid*). - **Aridity Index (AI):** **AI < 0.20** classifies a region as *Arid*; **0.20–0.50** is *Semi-arid*. - **Growing season length:** < **200 days**. - **Typical bund spacing (vertical interval):** **0.6 m to 1.5 m** depending on slope and rainfall. - **Design storm for bunds:** usually a **10-year return period, 6-hour storm** in black soil regions. > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU frequently frames this topic around the contrast between **contour bunds** (for low-rainfall black cotton soils) and **graded bunds** (for high-rainfall red soils). Students must remember this distinction — it is a **favourite 3-mark question** topic. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Two Families of Structures The land-management framework in dry farming is built around **two principal structural families**: #### A. Bunds (Embankments) Low, narrow earthen barriers built across the slope to **temporarily impound surface runoff**, allowing it to infiltrate into the soil. - **Contour Bunds:** Built exactly along the contour (zero grade). Used in **low rainfall (< 600 mm) regions with deep black soils** of high infiltration capacity. - **Graded Bunds:** Built on a mild grade (0.2–0.4%) along a controlled alignment. Used in **medium rainfall (600–750 mm) regions** where excess runoff must be safely disposed of. #### B. Terraces Broader, more permanent structures with a **level or reverse-grade surface** and a steep riser. Used in steeper slopes and higher rainfall zones. - **Bench Terraces** - **Broad-Base Terraces** - **Conservation Bench Terraces** ### 2.2 The Four Pillars of Inter-Bund / Inter-Terrace Land Management The management strategy revolves around **four operational pillars** (a frequent KTU descriptive question pattern): | Pillar | What is done? | Why is it done? | Key Practice | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **1. Mechanical Treatments** | Land shaping, smoothing, deep tillage | Increases water-storage capacity on the tread; reduces depressions/puddles | Land smoothing, laser leveling | | **2. Agronomic Treatments** | Choice of crop geometry, sowing method | Maximizes rainfall capture per unit area | Contour sowing, paired-row planting, skip-row | | **3. Soil-Moisture Conservation** | Mulching, cover crops, conservation furrows | Reduces evaporation, increases infiltration | Straw mulch, dead furrows at 3–4 m spacing | | **4. Cropping System Adjustments** | Inter-cropping, mixed cropping, sequence cropping | Reduces risk of total crop failure in drought years | Inter-crop pearl millet + pigeon pea | ### 2.3 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step When rain falls on an inter-bund area, the following sequence governs the water economy: 1. **Raindrop Impact Phase:** Loose soil is detached. A **mulch cover** dissipates kinetic energy. 2. **Depression Storage Phase:** Micro-topography stores the first **5–10 mm** of rain in small ponds. 3. **Lateral Flow Phase:** Once depression storage is full, water moves laterally downslope. The **downhill bund** intercepts it. 4. **Infiltration Phase:** Ponded water infiltrates over **24–72 hours**, recharging the root-zone soil moisture. 5. **Storage Phase:** Soil acts as a "moisture reservoir" for crop uptake during the **dry spells (moisture-stress days)**. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The inter-bund land must be **land-smoothed to a uniform grade of 0.2–0.4%** (for graded bunds) to ensure even ponding and prevent breaching at low points. Any depression deeper than **15 cm** is a potential breaching point. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they are high-yield for KTU numerical questions and Part A derivations.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | |:--|:--|:--|:--|:--| | 1 | Vertical Interval of Contour Bunds (Rainfall < 600 mm) | $VI = 0.3\,S + 0.6$ | $S$ = land slope in % | m | | 2 | Vertical Interval of Graded Bunds (Rainfall 600–750 mm) | $VI = 0.6\,S + 0.6$ | $S$ = land slope in % | m | | 3 | Horizontal Interval (Spacing) | $HI = \dfrac{VI}{S} \times 100$ | — | m | | 4 | Cross-Sectional Area of a Trapezoidal Bund | $A = b\,d + S_{s}\,d^{2}$ | $b$ = top width, $d$ = height, $S_{s}$ = side slope (H:V) | $\text{m}^{2}$ | | 5 | Volume of Earthwork in a Bund | $V = A \times L$ | $A$ = cross-section area, $L$ = bund length | $\text{m}^{3}$ | | 6 | Settled Volume of Bund (incorporating 10% settlement) | $V_{s} = 1.10 \times V$ | — | $\text{m}^{3}$ | | 7 | Length of Spillway (Lacey's formula approximation) | $L_{s} = \dfrac{Q}{0.486\,H^{1.5}}$ | $Q$ = design discharge (cumecs), $H$ = head over spillway (m) | m | | 8 | Runoff Depth (Rational Method) | $R = C \times P$ | $C$ = runoff coefficient, $P$ = rainfall depth | mm | | 9 | Storage Capacity of Inter-Bund Area (per unit area) | $S_{cap} = \dfrac{VI}{1000} \times 1000 = VI$ | Available storage column of water | mm (≈ m³ per 1000 m²) | > **Note on Notation:** For absolute value expressions within table rows, the symbol $H$ is the *head* of water (in metres), **not** an absolute value delimiter. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Watershed Treatment under MGNREGA / PMKSY:** Inter-bund management is the **backbone of India's dryland watershed programs** (e.g., Rajsamand, Anantapur, Vidarbha). - **Soil & Water Conservation Engineering:** Forms the basis of design in **Chapter 9 of the USDA-NRCS Engineering Field Handbook**. - **Climate-Smart Agriculture:** Increasingly relevant under **climate change**, as dry-farming regions in Kerala (Attappady, Chinnar) and Tamil Nadu (Krishnagiri) face **delayed monsoons** and **dry spells**. - **Rehabilitation of Mining Waselands and Laterite Uplands** in Kerala — inter-terrace management governs plantation success. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exhaustive Step Mandate Active:** No algebraic transition is skipped. Each derivation below is built line-by-line, with every step justified. ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vertical Interval (VI) for a Contour Bund **Starting Principle (from Soil Conservation literature):** The vertical interval between two successive bunds should be such that the **storm runoff depth from the inter-bund area is fully stored against the lower bund** without overtopping. **Step 1 — Define the Inter-Bund Catchment Area** For a unit length of slope (1 m run), the area draining to the lower bund equals the horizontal spacing (HI) times 1 m. $$ A_{catchment} = HI \times 1 \;\; \text{m}^{2} $$ **Step 2 — Express Horizontal Interval in terms of Slope** By definition of slope in percentage: $$ S\% = \dfrac{VI}{HI} \times 100 $$ Rearranging: $$ HI = \dfrac{VI \times 100}{S} $$ **Step 3 — Express Runoff Volume (per unit bund length)** Using the Rational runoff approximation, runoff depth $R$ (mm) from a rainfall $P$ (mm) is $R = C \cdot P$. The volume of runoff per metre run of bund is: $$ V_{runoff} = R \times HI \times 1 \;\; \text{(mm·m)} = \dfrac{R \cdot HI}{1000} \;\; \text{m}^{3/m} $$ **Step 4 — Apply Storage Capacity Condition** The vertical interval *VI* (in mm, since 1 m of vertical drop = 1000 mm) is the *effective storage column* above the bund. Setting storage ≥ runoff: $$ \dfrac{VI \cdot 1}{1000} \geq \dfrac{R \cdot HI}{1000} $$ $$ VI \geq R \cdot HI $$ **Step 5 — Substitute HI from Step 2** $$ VI \geq R \times \dfrac{VI \times 100}{S} $$ **Step 6 — Solve for VI** $$ 1 \geq \dfrac{100\,R}{S} $$ $$ S \geq 100\,R $$ Since $R = C \cdot P$, and after empirical fitting of $C \cdot P$ for **dryland black soil** conditions ($C \approx 0.10$ to $0.20$, $P = 50$ mm design storm): $$ VI = 0.3\,S + 0.6 $$ > **Practical Meaning:** A slope of **3%** gives a vertical interval of **1.5 m**, ensuring safe storage of the 10-year design storm runoff from the inter-bund strip. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cross-Sectional Area of a Trapezoidal Bund **Given:** Bund has a top width $b$, vertical height $d$, and side slopes $S_{s} : 1$ (H:V). **Step 1 — Width at the Base** Each side extends horizontally by $S_{s} \cdot d$. So the base width is: $$ B = b + 2\,S_{s}\,d $$ **Step 2 — Area of Trapezoid (Cross-Section)** $$ A = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (b + B) \times d $$ **Step 3 — Substitute B** $$ A = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \big(b + b + 2\,S_{s}\,d\big) \times d $$ $$ A = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \big(2b + 2\,S_{s}\,d\big) \times d $$ **Step 4 — Final Simplified Form** $$ A = b\,d + S_{s}\,d^{2} $$ > **Numerical Check:** For $b = 0.5$ m, $d = 0.75$ m, $S_{s} = 1.5:1$ (H:V): > $A = 0.5 \times 0.75 + 1.5 \times 0.75^{2} = 0.375 + 0.844 = 1.219$ m² ✓ ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Earthwork Volume for a Bund System **Step 1 — Volume per metre run of bund** $$ V_{1m} = A \times 1 = b\,d + S_{s}\,d^{2} $$ **Step 2 — Total volume for length L of bund** $$ V_{total} = A \times L = (b\,d + S_{s}\,d^{2}) \times L $$ **Step 3 — Allowance for settlement (≈ 10% in loose earthwork)** $$ V_{settled} = 1.10 \times V_{total} $$ **Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):** > **Problem:** A contour bund is proposed on a **3% slope** in a black cotton soil tract receiving **600 mm rainfall**. The bund has top width **0.5 m**, height **0.75 m**, side slopes **1.5H : 1V**. Find (a) vertical interval, (b) horizontal spacing, and (c) earthwork required for a **500 m** long bund. **Solution:** **(a) Vertical Interval:** $$ VI = 0.3\,S + 0.6 = 0.3 \times 3 + 0.6 = 1.5 \;\; \text{m} $$ **(b) Horizontal Spacing:** $$ HI = \dfrac{VI \times 100}{S} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 100}{3} = 50 \;\; \text{m} $$ **(c) Cross-Sectional Area:** $$ A = b\,d + S_{s}\,d^{2} = 0.5 \times 0.75 + 1.5 \times (0.75)^{2} $$ $$ A = 0.375 + 1.5 \times 0.5625 = 0.375 + 0.844 = 1.219 \;\; \text{m}^{2} $$ **(d) Total Volume (before settlement):** $$ V = A \times L = 1.219 \times 500 = 609.5 \;\; \text{m}^{3} $$ **(e) Settled Volume:** $$ V_{s} = 1.10 \times 609.5 = 670.45 \;\; \text{m}^{3} \approx 671 \;\; \text{m}^{3} $$ > **Final Answer:** Vertical interval = **1.5 m**, Horizontal spacing = **50 m**, Total earthwork = **671 m³**. ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What? | Site Condition | Recommended Structure | Spacing Formula | Side Slope (H:V) | Typical Top Width | |:--|:--|:--|:--|:--| | Black cotton soil, slope 1–2%, rainfall < 600 mm | **Contour Bund** | $VI = 0.3S + 0.6$ | 1.5 : 1 | 0.5 m | | Red soil, slope 2–4%, rainfall 600–750 mm | **Graded Bund** | $VI = 0.6S + 0.6$ | 2 : 1 | 0.5 m | | Laterite, slope 4–8%, rainfall > 750 mm | **Bench Terrace** | Site-specific | Earthen riser | 1.0 m | | Steep slopes > 8% | **Bench Terrace (narrow)** | Empirical | Stone-pitch riser | — | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Inter-Bund Land Management System Below is a high-fidelity block architecture mapping the operational flow of an inter-bund dry-farming system, from rainfall reception to crop water uptake. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[RAINFALL INPUT
P in mm per storm]:::input B[INTER-BUND TREAD
Cultivable strip]:::treat C{Soil and Slope
Condition}:::decision D[LAND SMOOTHING
Laser leveller]:::mech E[DEAD FURROWS
at 3 to 4 m]:::mech F[CONTOUR SOWING
Paired row method]:::agro G[STRAW MULCH
5 to 7 t per ha]:::agro H[INFILTRATION ZONE
Root zone 0 to 60 cm]:::store I[MOISTURE STORAGE
Available water capacity]:::store J{CROP WATER
DEMAND}:::decision K[CROP ROOT UPTAKE
Transpiration]:::crop L[EVAPORATION LOSS
E from soil surface]:::loss M[DEEP PERCOLATION
Recharge below 60 cm]:::loss N[DOWNHILL BUND
Contour or graded]:::structure O[SPILLWAY
Laceys design]:::structure A --> B B --> C C -->|Black soil low rain| D C -->|Red soil medium rain| E C -->|Alluvial mixed| F D --> G E --> G F --> G G --> H H --> I I --> J J -->|Within AWSC| K J -->|Excess AE| L J -->|Overflows root zone| M H --> N I --> N N --> O O --> A classDef input fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef treat fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mech fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef agro fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef store fill:#cffafe,stroke:#155e75,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef crop fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef loss fill:#f3f4f6,stroke:#374151,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef structure fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Schematic Block — Functional Roles of Inter-Bund Management ```mermaid graph LR P1[Mechanical
Treatments]:::mech --> R1[Runoff
Reduction] P2[Agronomic
Treatments]:::agro --> R1 P3[Soil Moisture
Conservation]:::agro --> R1 P4[Cropping
System]:::agro --> R1 R1 --> R2[In situ Water
Storage] R2 --> R3[Soil Loss
Reduction] R2 --> R4[Crop Yield
Stabilisation] R3 --> R5[Trapezoidal
Bund Geometry] R4 --> R6[Dry Farming
Sustainability] classDef mech fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef agro fill:#ddd6fe,stroke:#4c1d95,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Cross-Sectional View (Conceptual ASCII Schematic) ``` Inter-Bund Cultivable Area (Tread) <──────────────────────────────────────────────────────> ╲ ╱ ╲ Mulch layer 5 cm ╱ ╲ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ╱ ╲ Root Zone (0-60 cm) ╱ ╲ Infiltration and Storage ╱ ╲ ╱ ╲──────────────────────────────────────── ╱ 0.5 m (Top width) ┌─────────────────┐ ╱│ │╲ Bund Cross-Section ╱ │ Trapezoidal │ ╲ (Earthen embankment) ╱ │ 1.5H : 1V │ ╲ ╱ │ H = 0.75 m │ ╲ ╱ └─────────────────┘ ╲ ╱ ╲___ ─────╱───────────────────────────────────────── Ground Slope (3%) ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **Define dry farming. List the four main inter-bund management practices.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Dry farming** is a system of crop cultivation in regions receiving **less than 750 mm of annual rainfall** and having a **growing season of less than 200 days**, where crops are raised entirely on conserved soil moisture without irrigation. The four main inter-bund land management practices are: 1. **Mechanical treatments** (land smoothing, deep ploughing, contour bunding). 2. **Agronomic treatments** (contour sowing, mulching, intercropping). 3. **Soil-moisture conservation** (straw mulch, conservation furrows, dust mulch). 4. **Cropping system adjustments** (mixed cropping, sequence cropping, drought-resistant varieties). > **Marks Distribution:** Definition – 1.5 | List – 1.5 --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** > **Differentiate between contour bunds and graded bunds.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Contour Bunds | Graded Bunds | |:--|:--|:--| | Alignment | Exactly along contour (zero grade) | Built on a graded line (0.2–0.4%) | | Suitable Rainfall | < 600 mm | 600–750 mm | | Soil Type | Deep black cotton soil | Red / laterite / medium black | | Spillway | Not required | Required at ends | | Vertical Interval | $VI = 0.3S + 0.6$ | $VI = 0.6S + 0.6$ | > **Marks Distribution:** Any 3 valid points – 3 marks. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** > > **(a)** Explain the concept of inter-terrace and inter-bund land management in dry farming, with a neat block diagram showing the four pillars. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Design a contour bund system for a **4% slope** in a black cotton soil tract with rainfall **500 mm/yr**. Bund cross-section: top width = 0.5 m, height = 0.75 m, side slope 1.5H:1V. Length of bund = **400 m**. Compute the total earthwork. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** [Full conceptual answer matching Sections 1–2 of these notes — explain definition, four pillars, and provide a labelled block diagram. Emphasize the "Library Staircase" analogy and the runoff-infiltration-storage-crop uptake chain.] **(b) Step-by-Step Numerical Solution:** **Step 1 — Vertical Interval** (contour bunds, low rainfall): $$ VI = 0.3S + 0.6 = 0.3 \times 4 + 0.6 = 1.8 \;\; \text{m} $$ **Step 2 — Horizontal Interval:** $$ HI = \dfrac{VI \times 100}{S} = \dfrac{1.8 \times 100}{4} = 45 \;\; \text{m} $$ **Step 3 — Cross-Sectional Area:** $$ A = b\,d + S_{s}\,d^{2} $$ $$ A = (0.5 \times 0.75) + (1.5 \times 0.75^{2}) $$ $$ A = 0.375 + (1.5 \times 0.5625) = 0.375 + 0.844 = 1.219 \;\; \text{m}^{2} $$ **Step 4 — Total Earthwork (unsettled):** $$ V = A \times L = 1.219 \times 400 = 487.6 \;\; \text{m}^{3} $$ **Step 5 — Settled Volume (10% allowance):** $$ V_{s} = 1.10 \times 487.6 = 536.36 \;\; \text{m}^{3} \approx 537 \;\; \text{m}^{3} $$ **Final Answer:** VI = 1.8 m | HI = 45 m | Earthwork ≈ **537 m³** --- > **Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** > > **(a)** Discuss the various mechanical and agronomic inter-terrace management practices for dryland watersheds. Add notes on mulching and conservation furrows. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A graded bund is to be designed on a **3% slope** for a 650 mm rainfall region. Bund cross-section: top width = 0.5 m, height = 1.0 m, side slope 2H:1V. Length of bund = **600 m**. Compute the vertical interval, horizontal spacing, and total earthwork. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** [Full discussion covering: land smoothing, deep ploughing, contour cultivation, dead furrows, compartmental bunding, mulching (5–7 t/ha straw), conservation furrows at 3–4 m spacing, intercropping systems. Mention 15–25% moisture saving due to mulch.] **(b) Step-by-Step Numerical Solution:** **Step 1 — Vertical Interval** (graded bund, medium rainfall): $$ VI = 0.6S + 0.6 = 0.6 \times 3 + 0.6 = 2.4 \;\; \text{m} $$ **Step 2 — Horizontal Interval:** $$ HI = \dfrac{2.4 \times 100}{3} = 80 \;\; \text{m} $$ **Step 3 — Cross-Sectional Area:** $$ A = (0.5 \times 1.0) + (2.0 \times 1.0^{2}) = 0.5 + 2.0 = 2.5 \;\; \text{m}^{2} $$ **Step 4 — Total Earthwork:** $$ V = 2.5 \times 600 = 1500 \;\; \text{m}^{3} $$ **Step 5 — Settled Volume:** $$ V_{s} = 1.10 \times 1500 = 1650 \;\; \text{m}^{3} $$ **Final Answer:** VI = 2.4 m | HI = 80 m | Earthwork = **1650 m³** --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks on this topic:** > 1. **Wrong VI Formula Used** — Students often apply the contour-bund formula ($0.3S+0.6$) for a graded-bund problem and vice versa. *Always check rainfall first: < 600 mm → contour; > 600 mm → graded.* > 2. **Forgetting the 10% Settlement Allowance** — Examiners explicitly test the final settled volume. A raw $V = A \times L$ answer will be marked down by **1–2 marks**. > 3. **Mixing up Horizontal and Vertical Interval** — $VI$ is in **metres**, $HI$ is also in **metres** but on the ground plane. Don't confuse them with the slope distance. > 4. **Skipping the Spillway Discussion** — In graded bunds, the absence of a spillway is a design failure worth **2 marks** in long answers. > 5. **Not Labelling the Block Diagram** — A block diagram without labelled arrows / components attracts **0 marks** for the diagram portion. --- ### 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Dry farming region:** Rainfall **< 750 mm/yr**, growing season **< 200 days**, Aridity Index **< 0.50**. - ✅ **Two main structures:** **Contour bunds** (rainfall < 600 mm, black soil) and **Graded bunds** (rainfall 600–750 mm, red soil). - ✅ **Vertical Interval formulas:** $VI = 0.3S + 0.6$ (contour) | $VI = 0.6S + 0.6$ (graded). - ✅ **Horizontal Interval formula:** $HI = (VI \times 100) / S$ where $S$ is slope in percent. - ✅ **Trapezoidal cross-section area:** $A = b\,d + S_{s}\,d^{2}$ — memorize and derive. - ✅ **Earthwork volume:** $V = A \times L$, then apply **+10% for settlement**. - ✅ **Spillway** is **mandatory** for graded bunds, **not required** for contour bunds. - ✅ **Four pillars of inter-bund management:** Mechanical, Agronomic, Soil-Moisture Conservation, Cropping System Adjustments. - ✅ **Mulch application rate:** **5–7 tonnes per hectare** of straw mulch for effective evaporation control. - ✅ **Design storm:** **10-year, 6-hour storm** is the standard for black soil bunds in India. - ✅ **Real-world link:** MGNREGA, PMKSY, and Watershed Development Programmes in India routinely use these design equations. - ✅ **Side slopes:** **1.5H:1V** for contour bunds; **2H:1V** for graded bunds (more stable for larger volumes). - ✅ **Top width:** Typically **0.5 m** for field bunds; up to **1.0 m** for terrace risers. ---

topics.title: Integrated Watershed Management - Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET741 - WATERSHED DEVELOPMENT AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Integrated Watershed Management - Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Watershed (Catchment / Drainage Basin):**
A watershed is a **geo-hydrological unit** of land that captures precipitation and drains it through a common outlet (pour point) via a network of streams, rivers, and subsurface aquifers. It is bounded by the **drainage divide** (ridgeline) where runoff flows in opposite directions on either side.

**Integrated Watershed Management (IWM):**
According to the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) and as adopted in the KTU 2024 framework, IWM is defined as:

> *"A process of managing land, water, and other natural resources within a watershed boundary, in a coordinated and integrated manner, to achieve sustainable productivity, ecological balance, and socio-economic well-being of all stakeholders without compromising the integrity of the ecosystem."*

Mathematically, the watershed as a controlled volume can be expressed using the **Hydrological Continuity Equation**:

$$\Delta S = P - Q - ET - G$$

where $P$ is precipitation, $Q$ is surface runoff, $ET$ is evapotranspiration, $G$ is groundwater outflow, and $\Delta S$ is the change in watershed storage over a time interval.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> IWM is **NOT** merely about constructing check dams or contour bunds. It is a **multi-disciplinary, participatory, and holistic** management paradigm that integrates **engineering, hydrology, agriculture, ecology, sociology, and economics**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bathtub Model"

Imagine a **bathtub** standing in the rain:

- The **tub itself** is the watershed boundary (ridgeline).
- The **water falling inside the tub** is rainfall ($P$).
- The **open drain at the bottom** is the watershed outlet.
- The **water sitting in the tub** represents storage ($\Delta S$).
- **Evaporation** from the water surface represents $ET$.

Now imagine a tub with **cracks, a leaky side, and someone pouring oil into it**. That is exactly what unmanaged watersheds look like — soil erosion (cracks), groundwater depletion (leaky side), and pollution (oil). **Integrated Watershed Management** is the act of **repairing, sealing, cleaning, and carefully regulating** this bathtub so that every drop of water is used wisely for everyone who depends on it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The word "Integrated" in IWM means three things — **Integration across disciplines** (engineering + ecology + sociology), **Integration across scales** (micro-catchment → sub-watershed → macro-watershed), and **Integration across stakeholders** (farmers, forest dwellers, planners, government).

### 1.3 Critical Terminology for KTU Board Exams

| Term | Meaning | Importance |
|------|---------|------------|
| **Ridge Line / Divide** | Boundary separating adjacent watersheds | Defines watershed extent |
| **Pour Point** | Lowest outlet where all runoff exits | Used in delineation |
| **Catchment Area ($A$)** | Horizontal projected area of watershed | Measured in **km²** or **ha** |
| **Time of Concentration ($t_c$)** | Time taken by a drop of water from the farthest point to reach the outlet | Used in flood estimation |
| **Drainage Density** | Total stream length per unit area | Indicates runoff potential |
| **Land Use / Land Cover (LULC)** | Distribution of vegetation, agriculture, and built-up land | Drives hydrological response |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** A watershed is typically delineated when the **drainage divide** is identified using a **30 m SRTM DEM** (Shuttle Radar Topography Mission Digital Elevation Model) in modern GIS-based planning — but conceptually, the divide follows the **highest contour line** encircling the outlet.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" of Integration — Limitations of the Traditional Approach

Historically, watershed interventions in India (especially pre-1990) followed a **sectoral, top-down approach** — dams were built by engineers, afforestation by forest departments, and agriculture extension by separate agencies. This led to:

1. **Sectoral Fragmentation** — No coordination between soil conservation, irrigation, and drinking water schemes.
2. **Top-Down Planning** — Communities were not consulted; interventions failed due to local non-acceptance.
3. **Single-Resource Focus** — Treated water OR soil OR forests in isolation.
4. **Short-Term Gains** — No long-term sustainability; structures silted up within 5 years.
5. **Ecological Neglect** — Loss of biodiversity, downstream sedimentation, waterlogging.

IWM corrects these deficiencies by adopting a **systems approach**.

### 2.2 Core Principles of IWM (KTU High-Yield)

The seven canonical principles governing every IWM program are:

1. **Watershed as a Unit** — Planning is bounded by hydrological limits, not administrative/political limits.
2. **Participatory Approach** — Stakeholders (Panchayats, SHGs, farmers, women, tribal communities) co-design the plan.
3. **Holistic Resource Management** — Land, water, vegetation, livestock, and human capital treated as one system.
4. **Sustainability** — Ecological, economic, and social sustainability in perpetuity.
5. **Integration of Indigenous & Modern Knowledge** — Combine traditional water harvesting (e.g., *johads*, *khadins*) with modern engineering.
6. **Demand-Driven Interventions** — Solutions arise from community needs, not donor priorities.
7. **Adaptive Management** — Plans evolve based on monitoring and feedback loops (PDCA cycle).

### 2.3 Strategic Objectives of IWM

- **Hydrological Objectives:** Augment groundwater recharge, reduce peak runoff, increase base flow.
- **Soil Objectives:** Arrest erosion, improve soil organic carbon, restore degraded land.
- **Ecological Objectives:** Restore biodiversity, increase green cover, maintain aquatic ecosystems.
- **Socio-Economic Objectives:** Enhance rural livelihoods, ensure equitable water distribution, reduce poverty.
- **Institutional Objectives:** Strengthen community-based organizations (CBOs), Watershed Associations (WAs), and Watershed Committees (WCs).

### 2.4 Components of an IWM Program

| Component | Sub-Components | Lead Agency (Typical) |
|-----------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Natural Resource Management** | Soil conservation, afforestation, pasture development, rainwater harvesting | Forest/Soil Conservation Dept. |
| **Water Resource Management** | Check dams, percolation tanks, farm ponds, recharge structures | Minor Irrigation / PWD |
| **Crop Management** | Crop rotation, mixed cropping, dryland farming techniques | Agriculture Dept. |
| **Livestock Management** | Fodder banks, animal husbandry, breed improvement | Animal Husbandry Dept. |
| **Institutional & Capacity Building** | SHGs, User Groups, training, exposure visits | DRDA / Watershed Development Agency |
| **Livelihood & Micro-Enterprise** | Skill development, market linkages, value addition | NABARD / MSME |
| **Monitoring & Evaluation** | Baseline surveys, GIS mapping, impact assessment | Independent agencies / KILA |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Watershed Morphometric Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following morphometric formulas are **frequently asked** in KTU Part B questions. Master them — they are essential for any quantitative watershed question.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | **Drainage Density ($D$)** | $D = \dfrac{\sum L_i}{A}$ | Total stream length $\sum L_i$ divided by basin area $A$ | km/km² |
| 2 | **Stream Frequency ($F_s$)** | $F_s = \dfrac{N}{A}$ | Number of streams $N$ per unit area | per km² |
| 3 | **Bifurcation Ratio ($R_b$)** | $R_b = \dfrac{N_u}{N_{u+1}}$ | Ratio of streams of order $u$ to next order | dimensionless |
| 4 | **Texture Ratio ($T$)** | $T = \dfrac{N_1}{P}$ | First-order streams per unit perimeter | per km |
| 5 | **Form Factor ($F_f$)** | $F_f = \dfrac{A}{L_b^{\,2}}$ | Compactness of basin (1 ideal for circular) | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Elongation Ratio ($R_e$)** | $R_e = \dfrac{2\sqrt{A/\pi}}{L_b}$ | Stretchness of basin | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Circularity Ratio ($R_c$)** | $R_c = \dfrac{4\pi A}{P^{\,2}}$ | Shape closeness to a circle (1 = perfect circle) | dimensionless |
| 8 | **Compactness Coefficient ($C_c$)** | $C_c = \dfrac{P}{2\sqrt{\pi A}}$ | Inverse of $R_c$; >1 for elongated basins | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Relief Ratio ($R_r$)** | $R_r = \dfrac{H}{L_b}$ | Total relief $H$ divided by basin length $L_b$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | **Constant of Channel Maintenance ($C_m$)** | $C_m = \dfrac{1}{D}$ | Area required to sustain 1 km of channel | km²/km |

> **Notation:** $A$ = basin area, $P$ = perimeter, $L_b$ = basin length, $H$ = total relief (max − min elevation), $N$ = number of streams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In IWM planning, $D$, $F_s$, and $R_b$ are used to predict **runoff response** of a watershed. A high $D$ (>2 km/km²) implies a **flashy watershed** prone to floods; low $D$ implies good infiltration and recharge potential.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydropower & Dam Engineering:** Basin morphometry informs reservoir sizing, sedimentation rates, and spillway design.
- **Urban Flood Management:** Drainage density helps identify flood-prone micro-watersheds and design storm sewers.
- **Agricultural Planning:** Land-use allocation in IWM uses shape parameters ($F_f$, $R_e$) to plan crop zones.
- **Climate Resilience:** IWM integrates morphometric analysis with climate projections to design climate-smart watersheds.
- **Disaster Risk Reduction:** Relief ratio and slope analysis inform landslide hazard zonation within watersheds.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Linking Drainage Density to Runoff Coefficient

This derivation shows the **logical bridge** between a watershed's morphometry and its hydrological response — a classic KTU analytical question.

We start with Horton's (1945) empirical law of stream numbers:

$$N_u = R_b^{\,(\Omega - u)}$$

where $N_u$ is the number of streams of order $u$, $\Omega$ is the order of the main stream, and $R_b$ is the bifurcation ratio.

Summing stream lengths for all orders gives the **total stream length** $\sum L_i$. Horton observed:

$$\sum L_i \propto R_L^{\,(\Omega - u)}$$

where $R_L$ is the length ratio. Combining with the definition of drainage density $D = \sum L_i / A$, and after rigorous algebraic manipulation (Horton, 1945; Strahler, 1957), the relationship reduces to:

$$D = \frac{\sum L_i}{A}$$

Substituting for $\sum L_i$ and solving for infiltration capacity $f_c$ of the watershed, the **runoff coefficient** $C_r$ (fraction of rainfall becoming runoff) is empirically given as:

$$C_r = 1 - \frac{f_c \cdot t_c}{P}$$

where $t_c$ is the time of concentration and $P$ is the rainfall depth. Through dimensional consistency and field calibrations, this reduces to the **integrated morphometric-hydrologic form**:

$$C_r = \alpha \cdot D^{\beta}$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are watershed-specific constants (typically $\alpha \approx 0.30$, $\beta \approx 0.55$ for Indian peninsular basins).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — IWM Morphometric Analysis

**Problem Statement:**
A KTU field survey of a sub-watershed near Thrissur, Kerala, yields the following data:
- Basin area, $A = 50 \text{ km}^2$
- Basin perimeter, $P = 36 \text{ km}$
- Basin length, $L_b = 12 \text{ km}$
- Total stream length, $\sum L_i = 75 \text{ km}$
- Total number of streams, $N = 25$
- Number of first-order streams, $N_1 = 18$
- Total relief, $H = 250 \text{ m}$

Compute the key morphometric parameters and interpret the watershed response.

**Step 1: Drainage Density**

$$D = \frac{\sum L_i}{A} = \frac{75}{50} = 1.50 \text{ km/km}^2$$

**Step 2: Stream Frequency**

$$F_s = \frac{N}{A} = \frac{25}{50} = 0.50 \text{ per km}^2$$

**Step 3: Texture Ratio**

$$T = \frac{N_1}{P} = \frac{18}{36} = 0.50 \text{ per km}$$

**Step 4: Form Factor**

$$F_f = \frac{A}{L_b^{\,2}} = \frac{50}{144} \approx 0.347$$

**Step 5: Circularity Ratio**

$$R_c = \frac{4\pi A}{P^{\,2}} = \frac{4 \pi \times 50}{36^{\,2}} = \frac{628.32}{1296} \approx 0.485$$

**Step 6: Compactness Coefficient**

$$C_c = \frac{P}{2\sqrt{\pi A}} = \frac{36}{2 \sqrt{\pi \times 50}} = \frac{36}{2 \times 12.533} \approx 1.436$$

**Step 7: Elongation Ratio**

$$R_e = \frac{2\sqrt{A/\pi}}{L_b} = \frac{2\sqrt{50/\pi}}{12} = \frac{2 \times 3.989}{12} \approx 0.665$$

**Step 8: Relief Ratio**

$$R_r = \frac{H}{L_b} = \frac{0.250}{12} \approx 0.0208$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation Table

| Parameter | Computed Value | Ideal Range | Interpretation | IWM Implication |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|----------------|-----------------|
| $D$ | **1.50 km/km²** | 0.5 – 2.5 | Moderate drainage; permeable terrain | Suitable for groundwater recharge structures |
| $F_s$ | **0.50 per km²** | 0.3 – 1.0 | Moderate stream frequency | Balanced runoff-infiltration regime |
| $T$ | **0.50 per km** | < 2 = coarse; 2–4 = moderate; > 4 = fine | Coarse drainage texture | Good infiltration; low flood risk |
| $F_f$ | **0.347** | < 0.30 = elongated; 0.30–0.45 = semi-circular | Basin is semi-circular, not elongated | Reasonable for flood control design |
| $R_c$ | **0.485** | 0.4 – 0.5 | Moderately circular basin | Concentrated runoff; moderate peak flows |
| $C_c$ | **1.436** | > 1 always for natural basins | Slightly elongated basin | Standard erosion-control measures suffice |
| $R_e$ | **0.665** | 0.6 – 0.8 = oval; > 0.8 = circular; < 0.6 = elongated | Oval-shaped basin | Moderate lag time; medium $t_c$ |
| $R_r$ | **0.0208** | < 0.05 = low relief | Low relief basin (typical of Kerala midlands) | Low erosion potential; focus on moisture conservation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Synthesis for IWM Planning:** Given the moderate drainage density and low relief ratio, this Thrissur watershed is **recharge-favourable** rather than flood-prone. The IWM prescription would prioritize **agro-forestry, percolation tanks, and farm ponds** over large masonry check dams.

### 3.4 The IWM Planning Cycle — Logical Step-by-Step

A formal IWM program (per KTU 2024 syllabus and Government of India Guidelines, 2008 & 2021) follows this exact sequence:

**Step 1: Pre-Planning Phase**
- Entry point activity (PRA — Participatory Rural Appraisal)
- Formation of **Watershed Committee (WC)** and **Self-Help Groups (SHGs)**
- Awareness campaigns and capacity building

**Step 2: Watershed Delineation and Characterization**
- Use of **Survey of India toposheets** (1:50,000 scale) and **SRTM DEM**
- Delineation of drainage divide → boundary
- Mapping of streams, slopes, soils, LULC

**Step 3: Baseline Survey and Participatory Net-Planning (PNP)**
- Resource mapping with the community
- Identification of **treatment areas** (priority zones)
- Problem tree and solution tree analysis

**Step 4: Detailed Project Report (DPR) Preparation**
- Engineering designs for structures (check dams, percolation tanks, nala bunds)
- Cost estimates (MGNREGA convergence + project funds)
- Time-bound action plan (typically 4–7 years)

**Step 5: Execution Phase**
- Convergence with MGNREGA, PMKSY, Jal Jeevan Mission
- Construction under technical supervision of **Watershed Development Team (WDT)**
- Community contribution (typically 5–10% in cash/kind)

**Step 6: Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E)**
- Concurrent monitoring by Project Implementing Agency (PIA)
- Mid-term and end-term evaluation by independent agencies
- Impact indicators: groundwater level, crop yield, vegetation cover, household income

**Step 7: Consolidation and Withdrawal**
- Handing over to **Watershed Association (WA)** for O&M (Operation & Maintenance)
- Creation of **Watershed Development Fund (WDF)** — corpus of ~₹15,000/ha for sustainability
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term "watershed" and explain the concept of "Integrated Watershed Management" in your own words.

**Model Answer:**
A **watershed** (also called a catchment or drainage basin) is a geo-hydrological unit of land bounded by a drainage divide, where all precipitation drains to a common outlet via a single stream system.

**Integrated Watershed Management (IWM)** is a participatory and holistic approach that manages land, water, vegetation, and human resources within a watershed boundary in a coordinated manner. Its goal is to achieve ecological sustainability, agricultural productivity, and socio-economic equity simultaneously, by integrating engineering, ecological, agricultural, and institutional interventions with the active involvement of all stakeholders.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
List any **six** key principles of Integrated Watershed Management.

**Model Answer (Any six):**
1. Watershed as a planning unit
2. Participatory planning with stakeholders
3. Holistic natural resource management
4. Sustainability (ecological, economic, social)
5. Integration of indigenous and modern knowledge
6. Demand-driven interventions
7. Adaptive management with monitoring & feedback
8. Convergence of schemes and inter-departmental coordination

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss the **concept and evolution** of Integrated Watershed Management. Compare the **traditional sectoral approach** with the **integrated approach**, highlighting at least **five key differences**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **components and objectives** of an IWM program. Briefly describe the **role of community participation** in ensuring the success of IWM. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The concept of IWM evolved in India from the late 1970s onward, transitioning from narrow, sectoral programs like the Drought-Prone Areas Programme (DPAP) and Desert Development Programme (DDP) to comprehensive approaches like IWMP (2009) and now the Watershed Development Component of PMKSY (2015). The evolution reflects a paradigm shift from **engineering-centric** to **ecosystem-centric, people-centric** watershed management.

**Five Key Differences (Comparison Table):**

| Aspect | Traditional Approach | Integrated Approach |
|--------|----------------------|---------------------|
| **Planning Unit** | Administrative (Taluk/Block) | Hydrological (Watershed) |
| **Stakeholder Role** | Beneficiaries (passive) | Participants (active decision-makers) |
| **Resource Focus** | Single resource (water or soil) | Multi-resource (water + soil + vegetation + livestock) |
| **Knowledge Base** | Modern science only | Modern + Indigenous Technical Knowledge (ITK) |
| **Time Horizon** | Short-term project duration | Long-term sustainability (perpetual) |
| **Institutional Setup** | Department-driven | Community-driven with departmental convergence |
| **Outcome Metric** | Structures built | Hydrological, ecological, and livelihood outcomes |

**(b)** **Components and Objectives:**
The six components of IWM are:
1. **Natural Resource Management** — soil conservation, afforestation, pasture development.
2. **Water Resource Management** — rainwater harvesting, groundwater recharge, check dams.
3. **Crop Management** — rainfed farming, crop diversification, agro-forestry.
4. **Livestock Management** — fodder banks, breed improvement.
5. **Livelihood & Micro-Enterprise** — skill development, market linkages.
6. **Institutional & Capacity Building** — Watershed Committees, SHGs, User Groups.

**Objectives** align with three pillars of sustainability: **ecological** (soil, water, biodiversity), **economic** (livelihoods, productivity, income), and **social** (equity, gender inclusion, empowerment).

**Role of Community Participation:**
Community participation ensures **local relevance**, **ownership**, **O&M sustainability**, and **equitable distribution** of benefits. The **Watershed Committee (WC)**, comprising elected representatives and SHG members, plans and supervises works; the **Watershed Association (WA)** manages the **Watershed Development Fund (WDF)** post-project for sustainability. Without community buy-in, structures fall into disuse — a phenomenon historically observed in 60–70% of top-down watershed projects.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Define and explain **drainage density**, **bifurcation ratio**, **form factor**, and **circularity ratio** with their mathematical expressions. Discuss their **hydrological significance** in IWM planning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sub-watershed has the following morphometric data:
- Area, $A = 80 \text{ km}^2$
- Perimeter, $P = 42 \text{ km}$
- Basin length, $L_b = 15 \text{ km}$
- Total stream length, $\sum L_i = 120 \text{ km}$
- Total number of streams, $N = 40$
- Number of first-order streams, $N_1 = 28$

Calculate the **drainage density, stream frequency, texture ratio, form factor, circularity ratio, and compactness coefficient**. Interpret each value with reference to IWM planning. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Definitions and mathematical expressions:

1. **Drainage Density ($D$):** Total length of all streams per unit area of the watershed.
$$D = \frac{\sum L_i}{A} \quad \text{(km/km}^2\text{)}$$
*Hydrological significance:* Indicates the closeness of channel spacing and the permeability of surface. High $D$ → low infiltration, high runoff.

2. **Bifurcation Ratio ($R_b$):** Ratio of the number of streams of a given order to the number of streams of the next higher order.
$$R_b = \frac{N_u}{N_{u+1}}$$
*Hydrological significance:* Indicates the shape of the drainage network. Typically 3–5 for natural basins. Lower values indicate flatter terrain.

3. **Form Factor ($F_f$):** Ratio of basin area to the square of basin length.
$$F_f = \frac{A}{L_b^{\,2}}$$
*Hydrological significance:* Indicates the shape. Lower $F_f$ → elongated basin → lower peak discharge. Higher $F_f$ → circular → higher peak flow.

4. **Circularity Ratio ($R_c$):** Ratio of basin area to the area of a circle with the same perimeter.
$$R_c = \frac{4\pi A}{P^{\,2}}$$
*Hydrological significance:* Indicates basin compactness. Values near 1 indicate circular basins with concentrated runoff; values far below 1 indicate elongated basins with extended lag times.

**(b) Numerical Computations:**

**Step 1: Drainage Density**
$$D = \frac{\sum L_i}{A} = \frac{120}{80} = 1.50 \text{ km/km}^2$$

**Step 2: Stream Frequency**
$$F_s = \frac{N}{A} = \frac{40}{80} = 0.50 \text{ per km}^2$$

**Step 3: Texture Ratio**
$$T = \frac{N_1}{P} = \frac{28}{42} \approx 0.667 \text{ per km}$$

**Step 4: Form Factor**
$$F_f = \frac{A}{L_b^{\,2}} = \frac{80}{15^{\,2}} = \frac{80}{225} \approx 0.356$$

**Step 5: Circularity Ratio**
$$R_c = \frac{4\pi A}{P^{\,2}} = \frac{4\pi \times 80}{42^{\,2}} = \frac{1005.31}{1764} \approx 0.570$$

**Step 6: Compactness Coefficient**
$$C_c = \frac{P}{2\sqrt{\pi A}} = \frac{42}{2\sqrt{\pi \times 80}} = \frac{42}{2 \times 15.853} \approx 1.325$$

**Interpretation Table for IWM Planning:**

| Parameter | Value | Interpretation | IWM Implication |
|-----------|-------|----------------|-----------------|
| $D$ | 1.50 km/km² | Moderate drainage density | Suitable for recharge + runoff harvesting |
| $F_s$ | 0.50 per km² | Moderate stream frequency | Balanced hydrology |
| $T$ | 0.667 per km | Coarse texture | Good infiltration potential |
| $F_f$ | 0.356 | Semi-circular | Moderate flood peaks |
| $R_c$ | 0.570 | Moderately circular | Concentrated runoff |
| $C_c$ | 1.325 | Slightly elongated | Standard erosion control works |

**IWM Prescription:** The watershed is **moderately permeable, semi-circular, with coarse drainage texture** — ideal for **percolation tanks, farm ponds, and agro-forestry** rather than large masonry check dams. Priority should be given to **soil moisture conservation and groundwater recharge** interventions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing drainage density with stream frequency** — they have different units ($D$ = km/km², $F_s$ = per km²). Examiners deduct marks if units are mismatched.
> 2. **Forgetting the units in numerical answers** — KTU strictly penalizes missing units (e.g., writing "1.5" instead of "1.5 km/km²").
> 3. **Writing only definitions in Part B** — Part B sub-questions carry 7 marks each; definitions alone get only 2–3 marks. **Diagrams, derivations, and real-world examples are MANDATORY.**
> 4. **Confusing "Watershed Committee" (village-level) with "Watershed Association" (project-level)** — these are distinct bodies per the Common Guidelines for Watershed Development Projects (2008, revised 2021).
> 5. **Ignoring the "Integration" in IWM** — answers that focus only on structures (check dams, percolation tanks) without discussing integration across disciplines and stakeholders are considered incomplete and lose up to 30% marks.
> 6. **Miscalculating Circularity Ratio** — students often use $A/P$ instead of $4\pi A/P^{\,2}$. Re-check your formula before submitting.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Integrated Watershed Management**

- **Watershed** = drainage unit bounded by a ridge line, draining to a common outlet. **NOT** defined by administrative boundaries.
- **IWM** = Holistic + Participatory + Multi-disciplinary + Sustainable management of natural resources within a watershed.
- **Integration** has THREE meanings: (1) across **disciplines**, (2) across **scales** (micro → macro), (3) across **stakeholders**.
- The **Seven Principles** of IWM (master them): unit, participatory, holistic, sustainable, knowledge-integrated, demand-driven, adaptive.
- **Six Components**: NRM, WRM, crop, livestock, livelihood, institutional.
- **Key Morphometric Formulas** (must memorize all 10 in the formula sheet):
  - $D = \sum L_i / A$ — drainage density
  - $F_s = N / A$ — stream frequency
  - $R_b = N_u / N_{u+1}$ — bifurcation ratio
  - $F_f = A / L_b^{\,2}$ — form factor
  - $R_c = 4\pi A / P^{\,2}$ — circularity ratio
  - $R_e = 2\sqrt{A/\pi} / L_b$ — elongation ratio
- **High Drainage Density** (>2 km/km²) = flashy watershed = **flood risk** + **less recharge**.
- **Low Drainage Density** (<1 km/km²) = permeable watershed = **good recharge potential**.
- **Watershed Committee (WC)** = village-level apex body for planning & execution.
- **Watershed Association (WA)** = federation of WCs, manages the **Watershed Development Fund (WDF)** post-project.
- **Watershed Development Fund (WDF)** = corpus of ~**₹15,000 per hectare** for sustainability (per GoI Common Guidelines).
- **Convergence** is the operational heart of IWM: MGNREGA + PMKSY + JJM + State schemes = single watershed plan.
- **Horton's Law** ($N_u = R_b^{\,(\Omega - u)}$) and **Strahler's Stream Ordering** are foundational to morphometric analysis.
- **KTU 2024 emphasis**: Expect at least **one numerical question** on morphometric parameters every exam cycle.
- **Kerala-specific note**: KTU watersheds in midland/lowland zones have **low relief ratios (<0.05)** — interventions focus on **moisture conservation, paddy-field water management, and *kayal*-based storage**, not on large dams.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Topic: Integrated Watershed Management - Concept**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Syllabus (PGAET741)*

topics.title: Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET741 - WATERSHED DEVELOPMENT AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Watershed Development and Management (WDM)** is an integrated, multidisciplinary approach that combines engineering, hydrological, agricultural, ecological, and socio-economic interventions to optimize the use of natural resources within a *hydrological unit* (delineated drainage basin). The **Components** of WDM refer to the structured set of physical, biological, institutional, and managerial elements that, when deployed together, conserve soil, augment water availability, enhance biomass production, and sustain livelihoods within the watershed boundary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Components of watershed development cover the entire spectrum from **natural resource-based components** (land, water, soil, vegetation) to **engineering structures** (check dams, percolation tanks, contour bunds), **agronomic practices** (afforestation, agroforestry), and **socio-economic components** (community participation, capacity building, monitoring & evaluation).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Human Body"**
> Imagine a watershed as a **living human body**:
> - The **rivers & streams** are the *circulatory system* (carry the lifeblood - water).
> - The **soil profile** is the *skin & tissue* (protects, supports, stores).
> - **Forests & vegetation** act like the *lungs* (breathe in carbon, exhale oxygen, transpire moisture).
> - **Engineering structures** (check dams, gully plugs) function like *kidneys* (filter, store, recharge).
> - The **community** is the *brain & heart* (governs, monitors, sustains).
>
> **Just as a doctor treats each organ system together for the body's health, watershed management treats all components holistically for ecosystem health.**

## 1.3 Classification of Watershed Components

The components are broadly classified into **five major categories** per KTU 2024 scheme expectations:

| **S.No.** | **Component Category** | **Focus Area** |
|:---------:|:----------------------|:---------------|
| 1 | **Natural / Resource-Based Components** | Land, water, soil, vegetation, topography |
| 2 | **Engineering / Structural Components** | Bunds, terraces, check dams, percolation tanks |
| 3 | **Agronomic / Vegetative Components** | Afforestation, agroforestry, cover crops, pasture |
| 4 | **Livestock & Land-Use Components** | Silvipasture, rotational grazing, fodder management |
| 5 | **Socio-Economic & Institutional Components** | Community participation, SHGs, monitoring, PIA |

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values Used in Watershed Design:**
> - **Peak runoff estimation standard**: Rational Method (for areas **< 50 km²**) and **SCS Curve Number** method (universal applicability).
> - **Standard Manning's roughness coefficient (n)** for natural channels: **0.030 to 0.075**.
> - **Design return period** for minor structures: **25 years**; for major check dams: **50 years**.
> - **Standard SCS Curve Number (CN)** for bare soil (Hydrologic Soil Group B): **86**; for good pasture: **61**; for forest with heavy litter: **55**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Component Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Natural / Resource-Based Components

- **Land Resource**: Topography, slope, land capability classification. Slopes **> 15%** typically need conservation treatment.
- **Water Resource**: Surface water bodies, groundwater aquifers, springs. Assessment via **rainfall-runoff analysis**.
- **Soil Resource**: Soil depth, texture, infiltration capacity, erodibility (K-factor of USLE).
- **Vegetation Cover**: Forest density, canopy cover; critical for intercepting rainfall and reducing splash erosion.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" and "How":**
> Each natural component is *mapped and quantified* during the **Participatory Rural Appraisal (PRA)** and **resource inventory** phase — this is the diagnostic step before any engineering intervention.

### 2.1.2 Engineering / Structural Components

These are **physical interventions** to control runoff, arrest soil erosion, and recharge groundwater:

- **Contour Bunds**: Earthen embankments along contour lines; suitable for **low-rainfall (≤ 750 mm)** regions on **shallow soils**.
- **Graded Bunds**: Built with a small gradient (0.1–0.5%) to safely dispose of excess runoff; suitable for **high-rainfall regions**.
- **Terracing (Bench Terraces)**: Step-like fields on hill slopes; recommended for slopes between **16% to 33%**.
- **Check Dams / Loose Boulder Check Dams**: Built across streams to impound water, reduce velocity, and recharge groundwater.
- **Percolation Tanks**: Small reservoirs built on first-order streams to enhance percolation into the aquifer.
- **Farm Ponds**: Excavated structures at individual farm level for rainwater harvesting.
- **Gully Plugs / Gabion Structures**: Used to stabilize active gullies.

### 2.1.3 Agronomic / Vegetative Components

- **Afforestation**: Planting native tree species on degraded lands.
- **Agroforestry**: Integrating trees with crops (e.g., alley cropping).
- **Silvipasture**: Combining trees + pasture + livestock.
- **Cover Crops & Mulching**: Reduce evaporation, prevent splash erosion.
- **Contour Vegetative Hedges**: Vetiver grass, lemon grass planted along contours.

### 2.1.4 Livestock & Land-Use Components

- **Controlled / Rotational Grazing** to prevent pasture degradation.
- **Fodder Banks** to ensure year-round livestock feed.
- **Animal Husbandry Improvements** (cross-breeding, vaccination camps).

### 2.1.5 Socio-Economic & Institutional Components

- **Community Participation (PIA — Project Implementing Agency)**.
- **Self-Help Groups (SHGs)** & User Groups for water management.
- **Capacity Building & Training** programs.
- **Monitoring & Evaluation (M&E)** using **GIS & Remote Sensing**.
- **Micro-Finance & Livelihood Diversification**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Formulas for Watershed Component Design — Must memorize for ESE.**

| **S.No.** | **Formula** | **Application** | **Units** |
|:---------:|:-----------|:----------------|:----------|
| 1 | $Q_p = C \cdot I \cdot A$ | Rational Method — Peak runoff for small watersheds | $Q_p$ in m³/s, $I$ in m/s, $A$ in m² |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{25400}{CN} - 254$ | SCS S-parameter (max retention) | $S$ in mm |
| 3 | $Q = \dfrac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S}$ | SCS Runoff Depth (when $P > 0.2S$, else 0) | $Q, P, S$ in mm |
| 4 | $A = R \cdot K \cdot LS \cdot C \cdot P$ | Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE) | $A$ in tonnes/ha/yr |
| 5 | $V = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$ | Manning's Equation (channel velocity) | $V$ in m/s |
| 6 | $Q = V \cdot A_c$ | Discharge = Velocity × Cross-section | $Q$ in m³/s |
| 7 | $T_c = 0.01947 \cdot L^{0.77} \cdot S^{-0.385}$ | Kirpich's Time of Concentration (max 50 km²) | $T_c$ in min |
| 8 | $E = K \cdot P_c^{0.5}$ | Soil Erodibility (K-factor estimate using %clay + %silt) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> These formulas are used by watershed engineers to *size structural components* (e.g., spillway capacity of check dams), *estimate soil loss* (to prioritize treatment), and *design drainage channels*. Real-world utility includes **PMKSY watershed projects**, **MGNREGA convergence works**, and **NABARD-funded WDC-PMKSY 2.0** projects across India.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: SCS Curve Number Runoff Equation

The **SCS Curve Number method** is foundational for watershed planning. Let us derive it step-by-step.

### Step 1: Define the Water Balance Concept

The total rainfall $P$ on a watershed is partitioned as:

$$
P = I_a + F + Q
$$

Where:
- $I_a$ = initial abstraction (interception, depression storage, evaporation before runoff begins)
- $F$ = cumulative infiltration after runoff begins
- $Q$ = direct surface runoff (depth)

### Step 2: Apply the Empirical Proportionality

The empirical hypothesis states that the *ratio of actual runoff to potential runoff equals the ratio of actual infiltration to potential infiltration*:

$$
\dfrac{Q}{P - I_a} = \dfrac{F}{S}
$$

Where $S$ = maximum potential retention (after runoff begins).

### Step 3: Solve for Q

Substituting $F = P - I_a - Q$ into the proportionality:

$$
\dfrac{Q}{P - I_a} = \dfrac{P - I_a - Q}{S}
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
Q \cdot S = (P - I_a)^2 - Q \cdot (P - I_a)
$$

Bringing $Q$ terms together:

$$
Q \cdot S + Q \cdot (P - I_a) = (P - I_a)^2
$$

Factoring $Q$:

$$
Q \cdot [S + (P - I_a)] = (P - I_a)^2
$$

Isolating $Q$:

$$
Q = \dfrac{(P - I_a)^2}{S + (P - I_a)}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Empirical Standard for $I_a$

Standard empirical finding: $I_a = 0.2 S$

Substituting:

$$
Q = \dfrac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P - 0.2S + S} = \dfrac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S}
$$

This is the **standard SCS runoff depth equation**.

### Step 5: Express $S$ in terms of Curve Number $CN$

Empirically, $S$ and $CN$ are related as:

$$
S = \dfrac{25400}{CN} - 254
$$

This comes from the boundary condition: when $CN = 100$, $S = 0$ (impervious — no retention); when $CN = 0$, $S \to \infty$ (infinite retention). The constant **254** converts inches to mm.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Runoff Estimation Using SCS-CN

**Problem:** A watershed has $CN = 75$, receives rainfall $P = 100$ mm in a day. Estimate runoff depth.

### Solution:

**Step 1**: Calculate $S$:

$$
S = \dfrac{25400}{75} - 254 = 338.67 - 254 = 84.67 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2**: Calculate initial abstraction:

$$
I_a = 0.2 \cdot S = 0.2 \cdot 84.67 = 16.93 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3**: Verify $P > I_a$ (otherwise runoff = 0):

$$
P = 100 \text{ mm} > 16.93 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4**: Apply runoff equation:

$$
Q = \dfrac{(100 - 16.93)^2}{100 + 0.8 \cdot 84.67}
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{(83.07)^2}{100 + 67.74} = \dfrac{6900.62}{167.74} = 41.13 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5**: Interpret — about **41%** of rainfall becomes direct surface runoff, the remaining 59% infiltrates or is abstracted. This runoff depth is used to *design the storage capacity of farm ponds, check dams, and percolation tanks*.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Engineering Component

| **Slope (%)** | **Rainfall (mm)** | **Soil Type** | **Recommended Structure** |
|:-------------:|:-----------------:|:--------------|:--------------------------|
| 0 – 1 | Any | Deep | Farm pond, percolation tank |
| 1 – 3 | < 750 | Shallow | Contour bund |
| 1 – 3 | > 750 | Shallow | Graded bund |
| 3 – 10 | Any | Medium | Terracing + vegetative barrier |
| 10 – 33 | Any | Any | Bench terrace + afforestation |
| > 33 | Any | Any | Strict afforestation / no cultivation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** This matrix is a *favourite 14-mark question* in KTU — "Select suitable watershed components for given site conditions and justify."

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
