### Step 4: Average Weight Gain per Fish

**Logic:** Assumed initial fingerling weight = 20 g (0.02 kg). Average weight at harvest is the mean across all fish.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Avg harvest weight} &= \frac{5{,}825}{5{,}500} = 1.059 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Weight gain per fish} &= 1.059 - 0.020 = 1.039 \text{ kg} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Survival Rate

$$
\text{Survival Rate} = \frac{5{,}500}{8{,}000} \times 100 = 68.75\%
$$

### Step 6: Food Conversion Ratio (FCR)

$$
\text{FCR} = \frac{4{,}800 \text{ kg feed}}{5{,}825 - (8{,}000 \times 0.020) \text{ kg gain}} = \frac{4{,}800}{5{,}665} = 0.847
$$

**Interpretation:** An FCR < 1.0 indicates excellent feed efficiency — the pond's natural productivity (plankton) supplemented the feed. Typical FCR in intensive culture = **1.2 to 1.8**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Poultry Hen-Day Egg Production

> **Problem:** A poultry farm has **500 layer hens**. Over a 30-day month, total eggs collected = **9,500**. On day 15, 10 hens died. Calculate the **Hen-Day %** and **Hen-Housed %** for the month.

### Step 1: Calculate Hen-Day Production

**Logic:** Hen-Day % uses the *actual number of live hens on each day*. Since mortality occurred on day 15, we split the month.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hen-days (1–14)} &= 500 \times 14 = 7{,}000 \\
\text{Hen-days (15–30)} &= 490 \times 16 = 7{,}840 \\
\text{Total hen-days} &= 14{,}840 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Assume eggs distributed proportionally: (7,000/14,840) × 9,500 in first 14 days.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Eggs (days 1–14)} &= \frac{7{,}000}{14{,}840} \times 9{,}500 = 4{,}481 \\
\text{Eggs (days 15–30)} &= 9{,}500 - 4{,}481 = 5{,}019 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Hen-Day %

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hen-Day \% (1–14)} &= \frac{4{,}481}{7{,}000} \times 100 = 64.01\% \\
\text{Hen-Day \% (15–30)} &= \frac{5{,}019}{7{,}840} \times 100 = 64.02\% \\
\text{Overall Hen-Day \%} &= \frac{9{,}500}{14{,}840} \times 100 = 64.02\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Hen-Housed %

**Logic:** Hen-Housed % uses the *original number housed* at the start, regardless of mortality.

$$
\text{Hen-Housed \%} = \frac{9{,}500}{500 \times 30} \times 100 = \frac{9{,}500}{15{,}000} \times 100 = 63.33\%
$$

### Step 4: Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hen-Day %** > **Hen-Housed %** because mortality reduces the denominator in Hen-Day calculation after day 15. A **Hen-Day % > 70%** is considered good commercial layer performance.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Livestock Enterprises for Watershed Integration

| Parameter | Dairy | Poultry (Broiler) | Goatery | Apiculture | Sericulture |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Investment** | High | Medium | Low | Very Low | Low–Medium |
| **Space Required** | Large | Small | Medium | Minimal | Medium |
| **Returns/Year** | ₹40,000–80,000/cow | ₹30–50/bird in 6 wks | ₹3,000–5,000/goat | ₹6–8/kg honey | ₹2–4 lakh/ha mulberry |
| **Payback Period** | 3–4 years | 6–8 weeks | 1–2 years | 6 months | 6–8 months |
| **Watershed Compatibility** | Excellent (uses fodder from bunds) | Excellent (uses pond shed) | Good (grazes on marginal land) | Excellent (pollinates watershed crops) | Good (mulberry on bunds) |
| **Manure Output** | High (used in biogas) | High (pond fertilizer) | Medium | None | Mulberry leaf waste |
| **Risk Level** | Medium (disease) | Medium (price volatility) | Low (hardy) | Very Low | Medium (disease) |
| **Skill Required** | High | Medium | Low | Low | Medium |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Apiculture Economics

> **Problem:** A beekeeper maintains **20 colonies of *Apis mellifera***. Each colony yields **30 kg honey/year** at ₹250/kg, **0.5 kg wax** at ₹400/kg, and provides pollination services valued at ₹500/colony. Annual cost per colony = ₹1,500. Calculate **Net Annual Income** and **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)**.

### Step 1: Gross Returns

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Honey revenue} &= 20 \times 30 \times 250 = ₹1{,}50{,}000 \\
\text{Wax revenue} &= 20 \times 0.5 \times 400 = ₹4{,}000 \\
\text{Pollination service} &= 20 \times 500 = ₹10{,}000 \\
\text{Total Gross Return} &= ₹1{,}64{,}000 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Total Cost

$$
\text{Total Cost} = 20 \times 1{,}500 = ₹30{,}000
$$

### Step 3: Net Income

$$
\text{Net Income} = 1{,}64{,}000 - 30{,}000 = ₹1{,}34{,}000
$$

### Step 4: Benefit-Cost Ratio

$$
\text{BCR} = \frac{1{,}64{,}000}{30{,}000} = 5.47
$$

**Interpretation:** A BCR > 2.0 indicates a **highly profitable enterprise** with low capital risk. Apiculture is the most cost-effective watershed livelihood for small/marginal farmers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Integrated Watershed Livelihood Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rainfall Harvested in Watershed] --> B[Farm Pond / Check Dam]
    B --> C[Fishery - Composite Fish Culture]
    B --> D[Irrigation to Crops]
    D --> E[Crop Residue]
    E --> F[Dairy / Cattle]
    E --> G[Goatery]
    F --> H[Biogas Plant]
    H --> I[Slurry to Pond and Fields]
    I --> C
    I --> D
    D --> J[Apiculture - Pollination]
    J --> K[Increased Crop Yield]
    K --> D
    D --> L[Sericulture - Mulberry on Bunds]
    L --> M[Silk Production]
    B --> N[Poultry Shed over Pond]
    N --> O[Manure Drops to Pond]
    O --> C
    C --> P[Pond Silt to Fields]
    P --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each arrow represents a **nutrient or value flow**. The central hub is the **farm pond**, which is both a water source (for irrigation & fishery) and a nutrient sink (for animal manure). This closed loop embodies the **circular bioeconomy** principle in watershed management.

---

## 4.2 Composite Fish Culture — Niche Partitioning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Pond[Ecosystem of Composite Fish Pond]
        direction TB
        subgraph Layer1[Surface Zone - Zooplankton]
            nodeA1[Catla]
            nodeA2[Silver Carp]
        end
        subgraph Layer2[Middle Zone - Phytoplankton and Detritus]
            nodeB1[Rohu]
        end
        subgraph Layer3[Bottom Zone - Detritus and Benthos]
            nodeC1[Mrigal]
            nodeC2[Common Carp]
        end
        subgraph Layer4[Macrophyte Control]
            nodeD1[Grass Carp]
        end
    end
    Layer1 --> Layer2
    Layer2 --> Layer3
    Layer4 --> Pond
    Layer3 --> Pond
    Layer1 --> Pond
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Each species occupies a **distinct vertical niche**. Catla & Silver Carp feed at the surface (on zooplankton & phytoplankton respectively), preventing algal blooms. Grass Carp controls aquatic weeds. Mrigal & Common Carp stir up bottom sediments, releasing locked nutrients. This is **biological synergy**, not competition.

---

## 4.3 Hypophysation Procedure — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mature Female Brooder] --> B[Pituitary Gland Extraction]
    C[Mature Male Brooder] --> B
    B --> D[Gland Homogenization in Saline]
    D --> E[Female Injection at Caudal Peduncle]
    A2[Male Brooder] --> F[Male Injection Half Dose]
    E --> G[Latency Period 6-12 Hours]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Egg Stripping into Dry Tray]
    I[Milt Collection] --> J[Milt Mixed with Eggs]
    H --> J
    J --> K[Fertilized Eggs Washed]
    K --> L[Hatching Tank]
    L --> M[Fry after 18-24 Hours]
    M --> N[Nursery Pond - 15 Days]
    N --> O[Fingerlings Ready for Stocking]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Livestock Integration in Watershed

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Watershed[Watershed Land Unit]
        direction TB
        subgraph Cropland[Crop Production Block]
            nodeCrop1[Cereals - Paddy, Maize]
            nodeCrop2[Fodder Crops - Napier, Subabul]
            nodeCrop3[Cash Crops]
        end
        subgraph Livestock[Animal Husbandry Block]
            nodeLS1[Dairy Unit]
            nodeLS2[Poultry Unit]
            nodeLS3[Goat Unit]
            nodeLS4[Apiculture Unit]
            nodeLS5[Sericulture Unit]
        end
        subgraph Aquaculture[Aquaculture Block]
            nodeAQ1[Farm Pond - Composite Fish]
            nodeAQ2[Paddy-Cum-Fish]
        end
        subgraph Storage[Post Harvest and Marketing]
            nodeST1[Milk Collection Center]
            nodeST2[Egg Cold Storage]
            nodeST3[Honey Processing Unit]
            nodeST4[Silk Reeling Unit]
            nodeST5[Fish Market]
        end
    end
    nodeCrop1 --> nodeLS1
    nodeCrop2 --> nodeLS1
    nodeCrop2 --> nodeLS3
    nodeLS1 --> nodeAQ1
    nodeLS2 --> nodeAQ1
    nodeCrop1 --> nodeAQ2
    nodeAQ1 --> nodeCrop1
    nodeLS4 --> nodeCrop1
    nodeLS4 --> nodeCrop3
    nodeLS5 --> nodeCrop2
    nodeLS1 --> nodeST1
    nodeLS2 --> nodeST2
    nodeLS4 --> nodeST3
    nodeLS5 --> nodeST4
    nodeAQ1 --> nodeST5
```

---

## 4.5 Silkworm Life Cycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Egg - 10 to 14 Days] --> B[First Instar Larva]
    B --> C[Second Instar Larva]
    C --> D[Third Instar Larva]
    D --> E[Fourth Instar Larva]
    E --> F[Fifth Instar Larva - Mature]
    F --> G[Spinning Stage - 3 Days]
    G --> H[Cocoon Formation]
    H --> I[Pupa Inside Cocoon]
    I --> J[Moth Emerges]
    J --> K[Mating]
    K --> L[Egg Laying]
    L --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Total cycle duration:** 45 to 55 days. The **fifth instar larva** consumes **85% of total leaf intake** across its lifecycle — a critical fact for mulberry plantation planning.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Composite Fish Culture. List any four Indian major carps used. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Composite Fish Culture (Polyculture) is the practice of culturing **multiple compatible fish species** of different feeding habits in the same pond to utilize all available ecological niches and maximize production per unit area.
>
> **Four Indian Major Carps (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Catla** (*Catla catla*) — surface feeder, feeds on zooplankton
> 2. **Rohu** (*Labeo rohita*) — column feeder, feeds on phytoplankton and decaying vegetation
> 3. **Mrigal** (*Cirrhinus mrigala*) — bottom feeder, feeds on detritus
> 4. *(Optional 4th)* **Kalbasu** (*Labeo calbasu*) — bottom feeder, omnivorous

### Question 2: What is Hypophysation? Mention the hormone used. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Hypophysation (2 Marks):** Hypophysation is the technique of **induced breeding of fish** by injecting **pituitary gland extract** containing **Gonadotropin hormones (FSH & LH)** from a donor fish into a mature brooder. It was developed by **Dr. Hiralal Chaudhuri (1957)** to induce spawning in Indian major carps which do not breed naturally in confined waters.
>
> **Hormone Used (1 Mark):** **Gonadotropin** (from carp pituitary) or synthetic analogues like **Ovaprim / Ovatide** containing sGnRH + domperidone.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

> **Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **Composite Fish Culture system** suitable for watershed farm ponds in Kerala. Discuss species selection, stocking density, and management practices. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Describe the procedure of **Induced Breeding (Hypophysation)** in Indian major carps. Add a note on its significance in aquaculture development. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q. (a): Composite Fish Culture (7 Marks)

**1. Definition and Principle (1.5 Marks):**
Composite fish culture is the simultaneous rearing of **multiple fish species** having **non-overlapping feeding niches** (surface, column, bottom) in the same pond. The principle is **ecological complementarity** — species do not compete but together utilize the pond's full productivity.

**2. Species Selection for Kerala Watershed Ponds (2.5 Marks):**

| Species | Niche | Stocking % |
|---|---|---|
| Catla | Surface | 15% |
| Rohu | Column | 15% |
| Mrigal | Bottom | 15% |
| Silver Carp | Surface | 20% |
| Grass Carp | Herbivore | 20% |
| Common Carp | Bottom | 15% |

**Reasoning:** Kerala's warm tropical climate (water temperature 25–32°C) supports all six species. Grass carp controls *Hydrilla* and *Eichhornia* weeds. Silver carp prevents algal blooms.

**3. Stocking Density and Management (3 Marks):**
- **Stocking rate:** **5,000–10,000 fingerlings/ha** (size: 50–100 mm)
- **Pond preparation:** Liming @ 600 kg/ha, manuring with raw cow dung @ 10,000 kg/ha
- **Supplementary feeding:** Rice bran + groundnut oil cake (1:1) @ 3–5% body weight/day
- **Water quality:** DO > 5 mg/L, pH 7.0–8.5, transparency 25–40 cm
- **Harvesting:** Partial harvesting at 6 months, complete at 10–12 months
- **Expected yield:** **3,000–5,000 kg/ha/year** in well-managed watershed ponds

---

#### Model Solution to Q. (b): Induced Breeding / Hypophysation (7 Marks)

**1. Background (1 Mark):**
Indian major carps (Catla, Rohu, Mrigal) do **not spawn naturally** in confined pond waters due to absence of natural environmental triggers (riverine flow, flooding). Hypophysation solves this by hormonal induction.

**2. Procedure (4 Marks):**
1. **Selection of brooders:** Mature females (belly soft, swollen, reddish vent); mature males (milt flows on abdominal pressure)
2. **Pituitary gland extraction:** Donor carp sacrificed; pituitary removed from below the brain
3. **Preparation of extract:** Gland homogenized in **0.3% NaCl solution** (saline) using a tissue homogenizer
4. **Dose calculation:** Female = **5–6 mg/kg body weight**; Male = **2–3 mg/kg body weight**
5. **Injection:** Intramuscular at the **caudal peduncle** (just below dorsal fin)
6. **Latency period:** **6–12 hours** depending on water temperature
7. **Stripping:** Eggs stripped into a dry tray by gentle abdominal pressure
8. **Fertilization:** Milt from male added; mixed with feathers; washed with water
9. **Incubation:** Eggs transferred to hatching tank; hatch in **18–24 hours**

**3. Significance (2 Marks):**
- Ensures **year-round seed availability** (no dependence on riverine collection)
- Enables **selective breeding** and hybridization
- Triggered the **Blue Revolution** in India
- Supports commercial aquaculture and watershed-based livelihoods

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper 3]`**

> **Q. (a)** Discuss the major components of **Animal Husbandry** suitable for integration with watershed development programs. Compare the economics of dairy, poultry, and apiculture. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Explain **Apiculture** as a watershed-based livelihood option. Discuss the species, equipment, and management practices. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q. (a): Animal Husbandry Components (7 Marks)

**1. Dairy Farming (2 Marks):**
- Suitable breeds: **Murrah buffalo** (best for Kerala), **HF crossbred cows** (Holstein Friesian × Sahiwal)
- Average yield: Crossbred = **3,000–4,500 kg/lactation**
- Feed: Green fodder (Napier grass, Subabul) + concentrate
- Watershed role: Cattle dung → biogas → slurry → fish pond/field fertilizer

**2. Poultry Farming (2 Marks):**
- **Broilers:** Cobb 500, Vencobb — reach 2 kg in 6 weeks
- **Layers:** White Leghorn — 280 eggs/year
- Small land requirement; can be housed **over fish ponds** (poultry droppings fertilize pond directly)
- Watershed role: Manure increases pond plankton → enhances fish growth

**3. Goatery (1.5 Marks):**
- Hardy, low-investment enterprise suitable for marginal lands
- Breeds: Malabari (native to Kerala), Boer (exotic)
- Watershed role: Grazes on bund vegetation, controls bush growth

**4. Economic Comparison (1.5 Marks):**

| Enterprise | Investment/Unit | Annual Net Return | Payback |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dairy (1 cow) | ₹60,000 | ₹40,000 | 3–4 years |
| Poultry (500 broilers/batch) | ₹1,50,000 | ₹30,000/batch | 6–8 weeks |
| Apiculture (20 hives) | ₹60,000 | ₹1,34,000 | 6 months |

---

#### Model Solution to Q. (b): Apiculture in Watershed (7 Marks)

**1. Introduction (1 Mark):**
Apiculture is the scientific rearing of honey bees for honey, beeswax, and pollination services. It is an ideal watershed-based enterprise because it requires **minimal land and water**.

**2. Species of Honey Bees (2 Marks):**
- *Apis dorsata* (Rock bee) — wild, largest, builds single comb on trees/cliffs
- *Apis cerana indica* (Indian hive bee) — small, domesticatable, yield 6–8 kg/hive/year
- *Apis mellifera* (European/Italian bee) — introduced, docile, yield 25–40 kg/hive/year
- *Apis florea* (Little bee) — small, builds single comb on bushes

**3. Equipment (2 Marks):**
- **Newton's Bee Box** (ISI standard): Wooden box with 10 movable frames
- **Smoker:** Calms bees during inspection
- **Bee veil & gloves:** Personal protection
- **Honey extractor:** Centrifugal machine to extract honey without damaging comb
- **Uncapping knife:** To remove wax caps from cells

**4. Management Practices (2 Marks):**
- **Site selection:** Near nectar source (coconut, mango, rubber plantations); shaded; away from chemical-sprayed fields
- **Colony inspection:** Every 7–10 days; check for queen, brood, food stores
- **Swarming control:** Divide colonies; provide extra space
- **Honey extraction:** During peak flow season (Oct–Feb in Kerala)
- **Disease management:** Watch for *Thai sacbrood virus* and *Nosema*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Catla and Rohu niches (–1 to –2 marks):** Catla is a **surface zooplankton feeder**; Rohu is a **column feeder** that consumes **phytoplankton AND decaying vegetation** — not just plankton.
>
> 2. **Wrong Hypophysation dose:** Always remember **Male = Half of Female dose**. Writing equal doses = **–1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Missing the "why" in integrated systems:** Don't just list enterprises; explain the **nutrient/energy flow** between them. Examiners award marks for the linkage, not the list.
>
> 4. **Hen-Day vs Hen-Housed confusion:** Hen-Day uses *live hens on that day*; Hen-Housed uses *original housed number*. Reverse them = **–1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the "ecosystem service" of bees:** Apiculture is NOT just about honey. Its **pollination service increases crop yield by 10–20%** — this is a frequent 3-mark sub-question.
>
> 6. **Mixing silkworm instars:** There are **5 larval instars** before cocoon spinning. Students often write 4.
>
> 7. **No mention of watershed linkage:** Every answer in this module MUST tie back to **watershed structures** (farm ponds, check dams, percolation tanks, contour bunds). Missing this = **–2 marks** in 7-mark answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Fishery & Animal Husbandry**

### Core Definitions
- [ ] Composite Fish Culture = multi-species, multi-niche polyculture
- [ ] Hypophysation = pituitary gland extract-induced spawning
- [ ] FCR = feed given ÷ weight gain (lower is better)
- [ ] Hen-Day % = eggs today ÷ live hens today × 100
- [ ] Apiculture = scientific beekeeping using movable-frame hives
- [ ] Sericulture = silk production from *Bombyx mori* on mulberry
- [ ] Induced breeding uses **Gonadotropin** hormones

### Numerical Must-Knows
- [ ] Composite culture stocking: **5,000–10,000 fingerlings/ha**
- [ ] Standard fish yield: **3,000–5,000 kg/ha/year**
- [ ] Hypophysation female dose: **5–6 mg/kg BW**
- [ ] Hypophysation male dose: **2–3 mg/kg BW**
- [ ] Latency period: **6–12 hours**
- [ ] Hatching time: **18–24 hours**
- [ ] Broiler market weight: **2.0–2.5 kg in 6 weeks**
- [ ] Layer production: **250–300 eggs/year**
- [ ] *Apis mellifera* honey yield: **25–40 kg/hive/year**
- [ ] Silkworm total cycle: **45–55 days**
- [ ] Murrah buffalo lactation: **1,800–2,500 kg/lactation**

### Six Species of Composite Culture (Mnemonic: "CRMSGC")
- [ ] **C**atla — Surface
- [ ] **R**ohu — Column
- [ ] **M**rigal — Bottom
- [ ] **S**ilver Carp — Surface
- [ ] **G**rass Carp — Weed control
- [ ] **C**ommon Carp — Bottom

### Key Watershed Integration Points
- [ ] Farm pond → fish + irrigation
- [ ] Cattle dung → biogas → pond fertilizer
- [ ] Poultry shed over pond → direct manuring
- [ ] Pond silt → field top-dressing
- [ ] Mulberry on contour bunds → sericulture
- [ ] Bee hives near crops → pollination (+10–20% yield)

### Exam-Ready Phrases (Use Verbatim in Answers)
- [ ] "Different feeding niches ensure **ecological complementarity**"
- [ ] "Hypophysation ensures **year-round seed availability** for the **Blue Revolution**"
- [ ] "Apiculture provides **triple benefit** — honey, wax, and pollination services"
- [ ] "Poultry-cum-fish integration enables **zero external input** for pond fertilization"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Cropping Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PGAET741 - WATERSHED DEVELOPMENT AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Effect of Cropping Systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Cropping System** is a defined pattern or sequence of crops grown on a given land area over a specified period, encompassing the spatial and temporal arrangement of crops and their associated management practices. In the context of Watershed Development and Management, cropping systems are evaluated as **bio-physical land-use interventions** that directly modulate the **water balance, sediment yield, nutrient cycling, and micro-climate** of a watershed. According to KTU 2024 PGAET741 syllabus (Module 3), the term broadly encompasses: - **Monoculture** — continuous cultivation of a single crop species on the same land. - **Crop Rotation** — sequential cultivation of different crops in a recurring succession on the same field. - **Intercropping** — simultaneous cultivation of two or more crops on the same field with distinct spatial arrangement. - **Mixed Cropping** — sowing of two or more crops simultaneously without row arrangement. - **Sequential Cropping** — growing two or more crops in succession on the same field per year. - **Multi-Tier / Multi-Storied Cropping** — cultivation of crops of different heights in vertical strata (e.g., coconut + pepper + pineapple). - **Relay Cropping** — planting a succeeding crop before the harvest of the standing crop. - **Cover Cropping** — growing crops primarily to protect and improve soil between main crop cycles. - **Agroforestry / Silvi-Pasture Systems** — integration of trees, crops, and/or livestock. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Cropping systems are studied not in isolation, but as **watershed response modifiers**. Every cropping choice shifts the partitioning of rainfall into **surface runoff, infiltration, evapotranspiration, deep percolation, and soil moisture storage**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a watershed as a **large sponge-and-funnel system**. When rain falls on bare, monocultured land, the funnel is wide open — most water rushes out as runoff, taking precious topsoil with it. Now imagine covering that same sponge with a layered forest of different crops, grasses, and trees. The funnel narrows; water is absorbed, slowed, filtered, and stored. **That is what a well-designed cropping system does to a watershed.** Think of a **multi-tier cropping system** like a **multi-storey building during a rainstorm**: - The **top storey** (tall trees) breaks the force of raindrops — reducing splash erosion. - The **middle storey** (medium crops) intercepts throughfall and stemflow. - The **ground storey** (cover crops/grass) anchors the soil with roots and increases surface roughness — reducing overland flow velocity. The result: **less runoff, less soil loss, more groundwater recharge, and more crop yield** — a perfect example of an *ecological-economic win-win*. ### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (Watershed Context) | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Annual Soil Loss Tolerance | **T** | 4.5 – 11.2 | **t ha⁻¹ yr⁻¹** | | SCS Curve Number | **CN** | 30 (dense forest) – 91 (paved) | dimensionless | | Crop Cover Management Factor | **C** | 0.001 (forest) – 1.0 (bare soil) | dimensionless | | Runoff Coefficient | **Cᵣ** | 0.05 (forest) – 0.60 (bare) | dimensionless | | Water Use Efficiency | **WUE** | 0.5 – 1.5 | **kg m⁻³** | > [!NOTE] > The standard **soil loss tolerance (T)** value adopted by the **USDA Soil Conservation Service** is **11.2 t ha⁻¹ yr⁻¹** (≈ 5 tons/acre/year) for deep, well-drained soils. For shallow or erosion-prone soils, values as low as **2.24 t ha⁻¹ yr⁻¹** are recommended. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 How Cropping Systems Affect the Watershed — The Five Pillars **Pillar 1: Effect on Rainfall Interception & Kinetic Energy Dissipation** - Tall canopy crops (agroforestry, multi-tier systems) intercept rainfall and reduce the **kinetic energy** of falling drops. - Splash erosion is directly proportional to the square of raindrop terminal velocity. - Multi-tier systems can reduce splash erosion by **60 – 80 %** compared to bare fallow. **Pillar 2: Effect on Infiltration & Surface Runoff** - Continuous ground cover increases **soil organic matter (SOM)**, improves aggregate stability, and creates **macropores** (root channels, biopores). - Higher SOM → higher infiltration capacity → lower surface runoff. - Cover crops can increase infiltration by **30 – 50 %** over conventional systems. **Pillar 3: Effect on Soil Erosion (Universal Soil Loss Equation context)** - In the **USLE (Wischmeier & Smith, 1978)**: A = R × K × LS × C × P - The **C-factor** (cover-management factor) is *directly* controlled by the cropping system. - A shift from bare soil (C ≈ 1.0) to dense agroforestry (C ≈ 0.001) reduces predicted soil loss by a factor of **1000**. **Pillar 4: Effect on Soil Moisture & Evapotranspiration** - Transpiring crops convert stored soil moisture into atmospheric vapor. - However, residue mulch and cover crops **reduce direct evaporation** from the soil surface. - The net effect is often a **more stable soil moisture regime** in diversified systems. **Pillar 5: Effect on Nutrient Cycling & Water Quality** - Legume-based rotations (e.g., rice–pulses, maize–cowpea) fix atmospheric N, reducing fertilizer input. - Diverse root systems mine nutrients from different soil depths. - Reduced runoff → **less transport of N, P, and pesticides** to streams (lower eutrophication risk). ### 2.2 Cropping-System Effect on Hydrologic Soil Group (HSG) and Curve Number The **SCS Curve Number (CN)** is the most-used parameter in watershed runoff estimation. Cropping systems modify the CN via the **Hydrologic Condition (HC)** term (Poor, Fair, Good): | Cropping System | Hydrologic Condition | Typical CN (HSG-B Soil) | |---|---|---| | Fallow (Bare) | — | 86 | | Row Crops (Maize, Cotton) | Poor / Good | 78 / 67 | | Small Grain (Wheat) | Poor / Good | 78 / 65 | | Legumes (Pulses) | Good | 64 | | Pasture / Grassland | Poor / Good | 74 / 61 | | Meadow (Continuous Grass) | Good | 58 | | Woods / Agroforestry | Poor / Good | 60 / 35 | | Forest (Dense, Mulched) | Good | 30 | > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A switch from bare fallow (CN = 86) to dense agroforestry (CN = 30) on the same soil type reduces the runoff volume for a 50 mm storm by approximately **78 %** (computed later in Section 3). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $A = R \cdot K \cdot LS \cdot C \cdot P$ | Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE) | A = soil loss (t ha⁻¹ yr⁻¹); R = rainfall erosivity (MJ mm ha⁻¹ h⁻¹ yr⁻¹); K = soil erodibility (t h MJ⁻¹ mm⁻¹); LS = slope-length-gradient factor; C = cover-management factor; P = conservation practice factor | | 2 | $S = \dfrac{25400}{CN} - 254$ | SCS Maximum Retention | S = max retention (mm); CN = curve number (dimensionless) | | 3 | $Q = \dfrac{(P - 0.2S)^{2}}{P + 0.8S}$ for $P > 0.2S$ | SCS-CN Runoff Depth | Q = direct runoff (mm); P = rainfall (mm) | | 4 | $\eta_{runoff} = \dfrac{Q}{P} \times 100$ | Runoff Efficiency | $\eta_{runoff}$ in % | | 5 | $WUE = \dfrac{Y}{ET}$ | Water Use Efficiency | Y = crop yield (kg); ET = evapotranspiration (m³) | | 6 | $SLR = \dfrac{C_{crop}}{C_{fallow}}$ | Soil Loss Ratio (USLE C-factor computation) | SLR = soil loss ratio; C_crop = C-factor of cropped system; C_fallow = C-factor of continuous fallow (= 1.0) | | 7 | $R_{reduction} = \left(1 - \dfrac{Q_{new}}{Q_{old}}\right) \times 100$ | Percent Reduction in Runoff (comparative cropping systems) | Q_old = runoff under base system; Q_new = runoff under new system (mm) | | 8 | $CN_{composite} = \dfrac{\sum (CN_{i} \cdot A_{i})}{\sum A_{i}}$ | Area-weighted Composite Curve Number | CN_i = curve number of sub-area i; A_i = area of sub-area i (ha) | | 9 | $E_{loss} \propto v^{2} \cdot h$ | Empirical Runoff Erosion Power (relating flow to soil detachment) | v = overland flow velocity (m s⁻¹); h = flow depth (m) | | 10 | $E_{canopy} = k \cdot LAI$ | Canopy Interception Index | E_canopy = fraction of rainfall intercepted; LAI = Leaf Area Index; k = crop coefficient (0.10 – 0.25) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Note:** Questions from this topic almost always test the **C-factor manipulation** under USLE, the **SCS-CN method** for runoff comparison between cropping systems, and the **qualitative description of soil/water/biodiversity effects**. Memorize formulas **1, 2, 3, 6, and 8** as your top priority. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Watershed Treatment Plans:** Engineers use the **C-factor** to prioritize areas for soil and water conservation interventions. - **Command Area Development:** Diversified cropping reduces the irrigation duty in canal commands, saving water. - **Climate Resilience:** Mixed/intercropping systems buffer farmers against erratic monsoons — directly relevant to **Kerala's recurrent floods and droughts**. - **Payment for Ecosystem Services (PES):** A lower C-factor and CN translates into measurable **carbon sequestration and downstream water-quality credits**. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Effects of Cropping Systems | Parameter | Monoculture (Bare/Row) | Crop Rotation (with legumes) | Intercropping | Multi-tier Agroforestry | |---|---|---|---|---| | USLE **C** factor | 0.40 – 1.00 | 0.10 – 0.30 | 0.05 – 0.20 | 0.001 – 0.10 | | SCS **CN** (HSG-B) | 78 – 86 | 64 – 78 | 60 – 72 | 30 – 55 | | Runoff (% of rainfall) | 30 – 60 % | 15 – 30 % | 10 – 25 % | 5 – 15 % | | Soil Loss (t ha⁻¹ yr⁻¹) | 10 – 50 | 3 – 12 | 1 – 6 | 0.1 – 2 | | Soil Organic Matter trend | Declining | Stabilizing | Increasing | Strongly increasing | | Water Use Efficiency | Low | Moderate | Moderate–High | High | | Biodiversity | Very Low | Moderate | High | Very High | --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Effect of Cropping System on USLE Soil Loss **Problem Statement:** A 5 ha watershed on a 6 % slope of length 100 m has: - Rainfall erosivity **R = 850 MJ mm ha⁻¹ h⁻¹ yr⁻¹** - Soil erodibility **K = 0.30 t h MJ⁻¹ mm⁻¹** - LS factor = 1.6 - Conservation practice factor **P = 1.0** Compute the **annual soil loss** under three cropping systems and the **% reduction** in each case relative to bare fallow. **Step 1 — Compute soil loss under bare fallow (C = 1.0)** $$ A_{fallow} = R \cdot K \cdot LS \cdot C \cdot P $$ $$ A_{fallow} = 850 \times 0.30 \times 1.6 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 $$ $$ A_{fallow} = 408.0 \; t \; ha^{-1} \; yr^{-1} $$ This is **massively above the tolerable limit** of 11.2 t ha⁻¹ yr⁻¹ — the field would be rapidly degraded under bare fallow. **Step 2 — Soil loss under Maize–Cowpea Rotation (C = 0.20)** $$ A_{rotation} = 850 \times 0.30 \times 1.6 \times 0.20 \times 1.0 = 81.60 \; t \; ha^{-1} \; yr^{-1} $$ **Step 3 — Soil loss under Intercropping (C = 0.10)** $$ A_{intercrop} = 850 \times 0.30 \times 1.6 \times 0.10 \times 1.0 = 40.80 \; t \; ha^{-1} \; yr^{-1} $$ **Step 4 — Soil loss under Multi-Tier Agroforestry (C = 0.005)** $$ A_{agroforestry} = 850 \times 0.30 \times 1.6 \times 0.005 \times 1.0 = 2.04 \; t \; ha^{-1} \; yr^{-1} $$ **Step 5 — Compute % reduction relative to fallow** $$ R_{reduction} = \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{cropped}}{A_{fallow}}\right) \times 100 $$ | Cropping System | A (t ha⁻¹ yr⁻¹) | % Reduction vs. Fallow | Below T = 11.2? | |---|---|---|---| | Bare Fallow | 408.0 | 0 % | ❌ No | | Maize–Cowpea Rotation | 81.6 | 80.0 % | ❌ No | | Intercropping | 40.8 | 90.0 % | ❌ No | | Multi-Tier Agroforestry | 2.04 | 99.5 % | ✅ Yes | > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** Only the multi-tier agroforestry system brings soil loss **below the tolerance (T) value** in this erosive environment. Diversified cropping is therefore not just an agronomic choice but a **mandatory conservation engineering intervention** in vulnerable watersheds. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Effect of Cropping System on Runoff (SCS-CN Method) **Problem Statement:** For a 50 mm storm on HSG-B soil, compare direct runoff under: - **(a)** Bare fallow (CN = 86) - **(b)** Row crops, good condition (CN = 67) - **(c)** Dense agroforestry (CN = 35) **Step 1 — Compute S (Maximum Retention) for each** $$ S = \dfrac{25400}{CN} - 254 $$ For CN = 86: $$ S_a = \dfrac{25400}{86} - 254 = 295.35 - 254 = 41.35 \; mm $$ For CN = 67: $$ S_b = \dfrac{25400}{67} - 254 = 379.10 - 254 = 125.10 \; mm $$ For CN = 35: $$ S_c = \dfrac{25400}{35} - 254 = 725.71 - 254 = 471.71 \; mm $$ **Step 2 — Check the initial abstraction threshold $I_a = 0.2S$** $$ I_{a,a} = 0.2 \times 41.35 = 8.27 \; mm $$ $$ I_{a,b} = 0.2 \times 125.10 = 25.02 \; mm $$ $$ I_{a,c} = 0.2 \times 471.71 = 94.34 \; mm $$ For P = 50 mm, only the **agroforestry case** has P < I_a (i.e., 50 < 94.34) — meaning essentially **no runoff** under dense agroforestry. **Step 3 — Compute Q for cases (a) and (b)** $$ Q = \dfrac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S} $$ For CN = 86: $$ Q_a = \dfrac{(50 - 8.27)^2}{50 + 0.8 \times 41.35} = \dfrac{(41.73)^2}{50 + 33.08} = \dfrac{1741.39}{83.08} $$ $$ Q_a \approx 20.96 \; mm $$ For CN = 67: $$ Q_b = \dfrac{(50 - 25.02)^2}{50 + 0.8 \times 125.10} = \dfrac{(24.98)^2}{50 + 100.08} = \dfrac{623.99}{150.08} $$ $$ Q_b \approx 4.16 \; mm $$ For CN = 35: $P < I_a$ → $Q_c = 0 \; mm$ **Step 4 — Runoff efficiency and % reduction** $$ \eta_{runoff} = \dfrac{Q}{P} \times 100 $$ | Cropping System | CN | Q (mm) | $\eta_{runoff}$ (%) | % Reduction vs. Fallow | |---|---|---|---|---| | Bare Fallow | 86 | 20.96 | 41.92 | 0 % | | Row Crops (Good) | 67 | 4.16 | 8.32 | 80.2 % | | Dense Agroforestry | 35 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 100.0 % | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Switching from bare fallow to a well-managed row-crop system reduces surface runoff by **80 %** and switching further to dense agroforestry eliminates it entirely. This **translates directly into groundwater recharge, reduced flooding, and lower reservoir siltation** in downstream dams. --- ### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Composite Curve Number for a Mixed Cropping Watershed **Problem Statement:** A 20 ha sub-watershed has the following land use: - 8 ha — row crops in good condition, CN = 67 - 6 ha — pasture in good condition, CN = 61 - 4 ha — dense agroforestry, CN = 35 - 2 ha — farmstead (impervious), CN = 98 Compute the **area-weighted composite CN**. **Step 1 — Apply the area-weighting formula** $$ CN_{composite} = \dfrac{\sum (CN_i \cdot A_i)}{\sum A_i} $$ $$ CN_{composite} = \dfrac{(67 \times 8) + (61 \times 6) + (35 \times 4) + (98 \times 2)}{8 + 6 + 4 + 2} $$ $$ CN_{composite} = \dfrac{536 + 366 + 140 + 196}{20} $$ $$ CN_{composite} = \dfrac{1238}{20} = 61.9 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Use:** This composite CN can now be plugged into the SCS-CN method (Section 3.3) to estimate **storm-wise runoff** at the watershed outlet, which is the basis of design for **farm ponds, check dams, and contour bunds** in the watershed plan. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Cropping Systems ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Cropping Systems]:::root A --> B[Temporal Systems] A --> C[Spatial Systems] A --> D[Integrated Systems] B --> B1[Monoculture] B --> B2[Crop Rotation] B --> B3[Sequential Cropping] B --> B4[Relay Cropping] C --> C1[Intercropping] C --> C2[Mixed Cropping] C --> C3[Multi-Tier Cropping] C --> C4[Cover Cropping] D --> D1[Agroforestry] D --> D2[Silvi-Pasture] D --> D3[Agri-Horticulture] B1 --> E1[Lowest water use efficiency] B2 --> E2[Balances nutrients and water] C1 --> E3[Reduces runoff and erosion] C3 --> E4[Best watershed protection] D1 --> E5[Highest soil and water conservation] classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef leaf fill:#d9e8f5,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000 class B1,B2,B3,B4,C1,C2,C3,C4,D1,D2,D3,E1,E2,E3,E4,E5 leaf ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Effect Pathway of Cropping System on Watershed Hydrology ```mermaid flowchart LR R[Rainfall Input]:::in CS[Cropping System Choice]:::ctrl CV[Canopy Cover]:::leaf SO[Soil Surface Condition]:::leaf RO[Root System Architecture]:::leaf INF[Infiltration]:::proc RO2[Surface Runoff]:::proc ET[Evapotranspiration]:::proc GW[Groundwater Recharge]:::out SL[Soil Loss]:::out Y[Crop Yield]:::out WQ[Downstream Water Quality]:::out R --> CS CS --> CV CS --> SO CS --> RO CV --> INF SO --> INF RO --> INF CV --> ET INF --> GW INF --> RO2 RO2 --> SL RO2 --> WQ INF --> ET ET --> Y SL --> WQ classDef in fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff classDef ctrl fill:#c62828,color:#fff classDef leaf fill:#fff3c4,stroke:#a17500 classDef proc fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b classDef out fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c class R in class CS ctrl class CV,SO,RO leaf class INF,RO2,ET proc class GW,SL,Y,WQ out ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Multi-Tier Cropping Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[Top Tier - Tall Trees
Height 8 to 15 m]:::tier1 L2[Middle Tier - Medium Trees / Shrubs
Height 3 to 7 m]:::tier2 L3[Lower Tier - Tall Crops
Height 1 to 2 m]:::tier3 L4[Ground Tier - Cover Crops / Grasses
Height below 0.5 m]:::tier4 L5[Root Tier - Rooting Depths]:::tier5 L1 --> R1[Rainfall Interception
Wind Break]:::func L1 --> L2 L2 --> R2[Throughfall Reduction
Stemflow]:::func L2 --> L3 L3 --> R3[Soil Moisture Modulation
Microclimate]:::func L3 --> L4 L4 --> R4[Splash Erosion Control
Surface Roughness]:::func L4 --> L5 L5 --> R5[Infiltration
Nutrient Cycling]:::func classDef tier1 fill:#1b5e20,color:#fff classDef tier2 fill:#388e3c,color:#fff classDef tier3 fill:#66bb6a,color:#000 classDef tier4 fill:#a5d6a7,color:#000 classDef tier5 fill:#5d4037,color:#fff classDef func fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#8d6e63 class L1 tier1 class L2 tier2 class L3 tier3 class L4 tier4 class L5 tier5 class R1,R2,R3,R4,R5 func ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Architecture:** The diagram above shows how a multi-tier system functions as a **multi-layered watershed filter**. Each tier contributes a unique function — from raindrop energy dissipation at the top to deep nutrient mobilization at the root tier. This is the **biological analogue of a multi-stage treatment plant**, but engineered by nature over millennia. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define 'Cropping System'. How does it influence the hydrology of a watershed?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** A cropping system is the spatial and temporal pattern of crops grown on a piece of land along with the management practices adopted. It includes monoculture, rotation, intercropping, mixed cropping, sequential, relay, multi-tier, and agroforestry systems. - **Hydrologic influence (2 marks):** The cropping system controls the **partitioning of rainfall** into surface runoff, infiltration, evapotranspiration, and deep percolation. A dense, multi-tier system (e.g., agroforestry) increases canopy interception, raises infiltration by improving soil structure, lowers the SCS Curve Number (CN), reduces surface runoff and soil loss, and enhances groundwater recharge. A monoculture or bare fallow has the opposite effect — high CN, high runoff, and high erosion. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **What is the 'C-factor' in USLE? How is it affected by the choice of cropping system?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **C-factor definition (1 mark):** The **Cover and Management Factor (C)** in the Universal Soil Loss Equation is the ratio of soil loss from a given cropping system to that from a continuously clean, tilled fallow under identical slope, soil, and rainfall conditions. It is a **dimensionless factor** ranging from 0 (no erosion) to 1.0 (fallow). - **Effect of cropping system (2 marks):** Cropping systems that maintain continuous ground cover, dense canopy, and high root density — such as **dense agroforestry (C ≈ 0.001)**, multi-tier systems (C ≈ 0.01 – 0.05), or well-managed pasture (C ≈ 0.05 – 0.10) — yield very low C values. In contrast, **row crops with poor residue cover (C ≈ 0.4 – 0.6)** or bare fallow (C = 1.0) yield much higher values. Thus, switching to a more diversified cropping system can reduce soil loss by an order of magnitude. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]* **(a)** Explain in detail the **various types of cropping systems** practiced in India, with special reference to those relevant to Kerala's agro-climatic conditions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the **Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE)**, demonstrate quantitatively how a shift from a continuous **maize monoculture** to a **coconut-based multi-tier cropping system** reduces soil loss in a typical watershed. Use the following data and assume: **R = 900 MJ mm ha⁻¹ h⁻¹ yr⁻¹, K = 0.32 t h MJ⁻¹ mm⁻¹, LS = 1.4, P = 1.0.** C-factor for maize monoculture = 0.45; C-factor for coconut-based multi-tier system = 0.04. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** 1. **Temporal systems** (3 marks): - Monoculture — same crop year after year; depletes soil, increases pest cycles. - Crop rotation — legumes in rotation fix N, break pest cycles, improve soil structure. - Sequential cropping — two or more crops in a calendar year (e.g., rice–pulse in Kuttanad). - Relay cropping — sowing the next crop before harvesting the previous one. 2. **Spatial systems** (2 marks): - Intercropping (e.g., coconut + banana + pepper + pineapple in Kerala). - Mixed cropping and cover cropping. 3. **Kerala-specific relevance** (2 marks): - **Multi-tier cropping** in homesteads (Kerala's "homestead farming" model is a globally recognized example). - **Agroforestry** integrating coconut, areca nut, and black pepper — typical of the midland laterite zones. **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Soil loss under maize monoculture (C = 0.45):** $$ A_{maize} = 900 \times 0.32 \times 1.4 \times 0.45 \times 1.0 $$ $$ A_{maize} = 900 \times 0.32 \times 1.4 \times 0.45 $$ $$ A_{maize} = 181.44 \; t \; ha^{-1} \; yr^{-1} $$ **Step 2 — Soil loss under coconut-based multi-tier (C = 0.04):** $$ A_{multi} = 900 \times 0.32 \times 1.4 \times 0.04 \times 1.0 $$ $$ A_{multi} = 16.128 \; t \; ha^{-1} \; yr^{-1} $$ **Step 3 — Reduction in soil loss:** $$ R_{reduction} = \left(1 - \dfrac{16.128}{181.44}\right) \times 100 = 91.11 \; \% $$ > **Conclusion:** Shifting from maize monoculture to a coconut-based multi-tier cropping system reduces soil loss by **91.11 %** and brings the erosion rate **closer to the tolerable limit (T = 11.2 t ha⁻¹ yr⁻¹)**, demonstrating its effectiveness as a watershed conservation measure. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]* **(a)** Discuss the **effects of different cropping systems on soil and water conservation** in a watershed. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a 30 ha watershed, the following land-use pattern exists: **10 ha of row crops in good condition (CN = 67), 8 ha of pasture in good condition (CN = 61), 7 ha of dense forest (CN = 35), and 5 ha of fallow land (CN = 86).** Compute the **composite curve number** and the **direct runoff (Q)** for a storm of **P = 60 mm**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **Effects on Soil (4 marks):** - **Monoculture / bare fallow** → depletes SOM, reduces aggregation, increases erodibility. - **Crop rotation with legumes** → builds SOM, fixes atmospheric N, breaks pest cycles. - **Intercropping / multi-tier** → maintains year-round ground cover, reduces splash erosion, enhances microbial diversity. - **Agroforestry** → deep root systems bind subsoil; litter fall builds organic horizons. **Effects on Water Conservation (3 marks):** - Diversified systems **increase infiltration** (improved macropore structure). - Reduce **SCS Curve Number**, hence **lower surface runoff** and **higher groundwater recharge**. - Act as **biological sponges** that moderate peak flows and base flows. - Reduce **sediment yield** and **non-point source pollution** (N, P, pesticides). **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Composite CN:** $$ CN_{comp} = \dfrac{(67 \times 10) + (61 \times 8) + (35 \times 7) + (86 \times 5)}{10 + 8 + 7 + 5} $$ $$ CN_{comp} = \dfrac{670 + 488 + 245 + 430}{30} = \dfrac{1833}{30} = 61.1 $$ **Step 2 — Compute S:** $$ S = \dfrac{25400}{61.1} - 254 = 415.71 - 254 = 161.71 \; mm $$ **Step 3 — Compute $I_a = 0.2S$:** $$ I_a = 0.2 \times 161.71 = 32.34 \; mm $$ **Step 4 — Check threshold:** P = 60 mm > 32.34 mm → runoff occurs. **Step 5 — Compute Q using the SCS-CN equation:** $$ Q = \dfrac{(P - 0.2S)^2}{P + 0.8S} = \dfrac{(60 - 32.34)^2}{60 + 0.8 \times 161.71} $$ $$ Q = \dfrac{(27.66)^2}{60 + 129.37} = \dfrac{765.08}{189.37} $$ $$ Q \approx 4.04 \; mm $$ **Step 6 — Runoff efficiency:** $$ \eta_{runoff} = \dfrac{4.04}{60} \times 100 \approx 6.73 \; \% $$ > **Conclusion:** The diversified land use (dominated by pasture and forest) yields a composite CN of 61.1 and converts only **~6.73 %** of the 60 mm rainfall into surface runoff, retaining the rest for infiltration, ET, and groundwater recharge. This is a typical signature of a **well-managed watershed**. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students often write C-factor value but forget its **dimensionless** nature — partial marking may apply. > 2. **C-Factor Misconception:** Several students think C-factor is taken for a *single crop* — KTU expects you to compute the **weighted annual C** when multiple crops are grown in a year. > 3. **SCS-CN Initial Abstraction:** Forgetting to check $P > 0.2S$ before applying the runoff equation. If $P \le 0.2S$, then **Q = 0** (often appears in agroforestry questions). > 4. **USLE Sloppiness:** Using the C-factor for *bare soil* as 0 (instead of **1.0**) — this is a **favourite trick question**. > 5. **Conflating Mixed Cropping with Intercropping:** Mixed cropping is **no row arrangement**; intercropping is **with row arrangement**. Examiners mark this strictly. > 6. **Calculation Errors in CN Composite:** Forgetting to divide by the *total* area — only the weighted average formula is accepted. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Cropping system** = spatial + temporal arrangement of crops + management practices. - **USLE formula** ($A = R \cdot K \cdot LS \cdot C \cdot P$) — the **C-factor is the only one controlled by the cropping system**; everything else is fixed by climate, soil, topography, and conservation measures. - **SCS-CN method** — the Curve Number is **inversely related to the quality of the cropping system**: dense agroforestry (CN ≈ 30) vs. bare fallow (CN ≈ 86). - **The five pillars of cropping system effect** — canopy interception, infiltration, erosion control, soil moisture moderation, and nutrient cycling. - **Legume-based rotations** add N, break pest cycles, and build SOM — a cornerstone of sustainable watershed management. - **Multi-tier cropping** (homestead farming) is **Kerala's signature watershed-friendly system** — mention it for state-specific context in answers. - **Soil Loss Tolerance (T)** = 11.2 t ha⁻¹ yr⁻¹ for deep soils; the **goal of any cropping system intervention** is to bring predicted soil loss **below T**. - **Runoff reduction** of 80 – 100 % is achievable by switching from fallow/row crops to agroforestry — backed by SCS-CN calculations. - **Composite CN formula** is essential for sub-watershed scale planning. - **Kerala-specific crops to mention in answers:** coconut, areca nut, black pepper, banana, tapioca, rice (Kuttanad), cardamom, and tea. > **Final Exam Tip:** Always **begin every Part B answer** with a 1-line definition, then proceed to sub-parts. End with a **concluding remark** linking the cropping system choice to **watershed health and sustainability** — this is what KTU examiners reward with full marks. ---

topics.title: Land Management and Cultural Practices on Watershed Hydrology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET741 - WATERSHED DEVELOPMENT AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Land Management and Cultural Practices on Watershed Hydrology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Land Management and Cultural Practices** refer to the systematic manipulation of land use, vegetative cover, soil structure, and agricultural operations (such as tillage, cropping patterns, terracing, and residue management) carried out by humans to control the movement of water, soil, and nutrients within a watershed. In the context of watershed hydrology, these practices directly modulate the three dominant hydrological fluxes: **infiltration**, **surface runoff**, and **evapotranspiration (ET)**.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, the influence is quantified through:
- Changes in the **SCS Curve Number (CN)**
- Modification of the **infiltration capacity** of the soil
- Alteration of the **runoff coefficient (C)** in the Rational Method
- Reduction in **sediment yield** through the Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE)
- Modulation of **base flow** and **peak discharge** in the unit hydrograph

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Cultural practices are evaluated by KTU examiners as **non-structural conservation measures**. They are considered complementary to structural measures like check dams, and are highly important for **CO3 (Watershed Planning)** and **CO4 (Conservation Engineering)** outcomes.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your watershed is like a **giant natural sponge**.

- When the sponge is **healthy, covered in vegetation, and undisturbed** (forests, grasslands, contour-farmed lands), it absorbs almost all the rain, slowly releasing it as groundwater and clean streamflow.
- When the sponge is **overgrazed, ploughed along the slope, or paved with concrete** (urbanization), it becomes hard and rigid. Water bounces off the surface almost immediately, creating floods, soil erosion, and sediment-laden rivers.

**Land management and cultural practices are the "skin treatments" we apply to that sponge** so that it stays absorbent, porous, and resilient to heavy rainfall events. Just as a dermatologist's lotions restore skin elasticity, contour bunds, terracing, mulching, and cover crops restore the watershed's "hydrological elasticity."

### 1.3 Key Land-Use Categories in Watershed Hydrology

| Land-Use Class | Typical Hydrological Behavior | Dominant CN Range (HSG-B) |
|---|---|---|
| Forest with deep litter | High infiltration, low runoff | **30 – 55** |
| Pasture / Grassland | Moderate infiltration, low runoff | **58 – 70** |
| Row Crops (contoured) | Moderate infiltration | **70 – 80** |
| Row Crops (straight rows) | High runoff | **80 – 88** |
| Fallow (bare soil) | Very high runoff, high erosion | **85 – 91** |
| Paved / Urban | Almost 100% runoff | **95 – 98** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Curve Numbers (CN) are dimensionless integers ranging from **0** (all rain infiltrates) to **100** (all rain becomes runoff). The CN is the **single most important parameter** in KTU watershed hydrology problems.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Cultural Practices

Cultural practices in watershed management are broadly grouped into **three families**:

1. **Agricultural Tillage Practices** – Contour farming, conservation tillage, no-till, mulch tillage.
2. **Cropping & Vegetative Practices** – Strip cropping, crop rotation, cover crops, agroforestry.
3. **Land Configuration Practices** – Terracing (bench, broad-base, graded), contour bunding, grassed waterways.

Each family alters a specific portion of the hydrological cycle. The KTU 2024 scheme focuses on the **quantitative impact** of these practices on runoff volume, peak flow, infiltration, and sediment yield.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Hydrological Cycle Linkage

Every cultural practice influences a specific flux in the cycle. The relationship can be summarized as:

$$\text{P} = \text{R} + \text{ET} + \Delta S + \text{DP}$$

Where:
- $\text{P}$ = Precipitation (input)
- $\text{R}$ = Surface runoff (output)
- $\text{ET}$ = Evapotranspiration (loss)
- $\Delta S$ = Change in soil moisture / groundwater storage
- $\text{DP}$ = Deep percolation (recharge)

**Cultural practices shift the partitioning of P among R, ET, and $\Delta S$.** Good practices increase $\Delta S$ and ET, decreasing R.

---

## 2.2 Theory of Major Cultural Practices

### 2.2.1 Contour Farming
- **Definition:** Tillage and planting operations performed along lines of constant elevation (contours), perpendicular to the slope direction.
- **Hydrological Mechanism:** Each furrow acts as a miniature barrier, ponding water long enough for infiltration.
- **Effect on CN:** Reduces CN by **3 – 6 points** compared with up-and-down-hill farming.
- **Effect on Sediment:** Reduces soil loss by **30 – 50%** on slopes of 2–8%.

### 2.2.2 Conservation Tillage
- **Definition:** Any tillage system that leaves at least **30%** of the soil surface covered by crop residue after planting.
- **Variants:** No-till, ridge-till, mulch-till, strip-till.
- **Hydrological Mechanism:** Residue intercepts raindrop impact, preventing surface sealing and crusting → infiltration capacity is preserved.
- **Effect on CN:** Reduces CN by **5 – 8 points** versus conventional moldboard plowing.

### 2.2.3 Strip Cropping
- **Definition:** Alternating strips of close-growing crops (e.g., grass, legume) with row crops along the contour.
- **Types:** Contour strip cropping, field strip cropping, buffer strip cropping, wind strip cropping.
- **Hydrological Mechanism:** The dense strips act as **filter zones**, trapping sediment and slowing runoff velocity.

### 2.2.4 Terracing
- **Definition:** A series of level or nearly level steps constructed across the slope, intercepting and storing surface runoff.
- **Types:** Bench terraces, broad-base terraces, graded terraces, bench-terraced fields.
- **Hydrological Mechanism:** Converts sheet flow into **impounded storage**, increasing infiltration time.
- **Storage Equation:**
$$V_{t} = L \cdot h^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2S_{c}}$$
Where $V_{t}$ is terrace storage per unit width, $L$ is terrace length, $h$ is terrace height, and $S_{c}$ is the channel slope.

### 2.2.5 Crop Rotation and Cover Crops
- Continuous monoculture depletes soil structure; rotation restores aggregate stability and porosity.
- Cover crops (e.g., clover, rye) during the off-season provide **living mulch**, increasing surface roughness.

### 2.2.6 Agroforestry
- Tree-crop-livestock systems on the same land unit.
- Trees increase **deep percolation** through root channels and provide canopy interception (10–25% of rainfall in dense systems).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dfrac{(P - 0.2S)^{2}}{P + 0.8S}$ | SCS-CN runoff depth (valid for $P > 0.2S$) | mm |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{25400}{\text{CN}} - 254$ | Maximum retention (SI units) | mm |
| 3 | $S = \dfrac{1000}{\text{CN}} - 10$ | Maximum retention (FPS units) | inch |
| 4 | $I_{a} = 0.2S$ | Initial abstraction | mm |
| 5 | $Q_{p} = C \cdot i \cdot A$ | Rational Method peak discharge | m³/s |
| 6 | $A = R \cdot K \cdot LS \cdot C \cdot P$ | Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE) | t/ha/yr |
| 7 | $Y_{s} = A \cdot \text{SDR}$ | Sediment yield = soil loss × delivery ratio | t/yr |
| 8 | $f_{t} = f_{c} + (f_{0} - f_{c}) \cdot e^{-kt}$ | Horton's infiltration equation | mm/hr |
| 9 | $\Delta \theta = (P - R - \text{ET}) / z$ | Soil moisture change (per unit depth $z$) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $V_{t} = \dfrac{L \cdot h^{2}}{2S_{c}}$ | Terrace storage capacity (per unit width) | m³/m |
| 11 | $\eta_{c} = \dfrac{R_{b} - R_{a}}{R_{b}} \times 100$ | Runoff reduction efficiency of practice | % |
| 12 | $\tau = \gamma \cdot R \cdot S$ | Shear stress driving erosion (channel) | N/m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Convention:** In KTU numerical problems, the **SCS-CN method** is preferred. The constant **25400** in formula 2 comes from $\dfrac{1\ \text{inch}}{0.03937\ \text{inches/mm}} \cdot 1000 = 25400$, the unit-conversion factor for SI mm.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These practices are not academic. They are deployed by:
- **State Watershed Development Agencies** (e.g., IWMP in India)
- **NRCS-USDA** in the United States for farm planning
- **FAO Watershed Management Field Manuals** for hilly tropical catchments
- **Kerala State Land Use Board** for Wayanad, Idukki, and Palakkad watersheds

The **economic value** of cultural practices is measured in terms of avoided flood damage, retained topsoil, and sustained crop yield. A well-managed watershed with cultural practices can reduce peak discharge by **20–40%** for a 10-year return-period storm.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the SCS-CN Equation

The **Soil Conservation Service Curve Number (SCN-CN)** method is empirical but has a rigorous basis. The development proceeds as follows:

### Step 1: Define the Water Balance for a Storm Event
$$P = I_{a} + F + Q$$

Where:
- $P$ = total precipitation
- $I_{a}$ = initial abstraction (interception, depression storage, early infiltration)
- $F$ = cumulative infiltration after $I_{a}$ is satisfied
- $Q$ = direct surface runoff depth

### Step 2: Define Maximum Possible Retention
The maximum retention $S$ is the upper limit of $F$. The maximum possible runoff is therefore $P - I_{a}$. Define the **ratio of actual to potential quantities** as:
$$\frac{F}{S} = \frac{Q}{P - I_{a}}$$

This is the foundational proportionality assumption of the SCS-CN method. It assumes that the fraction of remaining capacity filled equals the fraction of potential runoff realized.

### Step 3: Empirical Relation Between $I_{a}$ and $S$
Empirical analysis of thousands of small experimental watersheds yielded:
$$I_{a} = 0.2 \cdot S$$

### Step 4: Solve for Q
Substitute $I_{a} = 0.2S$ into the water balance and the proportionality:
$$P = 0.2S + F + Q$$
$$\frac{F}{S} = \frac{Q}{P - 0.2S}$$
$$F = \frac{S \cdot Q}{P - 0.2S}$$

Substituting $F$ into the water balance:
$$P = 0.2S + \frac{S \cdot Q}{P - 0.2S} + Q$$

Rearranging and solving algebraically for $Q$:

\begin{aligned}
P - 0.2S &= Q + \frac{S \cdot Q}{P - 0.2S} \\
P - 0.2S &= Q \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{S}{P - 0.2S} \right) \\
P - 0.2S &= Q \cdot \left( \frac{P - 0.2S + S}{P - 0.2S} \right) \\
P - 0.2S &= Q \cdot \left( \frac{P + 0.8S}{P - 0.2S} \right) \\
Q \cdot (P + 0.8S) &= (P - 0.2S)^{2} \\
Q &= \frac{(P - 0.2S)^{2}}{P + 0.8S}
\end{aligned}

This is the **canonical SCS-CN runoff equation**, valid only when $P > 0.2S$. If $P \leq 0.2S$, then $Q = 0$ (all rain is absorbed by initial abstraction).

### Step 5: Express S in Terms of CN
The Curve Number is defined by the inverse linear mapping:
$$\text{CN} = \frac{25400}{S + 254} \quad \text{(SI)}$$

Rearranging to isolate $S$:
\begin{aligned}
\text{CN} \cdot (S + 254) &= 25400 \\
S + 254 &= \frac{25400}{\text{CN}} \\
S &= \frac{25400}{\text{CN}} - 254
\end{aligned}

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern Problem)

**Problem Statement (Adapted from KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**

A small agricultural watershed in the Palakkad plains has the following characteristics:
- Area $A = 50\ \text{ha}$
- Hydrologic Soil Group (HSG): **B**
- Land use: Row crops, contoured, with crop residue (conservation tillage equivalent)
- Hydrologic condition: Good
- 2-year, 24-hour storm rainfall: $P = 100\ \text{mm}$
- Antecedent Moisture Condition: **AMC-II** (normal)

**Required:**
1. Determine the SCS Curve Number.
2. Compute the maximum retention $S$ and initial abstraction $I_{a}$.
3. Compute the runoff depth $Q$ in mm.
4. Compute the total runoff volume in m³.
5. If the same watershed were cultivated using **straight-row up-and-down-hill farming** (poor hydrologic condition), determine the percentage increase in runoff volume.

---

### Solution

**Step 1: Determine the Curve Number**

From the standard **SCS-CN Table-2** (Row Crops, HSG-B):
- Contoured, good condition: $\text{CN}_{1} = 74$
- Straight row, poor condition: $\text{CN}_{2} = 86$

(These are standard tabulated values prescribed in NRCS Technical Release-55.)

**Step 2: Compute $S$ and $I_{a}$ for $\text{CN} = 74$**

\begin{aligned}
S_{1} &= \frac{25400}{74} - 254 \\
&= 343.24 - 254 \\
&= 89.24\ \text{mm} \\
I_{a1} &= 0.2 \times 89.24 = 17.85\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3: Compute runoff depth $Q_{1}$**

Since $P = 100\ \text{mm} > I_{a1} = 17.85\ \text{mm}$, runoff occurs:

\begin{aligned}
Q_{1} &= \frac{(P - 0.2S_{1})^{2}}{P + 0.8S_{1}} \\
&= \frac{(100 - 17.85)^{2}}{100 + 0.8 \times 89.24} \\
&= \frac{(82.15)^{2}}{100 + 71.39} \\
&= \frac{6748.62}{171.39} \\
&= 39.37\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}

**Step 4: Compute total runoff volume**

\begin{aligned}
V_{1} &= Q_{1} \times A \\
&= 39.37 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} \times 50 \times 10^{4}\ \text{m}^{2} \\
&= 39.37 \times 50 \times 10\ \text{m}^{3} \\
&= 19{,}685\ \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}

**Step 5: Compute runoff for the degraded scenario, $\text{CN}_{2} = 86$**

\begin{aligned}
S_{2} &= \frac{25400}{86} - 254 \\
&= 295.35 - 254 \\
&= 41.35\ \text{mm} \\
I_{a2} &= 0.2 \times 41.35 = 8.27\ \text{mm} \\
Q_{2} &= \frac{(100 - 8.27)^{2}}{100 + 0.8 \times 41.35} \\
&= \frac{(91.73)^{2}}{100 + 33.08} \\
&= \frac{8414.39}{133.08} \\
&= 63.23\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}

Total volume: $V_{2} = 63.23 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 10^{4} = 31{,}615\ \text{m}^{3}$

**Step 6: Percentage increase in runoff volume**

\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Increase} &= \frac{V_{2} - V_{1}}{V_{1}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{31{,}615 - 19{,}685}{19{,}685} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{11{,}930}{19{,}685} \times 100 \\
&= 60.6\%
\end{aligned}

**Conclusion:** Switching from contoured conservation tillage to up-and-down-hill cultivation increases the runoff volume by **60.6%** for the same storm event. This is a strong quantitative justification for cultural practices in watershed management.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Cultural Practices

| Practice | Infiltration Effect | Runoff Reduction | Erosion Reduction | CN Reduction (HSG-B) | Cost | Suitability in Kerala |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Contour Farming | Moderate (+15%) | 20–30% | 30–50% | 3–6 | Low | High (mid-slope) |
| Conservation Tillage | High (+40%) | 25–40% | 50–80% | 5–8 | Low | High (plains) |
| Strip Cropping | Moderate | 30–50% | 40–70% | 4–7 | Low | Medium |
| Bench Terracing | Very High | 60–80% | 70–90% | 10–15 | High | High (hilly) |
| Cover Crops | Moderate | 15–25% | 30–50% | 3–5 | Low | High |
| Agroforestry | High | 30–50% | 50–80% | 5–10 | Medium | High (Western Ghats) |
| Mulching | High (+30%) | 25–40% | 50–70% | 4–7 | Low–Medium | High (plantation crops) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala-Specific Note:** In Wayanad and Idukki districts, **bench terracing in cardamom and tea estates** combined with **mulching** is the most effective cultural practice. The CN reduction translates directly into reduced landslip-triggering runoff during the southwest monsoon.

---

## 3.4 USLE Application — Sediment Yield Estimation

**The Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE) by Wischmeier & Smith (1978):**

$$A = R \cdot K \cdot LS \cdot C \cdot P$$

Where the **P-factor** is the **Conservation Practice Factor** that explicitly quantifies the effect of cultural practices on soil loss:

| Practice | P-Factor |
|---|---|
| Up-and-down-hill farming | **1.0** |
| Contour farming | **0.5 – 0.6** |
| Contour strip cropping | **0.3 – 0.4** |
| Terracing | **0.1 – 0.2** |

**Example:** Compare soil loss under two scenarios for a hilly watershed:
- $R = 250$, $K = 0.35$, $LS = 1.2$
- Scenario 1: Up-and-down-hill ($C = 0.5$, $P = 1.0$)
- Scenario 2: Contour strip cropping with terracing ($C = 0.1$, $P = 0.2$)

Scenario 1: $A_{1} = 250 \times 0.35 \times 1.2 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 = 52.5\ \text{t/ha/yr}$
Scenario 2: $A_{2} = 250 \times 0.35 \times 1.2 \times 0.1 \times 0.2 = 2.1\ \text{t/ha/yr}$

**Reduction:** $(52.5 - 2.1)/52.5 \times 100 = 96\%$ reduction in soil loss. This is the **economic value** of cultural practices in erosion control.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

```mermaid
    D --> G4[Agroforestry]

    E --> H3[Graded Bunds]
```

