<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Define cultural practices in watershed management. List any four cultural practices with a brief note on their hydrological role.**

**Model Answer:**
Cultural practices are non-structural conservation measures involving manipulation of vegetation, tillage, and cropping systems to control runoff, soil loss, and water use at their source.

Four key cultural practices:
1. **Contour Farming:** Tillage along contour lines reduces effective slope length and ponding of water → increased infiltration.
2. **Conservation Tillage:** Leaving ≥ 30% crop residue on the surface prevents raindrop impact sealing → maintains infiltration capacity.
3. **Strip Cropping:** Alternating strips of row and close-grown crops filter sediment and slow runoff velocity.
4. **Crop Rotation:** Maintains soil aggregate stability, increases organic matter, and improves infiltration porosity.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Explain the role of the SCS Curve Number in quantifying the hydrological effect of land management practices.**

**Model Answer:**
The SCS Curve Number (CN) is a dimensionless index ranging from 0 to 100 that integrates the effect of **soil type (HSG), land use, hydrologic condition, and antecedent moisture** into a single parameter. Cultural practices such as contour farming, terracing, and conservation tillage reduce the effective CN of a watershed, which in turn increases the maximum retention $S$ and reduces the runoff depth $Q$. The relation is:
$$Q = \frac{(P - 0.2S)^{2}}{P + 0.8S}, \quad S = \frac{25400}{\text{CN}} - 254$$
A reduction of just 5–6 CN points can reduce runoff by **20–30%** for the same storm event, making CN the most sensitive indicator of land-management effectiveness in watershed hydrology.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Always state units** explicitly in numerical answers. Students lose 1–2 marks for writing "S = 89" without "mm."
> 2. **Do not blindly plug CN into the formula** without first verifying the antecedent moisture condition (AMC-I, II, or III). KTU expects AMC conversion for problems that span seasons.
> 3. **Mention the validity condition** $P > 0.2S$ before applying the runoff equation — failing to check this loses a mark.
> 4. **Show intermediate steps**: $S$, $I_{a}$, $Q$ must appear separately in the solution sheet.
> 5. For **USLE problems**, do not forget the **P-factor (conservation practice factor)** — it is the *most-skipped parameter* in valuation.
> 6. Do **not confuse** CN with the Rational Method runoff coefficient C. They are different. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks when students interchange them.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Question:**
A 75-hectare agricultural watershed in the Idukki hills is being proposed for watershed development. The following data is collected:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Soil group | **HSG-C** (clay loam) |
| Land use | Row crops, contoured |
| Hydrologic condition | Good |
| AMC condition | II |
| Design storm depth $P$ | 120 mm |
| Average slope | 6% |
| Crop residue cover | 40% (conservation tillage) |
| Practice factor $P_{USLE}$ | 0.5 |

**(a)** Determine the SCS Curve Number and compute the runoff depth $Q$ in mm. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compute the total runoff volume in m³, and estimate the soil loss using the USLE given $R = 280$, $K = 0.32$, $LS = 1.5$, $C = 0.18$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Curve Number and Runoff Depth**

Step 1: From SCS-CN Table-2, for row crops contoured, HSG-C, good condition:
$$\text{CN} = 78$$

Step 2: Maximum retention:
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{25400}{\text{CN}} - 254 \\
&= \frac{25400}{78} - 254 \\
&= 325.64 - 254 \\
&= 71.64\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}

Step 3: Initial abstraction:
$$I_{a} = 0.2 \times 71.64 = 14.33\ \text{mm}$$

Step 4: Check validity:
$$P = 120\ \text{mm} > I_{a} = 14.33\ \text{mm} \quad \text{(valid)}$$

Step 5: Runoff depth:
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{(P - 0.2S)^{2}}{P + 0.8S} \\
&= \frac{(120 - 14.33)^{2}}{120 + 0.8 \times 71.64} \\
&= \frac{(105.67)^{2}}{120 + 57.31} \\
&= \frac{11{,}166.15}{177.31} \\
&= 62.97\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}

**Part (b) — Runoff Volume and Soil Loss**

Step 6: Total runoff volume:
\begin{aligned}
V &= Q \times A \\
&= 62.97 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} \times 75 \times 10^{4}\ \text{m}^{2} \\
&= 62.97 \times 75 \times 10 \\
&= 47{,}227.5\ \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}

Step 7: USLE Soil Loss:
\begin{aligned}
A &= R \cdot K \cdot LS \cdot C \cdot P \\
&= 280 \times 0.32 \times 1.5 \times 0.18 \times 0.5 \\
&= 12.10\ \text{t/ha/yr}
\end{aligned}

Step 8: Total annual soil loss from the watershed:
$$\text{Total Loss} = 12.10 \times 75 = 907.5\ \text{t/yr}$$

**Final Answer (boxed values expected in KTU answer sheets):**
- $\text{CN} = 78$
- $Q = 62.97\ \text{mm}$
- $V = 47{,}227.5\ \text{m}^{3}$
- $A = 12.10\ \text{t/ha/yr}$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Internal Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Question:**
**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, describe the influence of land use and cultural practices on the components of the hydrological cycle. Explain how each practice modifies infiltration, runoff, and evapotranspiration. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A 100-hectare forested watershed (HSG-B, good condition, AMC-II) is being cleared for conventional straight-row cultivation. The 25-year, 24-hour storm depth is 150 mm. Compute the change in runoff depth and volume. Comment on the implications for downstream flood peaks. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Qualitative Description**

(Students should reproduce the functional flow from SECTION 4.1.) Each cultural practice is linked to its dominant hydrological effect:

| Practice | Infiltration | Runoff | ET |
|---|---|---|---|
| Contour Farming | ↑ | ↓ | — |
| Conservation Tillage | ↑↑ | ↓ | ↑ (residue shading) |
| Strip Cropping | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ |
| Terracing | ↑↑ | ↓↓ | ↑ (more soil moisture) |
| Agroforestry | ↑↑ | ↓ | ↑↑ (transpiration) |
| Deforestation | ↓↓ | ↑↑ | ↓ |

> [!NOTE]
> A fully labeled diagram with arrows should be drawn on the answer sheet. A schematic **block diagram** showing the cycle $\text{P} \rightarrow \text{I} \rightarrow \text{ET}, \text{R}, \Delta S$ with arrows pointing to where each practice acts will earn full 7 marks.

**Part (b) — Quantitative Comparison**

Step 1: Curve Numbers for HSG-B, AMC-II:
- Forest, good condition: $\text{CN}_{1} = 55$
- Row crops, straight-row, poor condition: $\text{CN}_{2} = 86$

Step 2: Compute $S$ values:
\begin{aligned}
S_{1} &= \frac{25400}{55} - 254 = 461.82 - 254 = 207.82\ \text{mm} \\
S_{2} &= \frac{25400}{86} - 254 = 295.35 - 254 = 41.35\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}

Step 3: Initial abstractions:
$$I_{a1} = 0.2 \times 207.82 = 41.56\ \text{mm}, \quad I_{a2} = 0.2 \times 41.35 = 8.27\ \text{mm}$$

Step 4: Runoff depths (note: $P = 150 > I_{a}$ in both cases):
\begin{aligned}
Q_{1} &= \frac{(150 - 41.56)^{2}}{150 + 0.8 \times 207.82} = \frac{(108.44)^{2}}{150 + 166.26} = \frac{11{,}759.23}{316.26} = 37.18\ \text{mm} \\
Q_{2} &= \frac{(150 - 8.27)^{2}}{150 + 0.8 \times 41.35} = \frac{(141.73)^{2}}{150 + 33.08} = \frac{20{,}087.39}{183.08} = 109.72\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}

Step 5: Runoff volumes:
\begin{aligned}
V_{1} &= 37.18 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 \times 10^{4} = 37{,}180\ \text{m}^{3} \\
V_{2} &= 109.72 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 \times 10^{4} = 109{,}720\ \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}

Step 6: Change:
\begin{aligned}
\Delta Q &= 109.72 - 37.18 = 72.54\ \text{mm (increase)} \\
\Delta V &= 109{,}720 - 37{,}180 = 72{,}540\ \text{m}^{3} \\
\% \text{ Increase} &= \frac{72{,}540}{37{,}180} \times 100 = 195.1\%
\end{aligned}

**Implications:** Deforestation followed by conventional cultivation **nearly triples** the runoff depth and volume for a 25-year storm. This translates to a sharp rise in the peak discharge of the unit hydrograph, reduced time of concentration, increased flood frequency in downstream areas, and loss of base flow during dry months. This finding is consistent with **Kerala's recurring post-2018 flood patterns** in the Western Ghats catchments.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**

- ✅ **Curve Number (CN)** is the cornerstone parameter; it is **dimensionless** and ranges from **0 to 100**.
- ✅ The **SCS-CN equation** $Q = (P - 0.2S)^{2} / (P + 0.8S)$ is valid **only when $P > 0.2S$**.
- ✅ The constant **25400** in $S = 25400/\text{CN} - 254$ converts to mm; use **1000** for inch units.
- ✅ **Hydrologic Soil Groups (HSG):** A (sandy, high infiltration), B (loam), C (clay loam), D (clay, low infiltration).
- ✅ **AMC-II** is the standard (normal) condition. KTU expects conversion tables for AMC-I and AMC-III when seasonal data is given.
- ✅ **Initial abstraction $I_{a} = 0.2S$** is an empirical result, not a physical law.
- ✅ **USLE factors** $R$, $K$, $LS$, $C$, $P$ are all **dimensionless** except $R$ (MJ·mm/ha·h·yr). Final $A$ is in **t/ha/yr**.
- ✅ **Contour farming** reduces CN by **3–6**; **Conservation tillage** by **5–8**; **Terracing** by **10–15**.
- ✅ **Runoff reduction efficiency** $\eta_{c}$ is the most common exam parameter in Part A.
- ✅ **Sediment Delivery Ratio (SDR)** converts gross erosion $A$ to net sediment yield at the outlet: $Y_{s} = A \cdot \text{SDR}$.
- ✅ **Horton's infiltration** decays exponentially from $f_{0}$ to $f_{c}$; useful for **tilled vs untilled** comparison.
- ✅ In **Kerala context**, emphasize **terracing, mulching, and agroforestry** for the Western Ghats, and **paddy-bound cultivation with contour farming** for the midlands.
- ✅ **Common examiner traps:** forgetting the P-factor in USLE; using CN as a percentage; ignoring AMC conversion; mixing up $C$ (Rational) and CN (SCS).
- ✅ Always conclude numerical problems with a **commentary sentence** on engineering significance — KTU examiners allocate **1–2 marks** for interpretation.

---

**End of Notes — Module 3, Topic: Land Management and Cultural Practices on Watershed Hydrology**

*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme — B.Tech PGAET741 — Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Watershed Programme - Execution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET741 - WATERSHED DEVELOPMENT AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Watershed Programme - Execution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Watershed Programme Execution** refers to the systematic, phased, and time-bound implementation of all approved physical, biological, and socio-economic interventions outlined in the Detailed Project Report (DPR) of a watershed project, carried out by the Project Implementing Agency (PIA) under the technical and administrative supervision of the Watershed Development Department / State Nodal Agency, strictly adhering to the operational guidelines, schedule of activities, financial protocols, and quality control standards prescribed under the relevant scheme (e.g., MGNREGA Convergence, IWMP, PMKSY-WDC, Neeranchal).

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, *execution* is distinct from *planning* (Module 2) and *monitoring* (Module 4) — it is the **"Do"** phase of the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle applied at the watershed scale.

### 1.2 Key Stakeholders in Execution

| Stakeholder | Role in Execution |
|---|---|
| **Project Implementing Agency (PIA)** | Ground-level execution, contracting, supervision |
| **Watershed Association (WA)** | Community mobilization, local prioritization |
| **Watershed Committee (WC)** | Decision-making, fund allocation, beneficiary selection |
| **Line Departments (Agriculture, Forest, Soil Conservation)** | Technical convergence and co-implementation |
| **State Nodal Agency (SNA)** | Quality assurance, fund flow, audit |
| **District Watershed Development Agency (DWDA)** | District-level coordination |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "House Construction" Model**
> Think of a watershed programme like building a house. **Planning** is the architect's blueprint. **Execution** is when the contractor actually lays the foundation, raises the walls, plumbs the pipes, and wires the electricals — according to the blueprint, on schedule, within budget, and to quality standards. If the contractor skips curing time for the concrete or uses sub-standard bricks, the house collapses later, even if the blueprint was perfect. Watershed execution is the same: it is the *doing* phase, and poor execution destroys even the best DPR.
>
> - **Blueprint** = Detailed Project Report (DPR)
> - **Contractor** = PIA + Watershed Committee
> - **Building Inspector** = Monitoring Cell / SQA
> - **Material Supplier** = Line Departments / Convergence schemes
> - **Homeowner Inspecting** = Gram Sabha / User Group

### 1.4 Standard Execution Metrics (KTU High-Yield Values)

- **Standard Project Duration:** 4 to 5 years (as per IWMP/PMKSY-WDC guidelines)
- **Phases:** Preparatory (0–6 months), Work Phase (6–48 months), Consolidation & Withdrawal (48–60 months)
- **Funding Pattern (PMKSY-WDC):** 60:40 (Centre:State for non-NE states); 90:10 for NE/Himalayan states
- **Treatment Cost Norm:** ₹15,000/ha (plain areas); ₹17,000/ha (hilly/tribal areas) — *indicative, latest SNA notifications prevail*
- **Community Contribution:** Minimum **5%** of project cost in cash or kind (labour)
- **Administrative Cost Cap:** ≤ **10%** of total project cost

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Sequential Phases of Watershed Programme Execution

The execution of a watershed programme follows a rigid, audit-compliant sequence. Deviation invites financial disallowance by the CAG and the SNA.

#### Phase 1: Entry Point Activities (EPA) — Months 0 to 3
- Purpose: Build community trust before technical works begin.
- Activities: Awareness camps, baseline survey, formation/strengthening of **Watershed Association (WA)**, **Watershed Committee (WC)**, and **User Groups (UGs)**.
- Output: Registered WC, opened bank account (joint signature of President + PIA representative), baseline socio-economic register.

#### Phase 2: Preparation Phase — Months 3 to 6
- Participatory Rural Appraisal (PRA), Transect Walk, Resource Mapping.
- Finalization of DPR with micro-watershed-wise treatment plan.
- Signing of **Memorandum of Understanding (MoU)** between PIA, WC, and SNA.
- Preparation of **Annual Action Plan (AAP)** with month-wise physical and financial targets.

#### Phase 3: Work Phase (The Core Execution) — Months 6 to 48
This is the longest and most marks-bearing phase for KTU. It has **four parallel sub-streams**:

1. **Soil & Moisture Conservation (SMC) Works**
   - Contour trenches, staggered trenches, percolation tanks, check dams, loose boulder structures, gabion structures, farm ponds, terracing.

2. **Plantation / Afforestation (Biological Works)**
   - Nursery raising, planting on ridge lines, canal banks, waste lands.
   - Species selection: nitrogen-fixing (e.g., *Acacia*, *Leucaena*, *Albizia*), fruit-bearing (mango, custard apple), and multi-purpose trees.

3. **Livelihood / Production System Activities**
   - Crop demonstration, horticulture plantations, dairy, goatery, poultry, fishery, micro-enterprises.

4. **Convergence Activities**
   - MGNREGA wage employment convergence.
   - NRLM (rural livelihoods) convergence.
   - Agriculture Department schemes (PM-KISAN, RKVY) convergence.

#### Phase 4: Consolidation Phase — Months 48 to 54
- Repair and maintenance of created assets.
- Documentation of outcomes.
- Formation of **Watershed Development Fund (WDF)** — interest income used for post-project maintenance.
- Handing over of assets to the community / User Groups.

#### Phase 5: Withdrawal / Exit Phase — Months 54 to 60
- Final social audit, financial audit, impact evaluation.
- Submission of **Completion Certificate** by PIA.
- Closure report to SNA.

### 2.2 Project Implementation Schedule (PIS) — The Critical KTU Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Project Implementation Schedule (PIS):** A bar-chart / Gantt-chart-based document that converts the AAP into a month-by-month action calendar showing *what activity*, *by whom*, *with what funds*, and *under which convergence*, will be undertaken. The PIS is the **single most important document a PIA maintains** during execution and is the primary audit object.

### 2.3 Financial Management During Execution

| Financial Activity | Governing Rule |
|---|---|
| Fund release to PIA | Sanction-based, in 3-4 instalments linked to milestone achievement |
| Bank account operation | Jointly by WC President + PIA representative (mandatory) |
| Utilization Certificate (UC) | To be submitted within 1 month of fund use, else next instalment is held |
| Social Audit | Conducted by Gram Sabha every 6 months |
| Financial Audit | By Chartered Accountant empanelled with SNA, annually |
| EAT (Expenditure, Advance, Transfer) module | Online entry mandatory under PMKSY |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Watershed Execution

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all high-yield equations and ratios for execution-related numerical problems.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Use Case in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Project Cost (TPC) | $\text{TPC} = (\text{Area in ha}) \times (\text{Normative Cost per ha})$ | ₹ | Budgeting question |
| 2 | Centre Share (CS) | $\text{CS} = \text{TPC} \times 0.60$ (non-NE) | ₹ | Fund-flow question |
| 3 | State Share (SS) | $\text{SS} = \text{TPC} \times 0.40$ (non-NE) | ₹ | Fund-flow question |
| 4 | Community Contribution (CC) | $\text{CC} = \text{TPC} \times 0.05$ | ₹ | Eligibility question |
| 5 | Administrative Cost Limit | $\text{AC}_{\max} = 0.10 \times \text{TPC}$ | ₹ | Cap-check question |
| 6 | Available for Works | $\text{AW} = \text{TPC} - \text{CC} - \text{AC}$ | ₹ | Works budget |
| 7 | MGNREGA Wage Component | $\text{Wage} = \text{AW} \times 0.60$ (typical) | ₹ | Convergence question |
| 8 | Material Component | $\text{Material} = \text{AW} \times 0.40$ (typical) | ₹ | Convergence question |
| 9 | Project Duration | $D = 5$ years (standard) | years | Scheduling |
| 10 | Work Phase Fraction | $W_f = \dfrac{42}{60} = 0.70$ | ratio | Phase-weightage |
| 11 | Beneficiary-to-Area Ratio | $B = \dfrac{N_{\text{hh}}}{\text{Area in ha}}$ | households/ha | DPR check |
| 12 | Treatment Density Index | $\rho_t = \dfrac{N_{\text{structures}}}{\text{Area in ha}}$ | structures/ha | Execution density |
| 13 | WDF Corpus | $\text{WDF} = 0.10 \times \text{TPC}$ (typical) | ₹ | Sustainability |
| 14 | Expected Runoff Reduction | $\Delta R = R_0 \left(1 - e^{-k \cdot t}\right)$ | mm | Impact estimation |

> *Where $R_0$ = pre-project runoff, $k$ = land-treatment coefficient (depends on slope, soil, vegetation), $t$ = years since execution.*

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Civil Engineering:** Design of check dams, farm ponds, percolation tanks — requires hydrological and structural calculations.
- **Agricultural Engineering:** Crop water budgeting, irrigation scheduling post-execution.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Quantifying carbon sequestration, sediment load reduction.
- **Sociology / Extension Education:** Community mobilization metrics during EPA and Consolidation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Execution Phase vs. Activity Type

The following table explicitly cross-tabs the execution phases with the type of activity, the responsible agency, the deliverable, and the **auditable document** produced. This is a *guaranteed* KTU question pattern.

| Phase | Duration (months) | Activity Type | Responsible Agency | Deliverable | Auditable Document |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| EPA | 0–3 | Community mobilization | PIA + WC | Baseline register, formed WC | Registration certificate, attendance sheet |
| Preparation | 3–6 | DPR finalization | PIA + SNA technical cell | Approved DPR | Approved DPR copy + MoU |
| Work Phase | 6–48 | SMC Works | PIA + contractor | Trenches, check dams | Measurement book (MB), photographs, vouchers |
| Work Phase | 6–48 | Biological Works | Forest Dept. + WC | Planted area | Nursery register, plantation journal |
| Work Phase | 6–48 | Livelihood | Line Dept. + UG | Assets to BPL families | Asset transfer register, photographs |
| Consolidation | 48–54 | Asset handover | WC + UG | WDF created | Bank passbook, MOU for maintenance |
| Withdrawal | 54–60 | Final closure | PIA + SNA | Completion certificate | Audit report + Impact Study |

### 3.2 Worked Example — Cost Allocation in a Watershed Project (14-Mark KTU Pattern)

> **Problem Statement (as typically asked in KTU ESE):**
> A watershed project covering **1500 hectares** in a non-NE, non-tribal area is sanctioned under PMKSY-WDC. The normative cost is **₹15,000/ha**. The community contributes the minimum mandated share in full. The PIA plans to spend 10% on administration. Calculate:
> 1. Total Project Cost
> 2. Centre and State share (as per 60:40)
> 3. Community Contribution
> 4. Administrative Cost
> 5. Amount available for direct field works
> 6. If 60% of works budget is converged with MGNREGA, calculate the wage and material components.
> 7. Calculate the WDF corpus at 10% of TPC.

#### Step 1 — Total Project Cost (TPC)

$$
\text{TPC} = \text{Area (ha)} \times \text{Normative Cost per ha}
$$

$$
\text{TPC} = 1500 \times 15000
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TPC} = ₹2,25,00,000 \; (\text{₹ 2.25 Crore})}
$$

*Textual logic:* We are told the area is 1500 ha, and the per-hectare cost is ₹15,000. Multiplying gives the total sanctioned outlay of the watershed project.

#### Step 2 — Centre and State Share (60:40 for non-NE)

$$
\text{CS} = \text{TPC} \times 0.60 = 22500000 \times 0.60
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CS} = ₹1,35,00,000}
$$

$$
\text{SS} = \text{TPC} \times 0.40 = 22500000 \times 0.40
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{SS} = ₹90,00,000}
$$

*Textual logic:* As per the funding pattern for general states under PMKSY-WDC, the central government bears 60% of the total cost and the state bears the remaining 40%. We directly apply these percentages to the TPC.

#### Step 3 — Community Contribution (CC)

$$
\text{CC} = \text{TPC} \times 0.05 = 22500000 \times 0.05
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CC} = ₹11,25,000}
$$

*Textual logic:* The scheme mandates a minimum 5% contribution from the community in cash or labour (kind). 5% of ₹2.25 Crore is ₹11.25 Lakh.

#### Step 4 — Administrative Cost (AC)

$$
\text{AC} = \text{TPC} \times 0.10 = 22500000 \times 0.10
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{AC} = ₹22,50,000}
$$

*Textual logic:* The administrative cost is capped at 10% of TPC. 10% of ₹2.25 Crore is ₹22.5 Lakh. This must be within the cap (which it is, by definition).

#### Step 5 — Amount Available for Field Works (AW)

$$
\text{AW} = \text{TPC} - \text{CC} - \text{AC}
$$

$$
\text{AW} = 22500000 - 1125000 - 2250000
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{AW} = ₹1,91,25,000}
$$

*Textual logic:* The total outlay is reduced by the community contribution (which is raised separately) and the administrative overhead, leaving the balance for direct field interventions such as SMC works, plantations, and livelihood activities.

#### Step 6 — MGNREGA Convergence Split (Wage and Material)

If 60% of AW is converged with MGNREGA, the convergence amount is:

$$
\text{Converged} = \text{AW} \times 0.60 = 19125000 \times 0.60
$$

$$
\text{Converged} = ₹1,14,75,000
$$

Within MGNREGA, the wage-to-material ratio is 60:40 (i.e., 60% of expenditure is on wages, 40% on materials). Applying this to the converged amount:

$$
\text{Wage} = \text{Converged} \times 0.60 = 11475000 \times 0.60
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Wage} = ₹68,85,000}
$$

$$
\text{Material} = \text{Converged} \times 0.40 = 11475000 \times 0.40
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Material} = ₹45,90,000}
$$

*Textual logic:* Under MGNREGA, the statutory wage share is 60% of total expenditure. So out of the ₹1.1475 Crore converged, ₹68.85 Lakh goes as wages to job-card holders and ₹45.9 Lakh is for materials like cement, sand, and bio-engineering inputs.

#### Step 7 — Watershed Development Fund (WDF) Corpus

$$
\text{WDF} = \text{TPC} \times 0.10 = 22500000 \times 0.10
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{WDF} = ₹22,50,000}
$$

*Textual logic:* The WDF is a revolving maintenance fund created at the end of the project to ensure post-execution sustainability. The corpus is parked in a fixed deposit, and the interest income is used for repairs to the created assets.

### 3.3 Final Summary Table for the Worked Example

| Parameter | Symbol | Value (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Total Project Cost | TPC | 2,25,00,000 |
| Centre Share | CS | 1,35,00,000 |
| State Share | SS | 90,00,000 |
| Community Contribution | CC | 11,25,000 |
| Administrative Cost | AC | 22,50,000 |
| Available for Works | AW | 1,91,25,000 |
| MGNREGA Converged Amount | — | 1,14,75,000 |
| Wage Component | W | 68,85,000 |
| Material Component | M | 45,90,000 |
| WDF Corpus | WDF | 22,50,000 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Master Workflow of Watershed Programme Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Project Sanctioned by SNA] --> B0[EPA: Months 0 to 3]
    B0 --> B1[Community Mobilization]
    B0 --> B2[Baseline Survey]
    B0 --> B3[WC and UG Formation]
    B0 --> B4[Bank Account Opened]

    B3 --> C0[Preparation: Months 3 to 6]
    B4 --> C0
    C0 --> C1[PRA and Transect Walk]
    C0 --> C2[DPR Finalization]
    C0 --> C3[MoU Signed]
    C0 --> C4[Annual Action Plan]

    C4 --> D0[Work Phase: Months 6 to 48]
    D0 --> D1[SMC Works]
    D0 --> D2[Biological Works]
    D0 --> D3[Livelihood Works]
    D0 --> D4[Convergence Works]

    D1 --> E0[Consolidation: Months 48 to 54]
    D2 --> E0
    D3 --> E0
    D4 --> E0
    E0 --> E1[Asset Repair]
    E0 --> E2[WDF Created]
    E0 --> E3[Asset Handover to UG]

    E3 --> F0[Withdrawal: Months 54 to 60]
    F0 --> F1[Social Audit]
    F0 --> F2[Financial Audit]
    F0 --> F3[Impact Study]
    F0 --> F4[Completion Certificate Issued]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fund Flow During Execution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SNA[State Nodal Agency] -->|Milestone Based Release| PIA[Project Implementing Agency]
    PIA -->|Joint Signatory Transfer| WC[Watershed Committee Bank A/c]
    WC -->|Work Order| CON[Contractor]
    WC -->|Wage Payment| UG[User Groups and Labourers]
    WC -->|Material Purchase| SUP[Vendors and Line Depts]
    SNA -->|Audit and Inspection| PIA
    WC -->|Social Audit| GS[Gram Sabha]
    SNA -->|UC and Compliance Check| GOI[Government of India]
    WC -->|Interest Income| WDF[Watershed Development Fund]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Convergence Architecture During Execution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WD[Watershed Project Funds] --> SM[Soil and Moisture Works]
    WD --> PL[Plantation and Afforestation]
    WD --> LV[Livelihood Activities]

    MGN[MGNREGA] --> WAGE[Unskilled Labour Wage]
    MGN --> MAT[Material Component 40 percent]
    NRLM[DAY NRLM] --> SK[Skill Training and SHGs]
    AGR[RKVY and PM KISAN] --> CRP[Crop Demonstrations]
    FRST[Forest Department] --> PL

    SM -.uses.-> WAGE
    SM -.uses.-> MAT
    PL -.uses.-> FRST
    LV -.uses.-> SK
    LV -.uses.-> CRP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Architecture?** Since the *physical* drawings of trenches, check dams, and contour bunds cannot be rendered in a Mermaid graph, the *functional block architecture* above faithfully represents the *operational reality* of how money, materials, and labour move during execution — which is the actual concern of the KTU examiner.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List the five sequential phases of watershed programme execution with their typical duration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Entry Point Activities (EPA):** Months 0–3 — community mobilization and WC formation.
2. **Preparation Phase:** Months 3–6 — DPR finalization, PRA, MoU signing.
3. **Work Phase:** Months 6–48 — execution of SMC, biological, and livelihood works.
4. **Consolidation Phase:** Months 48–54 — asset repair, WDF creation, handover.
5. **Withdrawal Phase:** Months 54–60 — audits, impact study, completion certificate.

*Each phase name with duration = 0.5 mark × 5 = 2.5; logical flow sentence = 0.5 mark. Total 3 marks.*

---

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is a Project Implementation Schedule (PIS)? Why is it critical during execution?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Project Implementation Schedule (PIS)** is a month-wise Gantt-chart calendar that converts the Annual Action Plan into a binding timetable specifying **what activity, by whom, with what funds, and under which convergence scheme** will be executed in each month of the 5-year project life.

**Why critical (2 marks):**
- Serves as the *primary audit document* for SNA and CAG.
- Enables milestone-based fund release.
- Prevents time-overrun and cost-overrun.
- Forms the basis for **physical and financial progress reporting**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

> **A watershed of 1200 hectares in a non-tribal plain area is sanctioned under PMKSY-WDC. Normative cost is ₹15,000/ha. The community contributes 5% and the PIA spends 10% on administration. The PIA converges 50% of the works budget with MGNREGA.**
>
> **(a)** Calculate the Total Project Cost, Centre share, State share, and Community Contribution.
> **(b)** Calculate the amount available for field works, the converged amount, and the wage and material components under MGNREGA. Also calculate the WDF corpus at 10% of TPC.

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Given Data:**
- Area $= 1200$ ha
- Normative cost $= ₹15{,}000$/ha
- Centre:State $= 60:40$ (non-NE, non-tribal)
- Community Contribution $= 5\%$ of TPC
- Administrative Cost $= 10\%$ of TPC
- MGNREGA Converged $= 50\%$ of AW
- WDF $= 10\%$ of TPC

**(a) Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Total Project Cost**

$$
\text{TPC} = 1200 \times 15000 = ₹1{,}80{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Centre Share**

$$
\text{CS} = 1{,}80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 = ₹1{,}08{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3: State Share**

$$
\text{SS} = 1{,}80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 = ₹72{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Community Contribution**

$$
\text{CC} = 1{,}80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.05 = ₹9{,}00{,}000
$$

*Each correct value with formula = 1.75 marks. Total 7 marks for part (a).*

**(b) Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 5: Administrative Cost**

$$
\text{AC} = 1{,}80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = ₹18{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 6: Amount Available for Works**

$$
\text{AW} = \text{TPC} - \text{CC} - \text{AC} = 1{,}80{,}00{,}000 - 9{,}00{,}000 - 18{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
\text{AW} = ₹1{,}53{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 7: Converged Amount with MGNREGA (50% of AW)**

$$
\text{Converged} = 1{,}53{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 = ₹76{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 8: Wage Component (60% of Converged)**

$$
\text{Wage} = 76{,}50{,}000 \times 0.60 = ₹45{,}90{,}000
$$

**Step 9: Material Component (40% of Converged)**

$$
\text{Material} = 76{,}50{,}000 \times 0.40 = ₹30{,}60{,}000
$$

**Step 10: WDF Corpus**

$$
\text{WDF} = 1{,}80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = ₹18{,}00{,}000
$$

*Each correct value with formula = 1.4 marks. Total 7 marks for part (b).*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

> **(a)** Describe in detail the **Work Phase (Months 6 to 48)** of watershed programme execution. Cover the four parallel sub-streams of activities.
>
> **(b)** Explain the **fund-flow mechanism** from SNA to the field during watershed execution, highlighting the role of the Watershed Committee's bank account, joint signatories, and the Utilization Certificate (UC) cycle.

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Part (a) — 7 marks — Detailed Sub-Streams of Work Phase**

The Work Phase is the **core execution window** and is divided into four parallel sub-streams:

**1. Soil and Moisture Conservation (SMC) Works — 2 marks**

- Contour / staggered trenches on degraded hill slopes.
- Loose boulder check dams across gullies and streams.
- Gabion structures, percolation tanks, farm ponds.
- Terracing on agricultural land.
- These works are the **physical backbone** of the watershed and consume ~50–55% of the works budget.

**2. Biological / Afforestation Works — 1.5 marks**

- Nursery raising by Forest Department or WCs.
- Plantation on ridge lines, canal banks, community lands, waste lands.
- Species: nitrogen-fixing (Acacia, Leucaena), fruit (mango, custard apple), and fodder species.
- Survival audits at 6, 12, 24 months.

**3. Livelihood / Production System Activities — 2 marks**

- Crop demonstrations, seed minikits, integrated nutrient management.
- Horticulture plantations (mango, cashew, coconut) on 0.2–1.0 ha per beneficiary.
- Animal husbandry: dairy, goatery, poultry, fishery.
- Micro-enterprise support through SHGs.
- **BPL/priority:** At least 50% of livelihood benefits to BPL/women/SC-ST households.

**4. Convergence Works — 1.5 marks**

- **MGNREGA:** Wage component (60%) and material component (40%) converged with SMC works.
- **NRLM:** SHG formation, skill training, bank linkage.
- **RKVY / PM-KISAN / NFSM:** Crop productivity interventions.
- **MGNREGS Convergence** is the *most important* because it dramatically expands the wage budget without straining the watershed's own funds.

**(b) Part (b) — 7 marks — Fund Flow and UC Cycle**

**Step 1 — Sanction and First Release:** The SNA, after technical and administrative approval of the DPR, releases the **first instalment** (typically 30% of CS+SS) to the PIA's account.

**Step 2 — Transfer to WC Account:** The PIA transfers the released funds to the **Watershed Committee's (WC) bank account**, which is operated under **joint signatures** — the WC President (community) and the PIA representative (government). This dual control prevents misappropriation and is *auditable*.

**Step 3 — Drawal against Work Orders:** The WC / PIA issues work orders to contractors or User Groups. Payments are made from the WC account against:
- Measurement Book (MB) entries
- Photographs (geo-tagged, dated)
- Vouchers and muster rolls

**Step 4 — Utilization Certificate (UC):** The WC, through the PIA, must submit a **UC to the SNA within 1 month** of incurring expenditure. UC is a statutory document under the Financial Rules and is the *trigger* for the next instalment.

**Step 5 — Subsequent Instalments:** The SNA releases the next instalment (typically 30% → 25% → 15%) **only upon receipt of satisfactory UC, audit compliance, and physical progress report**. The final 15% is released as **performance-based retention**.

**Step 6 — Audit Trail:** Concurrent social audit by Gram Sabha every 6 months, annual financial audit by a CA empanelled with the SNA, and final audit at project closure.

*Fund-flow diagram = 2 marks; UC cycle explanation = 2 marks; Joint signatory and audit trail = 1.5 marks; Conceptual clarity on milestone-based release = 1.5 marks. Total 7 marks.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write!**
>
> 1. **Funding Pattern Confusion:** Many students write 90:10 for all states. *Wrong.* 90:10 is only for **NE and Himalayan states**. For Kerala (a non-NE, non-Himalayan state), it is **60:40**. This single error will cost 2 marks.
> 2. **Community Contribution 5% vs. 10%:** Some schemes have different norms. For PMKSY-WDC, the community share is **5% of TPC**, *not* 10%. The 10% you may have seen elsewhere refers to Administrative Cost or WDF.
> 3. **Confusing AC with AW:** Administrative Cost (10%) is **deducted from TPC** along with Community Contribution before arriving at the *Available for Works* figure. Many students erroneously compute AW = TPC − CC only.
> 4. **MGNREGA Ratio:** The 60:40 split is **wage:material** within the *converged* amount, not of the entire TPC. Do not apply 60% to TPC.
> 5. **Phase Duration Mistakes:** Consolidation is 6 months (48–54), not 12 months. Withdrawal is 6 months (54–60), not 3 months.
> 6. **Missing MoU:** Forgetting to mention the Memorandum of Understanding in the Preparation Phase costs a mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Five Phases:** EPA → Preparation → Work → Consolidation → Withdrawal. (Total **5 years / 60 months**.)
- **Work Phase is the longest** (42 months, 70% of project life) and has **4 parallel sub-streams**: SMC, Biological, Livelihood, Convergence.
- **Funding Pattern (Kerala / general state):** Centre **60%** : State **40%** : Community **5%** of TPC.
- **Admin Cost Cap:** **10%** of TPC (mandatory ceiling).
- **MGNREGA Convergence:** Wage **60%** : Material **40%** of the *converged* amount (not of TPC).
- **WDF Corpus:** Typically **10% of TPC** parked in fixed deposit; interest used for post-project maintenance.
- **Joint Signatory Rule:** WC bank account operated jointly by **WC President + PIA representative**.
- **UC is the lifeline of fund flow:** No UC → No next instalment.
- **Project Implementation Schedule (PIS):** The single most important document for execution; converts AAP into a month-wise Gantt chart.
- **Social Audit:** Every 6 months by Gram Sabha.
- **50% Livelihood Quota:** At least 50% of livelihood benefits must go to BPL/women/SC-ST households.
- **MGNREGA Convergence is the financial multiplier** that amplifies the works budget without extra fiscal burden.
- **Kerala-specific note:** Since Kerala is a non-NE, non-Himalayan, non-tribal-dominant state, the **60:40** funding pattern and **₹15,000/ha** norm apply; the 5% community contribution and 10% admin cap remain the same.
- **Audit-Ready Mindset:** Every rupee spent must have an MB entry, a voucher, a photograph, and a UC. The CAG audit begins after 60 months.

> **End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Watershed Programme: Execution**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Follow-up Practices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET741 - WATERSHED DEVELOPMENT AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Follow-up Practices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Follow-up Practices** in Watershed Development and Management refer to the systematic, continuous, and structured set of post-implementation activities, monitoring mechanisms, and management interventions undertaken **after the completion of watershed development works** to ensure the **sustainability, functional efficiency, and long-term impact** of the established watershed structures and interventions.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme context, follow-up practices encompass:

1. **Post-project monitoring** of physical, hydrological, and agronomic parameters.
2. **Maintenance and repairs** of watershed structures (check dams, percolation tanks, gully plugs, farm ponds, etc.).
3. **Capacity building** and **institutional development** of the watershed community.
4. **Impact assessment** using baseline vs. post-intervention indicators.
5. **Adaptive management** based on continuous feedback loops.
6. **Sustainability audits** to ensure continuity of the watershed benefits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:**
> *"Follow-up practices constitute the post-implementation phase of watershed management that ensures the transition from project-mode to programme-mode operation, integrating biophysical monitoring, socio-economic evaluation, institutional strengthening, and adaptive management for sustainable watershed governance."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you have just planted a beautiful garden. You dug the soil, planted the saplings, installed a drip irrigation system, and built a small fence. **Would you walk away and never look at it again?** Of course not!

- You would **water the plants regularly** (Maintenance).
- You would **check if the drip system is clogged** (Monitoring).
- You would **train your family members** to care for the garden (Capacity Building).
- You would **observe which plants are thriving and which are dying** (Impact Assessment).
- You would **add compost or change the watering schedule** based on observations (Adaptive Management).

**Follow-up practices in a watershed are EXACTLY this — but at a landscape scale.** The watershed is the "garden," the structures (check dams, percolation tanks, contour bunds) are the "plants and irrigation system," and the villagers / Watershed Committee (WC) / Self-Help Groups (SHGs) are the "family members" who must sustain it.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Watershed Management:**
> *"A watershed without follow-up is a building without maintenance — it WILL collapse. The 5-7 year post-project phase is the MOST critical period determining whether the watershed delivers its promised benefits for 50+ years or fails within 5."*

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Parameters in Follow-up

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Focus Areas for Follow-up:**
> - **Monitoring Frequency** — usually **monthly, quarterly, and annually**
> - **Cost Norms** — typically **5–10% of total project cost** reserved for follow-up activities
> - **Maintenance Schedule** — **pre-monsoon (April-May) and post-monsoon (Oct-Nov)** inspections
> - **Impact Indicators** — measured at **baseline, mid-term, and end-line** stages
> - **Community Contribution** — minimum **15–25% of maintenance cost** to be borne by the community for ownership

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Follow-up Practices (Structured Logic)

The follow-up phase of watershed management is built on **five interconnected pillars**. Understanding each pillar is essential for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

### Pillar 1: Physical Monitoring of Structures
- **Why?** Structures like check dams, percolation tanks, and gully plugs degrade over time due to siltation, scour, breaching, and weathering.
- **How?** Periodic structural inspection is conducted at **pre-monsoon** and **post-monsoon** periods.
- **What to monitor?**
  - Silt deposition level
  - Structural cracks and seepage
  - Vegetation cover on embankments
  - Breaches or partial failures
  - Functionality of spillways and weirs

### Pillar 2: Hydrological & Agronomic Monitoring
- **Why?** To verify whether the watershed interventions are actually improving groundwater, soil moisture, and crop productivity.
- **How?** Comparative analysis of **pre-project (baseline)** and **post-project** hydrological and crop data.
- **Key Parameters:**
  - Groundwater table depth
  - Soil moisture content
  - Crop yield (quintals/hectare)
  - Runoff coefficient
  - Sediment yield (tonnes/ha/year)

### Pillar 3: Institutional & Community Follow-up
- **Why?** Without active community ownership, structures become "orphaned" within 3-5 years.
- **How?** Empowering the **Watershed Committee (WC)**, **User Groups (UGs)**, and **Self-Help Groups (SHGs)**.
- **Activities:**
  - Training programmes (capacity building)
  - Exposure visits
  - Formation of **Watershed Development Fund (WDF)** — typically 5% of project cost
  - Regular **Watershed Committee meetings**
  - Conflict resolution mechanisms

### Pillar 4: Impact Assessment & Evaluation
- **Why?** To justify the investment, learn lessons, and replicate successes.
- **How?** Using **Participatory Rural Appraisal (PRA)**, **Remote Sensing & GIS**, and **statistical indicators**.
- **Levels of Evaluation:**
  1. **Process Evaluation** — Are activities being done as planned?
  2. **Output Evaluation** — Are outputs (structures, plantations) achieved?
  3. **Outcome Evaluation** — Are benefits (yield, groundwater, income) realized?
  4. **Impact Evaluation** — Long-term transformative change

### Pillar 5: Adaptive Management & Sustainability
- **Why?** Climate variability, socio-economic shifts, and new challenges require continuous adaptation.
- **How?** Feedback loops from monitoring → analysis → revised management interventions.
- **Key Strategies:**
  - Crop diversification based on water availability
  - Introduction of drought-resistant varieties
  - Conjunctive use of surface and groundwater
  - Integration with **MGNREGA** for maintenance funding
  - Convergence with **agriculture, horticulture, animal husbandry, and fisheries** departments

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S. No.** | **Parameter / Concept** | **Formula / Standard** | **Units / Typical Value** | **Application in Follow-up** |
|:----------:|:------------------------|:-----------------------|:--------------------------|:-----------------------------|
| 1 | **Siltation Rate of Check Dam** | $SR = \dfrac{V_{silt}}{A_c \cdot t}$ | m³/year | Estimating desilting frequency |
| 2 | **Runoff Coefficient Change** | $C_{post} = \dfrac{Q_{post}}{P \cdot A}$ | dimensionless (0.1–0.6) | Comparing pre/post-project runoff |
| 3 | **Sediment Yield Reduction** | $SYR = \dfrac{SY_{before} - SY_{after}}{SY_{before}} \times 100$ | % | Assessing erosion control effectiveness |
| 4 | **Groundwater Table Fluctuation** | $\Delta GW = GW_{post} - GW_{pre}$ | meters (m) | Hydrological impact indicator |
| 5 | **Crop Yield Increase Index** | $CYI = \dfrac{Y_{post} - Y_{pre}}{Y_{pre}} \times 100$ | % | Economic impact indicator |
| 6 | **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** | $BCR = \dfrac{\sum B_t / (1+r)^t}{\sum C_t / (1+r)^t}$ | dimensionless (>1 viable) | Economic sustainability check |
| 7 | **Community Contribution Ratio** | $CCR = \dfrac{C_{community}}{C_{total}} \times 100$ | % (target: 15–25%) | Ownership assessment |
| 8 | **Maintenance Cost Allocation** | $M_{annual} = 0.05 \text{ to } 0.10 \times C_{project}$ | ₹/year | Budget for follow-up phase |
| 9 | **Soil Moisture Index** | $SMI = \dfrac{\theta_{field}}{\theta_{sat}} \times 100$ | % | Agronomic monitoring |
| 10 | **Sustainability Index (SI)** | $SI = w_1 E + w_2 S + w_3 I + w_4 F$ | 0–1 (weighted score) | Composite sustainability scoring |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Glossary:**
> - $V_{silt}$ = Volume of silt deposited, $A_c$ = Catchment area, $t$ = time period
> - $Q$ = Runoff volume, $P$ = Precipitation, $A$ = Area
> - $SY$ = Sediment yield, $Y$ = Yield, $B_t$ / $C_t$ = Benefits/Costs at time $t$, $r$ = discount rate
> - $E, S, I, F$ = Environmental, Social, Institutional, Financial sub-indices
> - $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ = Respective weights (sum to 1)

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Follow-up practices are the **operational backbone** of every major watershed programme in India, including:

- **Integrated Watershed Management Programme (IWMP)** — now merged into **PMKSY-WDC (Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana — Watershed Development Component)**
- **MGNREGA convergence** for maintenance of watershed assets
- **Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act** for funding post-project repairs
- **National Watershed Atlas of India** — uses follow-up data for nationwide impact mapping
- **Kerala State Watershed Development Programmes** under the **Kerala State Land Use Board (KSLUB)** and **Kerala Agricultural University (KAU)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Industry Connection:** Follow-up practices link watershed engineering to **SDG 6 (Clean Water)**, **SDG 13 (Climate Action)**, and **SDG 15 (Life on Land)** — students should reference these for higher-order answers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sediment Yield Reduction (SYR) — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

The **Sediment Yield Reduction** is the single most important indicator in watershed follow-up to assess whether soil conservation measures are working. Let us derive its evaluation logic in full detail.

### Given:
- $SY_{before}$ = Sediment yield before watershed intervention (tonnes/ha/year)
- $SY_{after}$ = Sediment yield measured during follow-up phase (tonnes/ha/year)

### Conceptual Foundation:
Sediment yield is the amount of soil eroded and transported out of a defined catchment area per unit time. A successful watershed programme **reduces** this value through structures like check dams (which trap silt), gully plugs, and vegetation cover.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Define the absolute reduction in sediment yield.

$$
\Delta SY = SY_{before} - SY_{after}
$$

This represents the *physical quantity* of sediment retained within the watershed (or prevented from leaving it) due to conservation structures.

**Step 2:** To express this as a **dimensionless percentage** (which is universally comparable across watersheds of different sizes), we normalize by the baseline value.

$$
SYR = \dfrac{\Delta SY}{SY_{before}} = \dfrac{SY_{before} - SY_{after}}{SY_{before}}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to percentage form by multiplying by 100.

$$
SYR = \dfrac{SY_{before} - SY_{after}}{SY_{before}} \times 100 \quad (\%)
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation of the result.
- If $SYR > 50\%$ → **Excellent** watershed performance
- If $30\% \le SYR \le 50\%$ → **Good** performance
- If $10\% \le SYR < 30\%$ → **Moderate** performance
- If $SYR < 10\%$ → **Poor** — indicates failure of conservation measures

**Step 5:** Example numerical evaluation.

*Suppose* $SY_{before} = 12$ tonnes/ha/year and $SY_{after} = 5$ tonnes/ha/year.

$$
\Delta SY = 12 - 5 = 7 \text{ tonnes/ha/year}
$$

$$
SYR = \dfrac{7}{12} \times 100 = 58.33\%
$$

This indicates an **excellent** reduction — the watershed programme is performing well during the follow-up phase.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sustainability Index (SI) — Composite Scoring Logic

### Given:
- $E$ = Environmental sub-index (0 to 1)
- $S$ = Social sub-index (0 to 1)
- $I$ = Institutional sub-index (0 to 1)
- $F$ = Financial sub-index (0 to 1)
- $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ = Weights assigned to each sub-index, such that $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$

### Conceptual Foundation:
A watershed can be environmentally sound but socially rejected, or financially viable but environmentally damaging. The **Sustainability Index (SI)** aggregates these dimensions into a single comparable score.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Express each sub-index as a normalized value between 0 and 1.

$$
E, S, I, F \in [0, 1]
$$

**Step 2:** Apply weighted linear aggregation.

$$
SI = w_1 E + w_2 S + w_3 I + w_4 F
$$

**Step 3:** Normalization check.

$$
0 \le SI \le 1
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation thresholds.

$$
\text{Interpretation} = 
\begin{cases} 
\text{Highly Sustainable} & \text{if } SI \ge 0.75 \\
\text{Moderately Sustainable} & \text{if } 0.50 \le SI < 0.75 \\
\text{Marginally Sustainable} & \text{if } 0.25 \le SI < 0.50 \\
\text{Not Sustainable} & \text{if } SI < 0.25
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5:** Numerical example.

*Let* $E = 0.80, S = 0.70, I = 0.65, F = 0.55$ and $w_1 = 0.35, w_2 = 0.25, w_3 = 0.20, w_4 = 0.20$.

$$
SI = (0.35)(0.80) + (0.25)(0.70) + (0.20)(0.65) + (0.20)(0.55)
$$

$$
SI = 0.280 + 0.175 + 0.130 + 0.110 = 0.695
$$

This watershed is **Moderately Sustainable** — institutional and financial sub-indices need strengthening during the follow-up phase.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) for Follow-up Phase

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1:** Define the net present value (NPV) form.

$$
NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{B_t - C_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Step 2:** Define present value of benefits and costs separately.

$$
PV_B = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{B_t}{(1+r)^t}, \quad PV_C = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**Step 3:** Form the BCR.

$$
BCR = \dfrac{PV_B}{PV_C}
$$

**Step 4:** Decision rule.
- $BCR > 1$ → Project economically viable
- $BCR = 1$ → Break-even
- $BCR < 1$ → Not economically viable — requires restructuring

**Step 5:** Typical KTU-style calculation (assume simplified 5-year follow-up with uniform values).

*Let* $B = 5,00,000$ ₹/year, $C = 3,00,000$ ₹/year, $r = 8\% = 0.08$, $n = 5$ years.

Present value annuity factor:

$$
PVA = \dfrac{1 - (1+0.08)^{-5}}{0.08} = \dfrac{1 - 0.6806}{0.08} = 3.9925
$$

Then:

$$
PV_B = 5,00,000 \times 3.9925 = 19,96,250 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
PV_C = 3,00,000 \times 3.9925 = 11,97,750 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
BCR = \dfrac{19,96,250}{11,97,750} = 1.667
$$

Since $BCR = 1.667 > 1$, the follow-up investments are **economically justified**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Follow-up Practices in Watershed Management.
**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Follow-up practices in watershed management refer to the **systematic post-implementation activities** undertaken after the completion of watershed development works to ensure **sustainability, functional efficiency, and long-term benefits**. They include:
1. **Physical monitoring** of watershed structures (check dams, percolation tanks, gully plugs).
2. **Hydrological and agronomic monitoring** (groundwater levels, soil moisture, crop yield).
3. **Institutional follow-up** through Watershed Committees, SHGs, and capacity building.
4. **Impact assessment** using baseline comparisons, PRA, and remote sensing.
5. **Adaptive management** and **maintenance planning** for long-term sustainability.

> **Marking Scheme:** Definition (1M) + Any 4 key components (2M)

---

### Question 2: List the Five Pillars of Follow-up Practices.
**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. Physical Monitoring of Structures
2. Hydrological and Agronomic Monitoring
3. Institutional and Community Follow-up
4. Impact Assessment and Evaluation
5. Adaptive Management and Sustainability

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 Mark for naming, 2 Marks for brief explanation of any 2 pillars.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is **mandatory** — students must answer **ONE full question from each choice pair**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss in detail the components of post-project physical monitoring of watershed structures. What parameters are monitored and at what frequency?**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

Post-project physical monitoring is the **first pillar** of follow-up practices. It ensures that the structures built during the implementation phase continue to function as designed.

**Structures Monitored:**
1. Check dams and mini percolation tanks
2. Farm ponds
3. Gully plugs and contour bunds
4. Stone walls and vegetative barriers
5. Spillways and weirs

**Parameters Monitored (with frequency):**
- **Silt deposition level** — measured **quarterly** using graduated rods
- **Structural cracks and seepage** — visual inspection **pre-monsoon (April–May)** and **post-monsoon (Oct–Nov)**
- **Breach or partial failure** — emergency inspection after extreme rainfall events
- **Vegetation cover on embankments** — assessed **bi-annually**
- **Spillway functionality** — checked **pre-monsoon** for obstructions

**Maintenance Schedule:**
- **Pre-monsoon repairs:** April–May (desilting, crack sealing)
- **Post-monsoon repairs:** October–November (breach repair, structural reinforcement)

**Documentation:** Maintained in a **Watershed Asset Register** with photographic evidence and GPS coordinates.

> **Mark Split:** Structures (1.5M) + Parameters (2.5M) + Frequency (1.5M) + Maintenance schedule + documentation (1.5M)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Sustainability Index (SI) used in watershed follow-up. How is it computed and interpreted?**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

The **Sustainability Index (SI)** is a composite scoring metric used in the follow-up phase to assess whether a watershed programme is sustainable across **four dimensions**.

**Four Sub-Indices:**
1. **Environmental (E):** Groundwater recharge, soil conservation, biodiversity
2. **Social (S):** Community participation, equity, gender inclusion
3. **Institutional (I):** Functioning of WC, SHGs, UGs
4. **Financial (F):** WDF corpus, O&M funding, convergence funds

**Formula:**

$$
SI = w_1 E + w_2 S + w_3 I + w_4 F
$$

where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$ and $E, S, I, F \in [0, 1]$.

**Computation Steps:**
1. Normalize each sub-index to a 0–1 scale using indicator scores.
2. Assign weights based on local priorities (e.g., $w_1 = 0.35$ for environment, $w_2 = 0.25$ for social, $w_3 = 0.20$ for institutional, $w_4 = 0.20$ for financial).
3. Compute the weighted sum.

**Interpretation:**
- $SI \ge 0.75$ → Highly Sustainable
- $0.50 \le SI < 0.75$ → Moderately Sustainable
- $0.25 \le SI < 0.50$ → Marginally Sustainable
- $SI < 0.25$ → Not Sustainable

**Application:** Used in annual sustainability audits and in convergence planning with MGNREGA, PMKSY-WDC, and state watershed programmes.

> **Mark Split:** Concept (1.5M) + Sub-indices (2M) + Formula + computation steps (2M) + Interpretation (1.5M)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the role of community participation and institutional mechanisms in the follow-up phase of watershed development.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

Community participation is the **backbone** of follow-up practices. Without active local ownership, even the best-engineered structures become non-functional within 5–7 years.

**Institutional Mechanisms:**
1. **Watershed Committee (WC):** Apex body at the watershed level, comprising elected representatives, SHG leaders, and government officials. Conducts **monthly review meetings**.
2. **User Groups (UGs):** Sub-groups responsible for specific assets (e.g., a particular check dam or farm pond).
3. **Self-Help Groups (SHGs):** Women's groups managing micro-enterprises, savings, and livelihood activities.
4. **Watershed Development Team (WDT):** Technical team providing expert guidance.
5. **Project Implementing Agency (PIA):** Line department or NGO coordinating follow-up.

**Capacity Building Activities:**
- **Training programmes** on maintenance of structures
- **Exposure visits** to successful watersheds
- **Skill development** in agronomic practices, water budgeting
- **Leadership training** for WC office bearers

**Watershed Development Fund (WDF):**
- Corpus: **5% of total project cost**
- Used for: Maintenance, repair, and community incentives
- Managed by: WC with bank account and audit

**Community Contribution Norm:**
- Target: **15–25% of maintenance cost** borne by community
- This ensures **ownership** and reduces free-rider problems

> **Mark Split:** Institutional mechanisms (2.5M) + Capacity building (1.5M) + WDF + community contribution (1.5M) + Why it matters (1.5M)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the process of impact assessment during the follow-up phase. What indicators are used and what tools are employed?**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

Impact assessment is the **fourth pillar** of follow-up practices, validating whether the watershed programme has achieved its intended outcomes.

**Levels of Impact Assessment:**
1. **Process Evaluation** — Were activities carried out as planned?
2. **Output Evaluation** — Were deliverables (structures, plantations) achieved?
3. **Outcome Evaluation** — Are intermediate benefits (yield, groundwater) realized?
4. **Impact Evaluation** — What is the long-term transformative change?

**Key Impact Indicators:**
- **Hydrological:** Groundwater table depth, runoff coefficient, base flow
- **Agronomic:** Crop yield, cropping intensity, crop diversity index
- **Soil:** Soil organic carbon, soil moisture, erosion rate
- **Economic:** Net farm income, BCR, employment days generated
- **Social:** Household coverage, gender participation, migration reduction
- **Ecological:** Vegetation cover, species diversity, carbon sequestration

**Tools and Methods:**
1. **Participatory Rural Appraisal (PRA):** Focus group discussions, transect walks, resource mapping
2. **Remote Sensing and GIS:** NDVI analysis, land use/land cover change detection
3. **Field Measurements:** Piezometers, rain gauges, yield sampling
4. **Household Surveys:** Structured questionnaires at baseline, mid-term, and end-line
5. **Statistical Analysis:** t-tests, ANOVA, regression models

**Reporting:** Findings are compiled in an **Impact Assessment Report** and shared with WC, PIA, and funding agencies.

> **Mark Split:** Levels of evaluation (1.5M) + Indicators (2.5M) + Tools/methods (2M) + Reporting (1M)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Follow-up Practices Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "Follow-up" with "Implementation"** — Many students write about construction activities. Follow-up is **POST-implementation**.
> 2. **Ignoring the 5% Watershed Development Fund (WDF) norm** — This is a **favourite KTU question** and carries direct marks.
> 3. **Not mentioning community contribution percentage (15–25%)** — Examiners specifically look for ownership indicators.
> 4. **Forgetting the four-tier evaluation** (Process → Output → Outcome → Impact) — Listing only one tier loses 1-2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the formula in Sustainability Index questions** — Even if conceptual answer is correct, **formula is mandatory** for full marks.
> 6. **Not specifying monitoring frequency** (pre-monsoon, post-monsoon) — Examiners expect **time-specific** answers.
> 7. **Omitting convergence with MGNREGA / PMKSY-WDC** — This shows awareness of current national programmes and earns extra credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Follow-up Practices:**

- **Definition:** Follow-up practices = post-implementation activities ensuring sustainability of watershed interventions.
- **Five Pillars:** Physical Monitoring | Hydrological Monitoring | Institutional Follow-up | Impact Assessment | Adaptive Management.
- **Watershed Development Fund (WDF):** 5% of project cost; managed by WC; used for maintenance.
- **Community Contribution Norm:** 15–25% of maintenance cost for ownership.
- **Monitoring Frequency:** Pre-monsoon (Apr–May) and post-monsoon (Oct–Nov) inspections + quarterly data collection.
- **Maintenance Budget:** 5–10% of total project cost annually.
- **Impact Assessment Levels:** Process → Output → Outcome → Impact (4-tier).
- **Key Indicators:** Groundwater depth, soil moisture, crop yield, runoff coefficient, sediment yield, BCR, household income.
- **Tools:** PRA, Remote Sensing & GIS, household surveys, field measurements, statistical analysis.
- **Sustainability Index Formula:** $SI = w_1 E + w_2 S + w_3 I + w_4 F$; range 0–1.
- **SI Thresholds:** $\ge 0.75$ Highly | 0.50–0.75 Moderate | 0.25–0.50 Marginal | $< 0.25$ Not Sustainable.
- **BCR Rule:** $> 1$ viable, $= 1$ break-even, $< 1$ not viable.
- **Convergence:** MGNREGA for maintenance funding; PMKSY-WDC for programme alignment.
- **Institutional Bodies:** WC (apex) | UGs (asset-specific) | SHGs (women's livelihood) | WDT (technical) | PIA (coordination).
- **Common KPIs:** SYR (sediment yield reduction), CYI (crop yield increase), CCR (community contribution ratio).
- **Golden Rule:** *"No follow-up = guaranteed failure of watershed structures within 5–7 years."*
- **SDGs Linked:** SDG 6 (Water) | SDG 13 (Climate) | SDG 15 (Land).
- **Key Indian Programmes:** IWMP → PMKSY-WDC, MGNREGA convergence, KSLUB (Kerala).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
