<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the term "Abstract of Estimate" and state its significance in a watershed development project.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Abstract of Estimate** is a concise tabular financial document that summarizes the cost of all items of work in a project by listing each item with its quantity, unit, rate per unit, and total amount. It is derived from the **Detailed Estimate** and serves as the **basis for administrative approval, budget sanction, and tender processing**. In watershed projects, it enables funding agencies like **MoRD, NABARD, and state watershed cells** to release funds in installments and conduct financial audits.

*(Mark Distribution: 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for significance)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Sample Paper]**
**List any six standard items typically included in the Abstract of Estimate of a watershed project.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The six items typically included are:

1. Earthwork excavation (for farm ponds, percolation tanks)
2. Masonry / concrete works (check dams, spillways)
3. Contour bunding and terracing
4. Vegetative plantation of saplings
5. Grass seeding and bio-engineering works
6. Drainage and gabion structures

*(Mark Distribution: 0.5 marks each × 6 = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]*

**Q3(a)** Prepare the **Abstract of Estimate** for the following watershed interventions and calculate the **Grand Total Cost** including 8% contingencies, 1.5% WCE, and 18% GST.

| Item | Description | Quantity | Unit | Rate (₹) |
|:----:|:------------|---------:|:----:|:--------:|
| 1 | Earthwork in excavation for percolation tank | 800 | cum | 195 |
| 2 | RR masonry for check dam | 120 | cum | 3,400 |
| 3 | M20 concrete for drop structure | 45 | cum | 6,200 |
| 4 | Vetiver plantation on bunds | 3,500 | Rmt | 28 |
| 5 | Agroforestry saplings (3-year-old) | 1,500 | Nos | 75 |

**[7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

- $A_1 = 800 \times 195 = 1{,}56{,}000 \, \text{₹}$
- $A_2 = 120 \times 3{,}400 = 4{,}08{,}000 \, \text{₹}$
- $A_3 = 45 \times 6{,}200 = 2{,}79{,}000 \, \text{₹}$
- $A_4 = 3{,}500 \times 28 = 98{,}000 \, \text{₹}$
- $A_5 = 1{,}500 \times 75 = 1{,}12{,}500 \, \text{₹}$

$$
\text{Subtotal} = 1{,}56{,}000 + 4{,}08{,}000 + 2{,}79{,}000 + 98{,}000 + 1{,}12{,}500 = 10{,}53{,}500 \, \text{₹}
$$

**Contingency @ 8%:**
$$
C = 10{,}53{,}500 \times 0.08 = 84{,}280 \, \text{₹}
$$

**WCE @ 1.5%:**
$$
W = 10{,}53{,}500 \times 0.015 = 15{,}802.50 \, \text{₹}
$$

**GST @ 18% on (Subtotal + C + W):**
$$
\text{Taxable Base} = 10{,}53{,}500 + 84{,}280 + 15{,}802.50 = 11{,}53{,}582.50 \, \text{₹}
$$

$$
\text{GST} = 11{,}53{,}582.50 \times 0.18 = 2{,}07{,}644.85 \, \text{₹}
$$

**Grand Total:**
$$
\boxed{\text{Grand Total} = 11{,}53{,}582.50 + 2{,}07{,}644.85 = 13{,}61{,}227.35 \, \text{₹}}
$$

*(Full marks awarded for clear tabular presentation, correct arithmetic, and proper sequence of additions.)*

---

**Q3(b)** Explain the **importance of Schedule of Rates (SOR)** in preparing the Abstract of Estimate. Discuss the role of **contingency provisions** in watershed projects.

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Role of SOR (3.5 marks):**
- The **Schedule of Rates** is a document published annually by PWD / MoRD / State Departments, listing standardized unit rates for all items of construction work.
- It ensures **uniformity, transparency, and fairness** in cost estimation across regions.
- It eliminates the need for individual rate analysis for every project, saving time and reducing disputes.
- SOR rates include **material cost + labour cost + contractor profit + overheads**, all bundled into a single unit rate.
- Different SOR editions (e.g., CPWD SOR 2023, Kerala PWD DSR 2024) apply depending on jurisdiction.

**Role of Contingency (3.5 marks):**
- **Contingency** is an additional provision (typically **3%–15%**) to cover **unforeseen expenses** during execution.
- In watershed projects, contingency is essential because of:
  - Unpredictable site conditions (rock, water table, soil variability)
  - Weather disruptions (monsoon delays)
  - Market price fluctuations (cement, steel, fuel)
  - Design modifications during construction
- Without adequate contingency, projects face **fund shortage and incomplete execution**, defeating watershed objectives.
- Contingency is **non-divertible** in most funding schemes and requires proper audit documentation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**Q4(a)** A watershed project covers **75 hectares** and the Abstract of Estimate shows a Grand Total Cost of **₹32,50,000**. Calculate the **per-hectare cost** and compare it with the standard benchmark range prescribed by MoRD for watershed treatment projects. Comment on whether the project is **economically justified**.

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

$$
\text{Cost per hectare} = \frac{32{,}50{,}000}{75} = 43{,}333.33 \, \text{₹/ha}
$$

**Standard MoRD Benchmark:** ₹15,000 to ₹60,000 per hectare for watershed treatment (depending on terrain and intervention density).

**Comparison:** ₹43,333.33/ha falls **within the prescribed range**, indicating the project is **economically justified and within normative cost limits**.

**Comment:** The project demonstrates **cost-efficiency** and aligns with national watershed development guidelines.

---

**Q4(b)** Differentiate between **Detailed Estimate** and **Abstract of Estimate**. Prepare a sample Abstract of Estimate template with column headings as used in PWD / Watershed Development projects.

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form):**

| Parameter | Detailed Estimate | Abstract of Estimate |
|:----------|:------------------|:---------------------|
| Purpose | Computes quantity of each item with full dimensions | Summarizes cost of all items |
| Level of detail | Highly detailed (length, breadth, height) | Consolidated and summarized |
| Source of rates | Not required here | Requires SOR rates |
| Output | Quantity only | Quantity + Rate + Amount |
| Stage | First stage of estimation | Final stage of estimation |

**Sample Template Column Headings:**

| Sl. No. | Description of Item | Quantity | Unit | Rate (₹ per unit) | Amount (₹) |
|:-------:|:--------------------|---------:|:----:|:-----------------:|-----------:|
| 1 | — | — | — | — | — |
| 2 | — | — | — | — | — |
| ... | — | — | — | — | — |
| **Total** | | | | | **Σ Amount** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong unit usage:** Students often write "cubic meter" instead of standard abbreviation **"cum"** or confuse "sqm" with "Rmt." Always use standard PWD notation.
> 2. **GST calculation base:** A very common error is computing GST on **only the subtotal** instead of on **Subtotal + Contingency + WCE**. KTU examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Missing contingency percentage:** Students may forget to add contingencies. Always remember: **no estimate is complete without contingency provision.**
> 4. **Arithmetic mistakes:** Carry-over errors in multiplication are penalized heavily. Use proper **digit grouping (Indian system: 1,00,000)** as shown in model solutions.
> 5. **No SOR reference cited:** Examiners expect you to mention that rates are taken from the **prevailing Schedule of Rates** of the relevant year.
> 6. **Forgetting per-hectare benchmark:** For watershed projects, always compute and comment on **per-hectare cost** as a validation metric.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Abstract of Estimate = Tabular summary of all item-wise costs leading to total project cost.
- **Core Formula:** $\text{Amount} = \text{Quantity} \times \text{Rate}$.
- **Grand Total Formula:** $\text{GT} = \text{Subtotal} + \text{Contingency} + \text{WCE} + \text{GST}$.
- **Standard Contingency Range:** **3%–5%** (institutional) or **10%–15%** (rural/watershed).
- **WCE Provision:** **1.5%–2.5%** of subtotal.
- **GST Rate:** Typically **18%** on taxable base.
- **Source of Rates:** Schedule of Rates (SOR) of PWD / MoRD / DSR.
- **Mandatory Columns:** Sl. No., Particulars, Quantity, Unit, Rate, Amount.
- **Benchmark Metric:** ₹15,000–₹60,000 per hectare for watershed projects.
- **Governing Authorities:** MoRD, NABARD, IWMP, MGNREGA convergence, State Watershed Cells.
- **Purpose:** Administrative approval, technical sanction, tendering, audit, fund release.
- **Always cross-verify** every item amount against the **Detailed Estimate / Measurement Sheet** before final submission.
- **GST computation base = Subtotal + Contingency + WCE** (NOT just subtotal — this is the most common error).
- **Document traceability:** Every rate must be referenced to a specific SOR edition and page number.
- **Certification:** The Abstract of Estimate must bear the **signature, seal, and registration number** of the certifying engineer.
- **Practical application:** Used in check dams, farm ponds, percolation tanks, contour bunds, plantations, and all watershed structures.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning and Formulation of Project Proposal for Watershed Management Programme Including Cost
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET741 - WATERSHED DEVELOPMENT AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Planning and Formulation of Project Proposal for Watershed Management Programme Including Cost

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Watershed Management Project Proposal** is a systematically prepared, scientifically justified, and financially viable document that outlines the **planning, formulation, implementation strategy, cost structure, monitoring framework, and expected socio-economic and environmental outcomes** of interventions proposed within a defined hydrological unit (watershed). It serves as the foundational reference document for obtaining administrative approval, financial sanction, and stakeholder consensus before the commencement of any watershed development programme.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the proposal is built upon the triad of **engineering feasibility**, **hydrological viability**, and **socio-economic sustainability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology Distinction (High Yield for KTU Exams)**
> - **Project Proposal:** A written request seeking approval/funding (Administrative document).
> - **Project Plan:** An internal roadmap detailing *how* the work will be executed (Operational document).
> - **Detailed Project Report (DPR):** The comprehensive technical, financial, and management blueprint submitted to funding agencies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Watershed as a "Patient"

Imagine a watershed as a **sick patient** visiting a hospital. Before any treatment begins, the doctor must:

1. **Diagnose** the ailment → *Baseline Survey & Participatory Rural Appraisal (PRA)*.
2. **Prescribe a treatment plan** → *Treatment Plan with Soil & Water Conservation measures*.
3. **Estimate the cost of treatment** → *Budget Preparation (Capital + Recurring)*.
4. **Predict the recovery period & outcomes** → *Expected Outputs, Outcomes & Impact*.
5. **Schedule follow-up visits** → *Monitoring, Evaluation & Learning (MEL) framework*.

Just as no doctor prescribes medicine without a diagnosis and a cost estimate, no watershed programme can be sanctioned without a well-formulated **Project Proposal**. The proposal is essentially the "treatment card" of the watershed.

## 1.3 Why is Planning & Formulation Critical?

> [!NOTE]
> A watershed without a proper project proposal is like a building without a blueprint — **it will eventually collapse under its own structural, financial, or social contradictions.**

The proposal ensures:

- **Logical sequencing** of interventions (treatment → protection → production → livelihood).
- **Optimal allocation of scarce financial resources** across competing needs.
- **Transparency and accountability** to funding agencies, government, and communities.
- **Measurable benchmarks** for monitoring performance.
- **Risk identification and mitigation** strategies.

## 1.4 Guiding Principles for Watershed Project Formulation

The following principles, as adopted by national agencies (e.g., Ministry of Jal Shakti, NABARD, IWMP), govern the formulation of watershed proposals in India:

- **Participatory Approach:** Bottom-up planning involving Gram Sabhas, Self-Help Groups (SHGs), and User Associations.
- **Integrated Resource Management:** Treating soil, water, vegetation, and human resources as a single system.
- **Sustainability & Convergence:** Linking with MGNREGA, PMKSY, and state-level schemes.
- **Gender & Equity Focus:** Ensuring women's representation (minimum **33%** as per national norms).
- **Climate Resilience:** Incorporating climate-smart interventions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phases of Watershed Project Planning

The planning of a watershed management programme follows a **structured four-phase model**:

### Phase 1 — Pre-Feasibility Stage (Reconnaissance)

- Identification of the watershed boundary using topo-sheets and satellite imagery.
- Preliminary field visits.
- Estimation of approximate catchment area ($A$ in km² or hectares).
- Initial stakeholder consultation.

### Phase 2 — Feasibility / Detailed Survey Stage

This is the most data-intensive phase. Activities include:

- **Topographical Survey** using Total Station / DGPS for slope, contour, and drainage network mapping.
- **Soil Survey** for texture, depth, erodibility, and infiltration capacity.
- **Hydrological Assessment** for rainfall, runoff, and groundwater.
- **Socio-Economic Survey** through PRA, focus group discussions, and household questionnaires.
- **Land Use Land Cover (LULC)** mapping using GIS tools.

### Phase 3 — Project Formulation Stage

- Synthesis of survey data into a logical intervention matrix.
- Selection of appropriate **engineering** (e.g., check dams, percolation tanks, terraces) and **biological** (e.g., afforestation, pasture development) measures.
- Cost estimation and financial structuring.
- Preparation of the project document (DPR).

### Phase 4 — Appraisal, Approval & Implementation Stage

- Submission to funding agency.
- Technical and financial appraisal.
- Sanction and release of funds (usually in **installments** linked to milestones).

## 2.2 Standard Structure of a Watershed Project Proposal

A KTU-aligned project proposal should contain the following **nine mandatory sections**:

| Section No. | Section Title | Purpose |
|:-:|:--|:--|
| 1 | **Executive Summary** | One-page snapshot of the proposal. |
| 2 | **Introduction & Background** | Context, location, justification. |
| 3 | **Baseline Profile** | Physical, biological, socio-economic data. |
| 4 | **Problem Definition & SWOT** | Identifies gaps, strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, threats. |
| 5 | **Objectives & Expected Outcomes** | SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) goals. |
| 6 | **Proposed Interventions** | Engineering + biological + livelihood activities. |
| 7 | **Cost Estimates & Budget** | Item-wise capital and recurring cost. |
| 8 | **Implementation Mechanism** | Institutional arrangements, roles, responsibilities, timelines (Gantt chart). |
| 9 | **Monitoring & Evaluation (M&E)** | Indicators, mid-term review, impact assessment. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners frequently ask students to "list the components of a watershed project proposal." Memorize the **nine sections** above in order.

## 2.3 Components of Project Cost (High Yield for KTU Board Exams)

The total project cost ($C_{\text{total}}$) of a watershed programme is the sum of **four major cost heads**:

$$C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{DC}} + C_{\text{RC}} + C_{\text{MC}} + C_{\text{Misc}}$$

Where each component is defined below:

| Symbol | Cost Head | Description | Typical % of Total |
|:-:|:--|:--|:-:|
| $C_{\text{DC}}$ | **Direct Capital Cost** | Earthwork, structures, saplings, machinery, materials. | **60–70%** |
| $C_{\text{RC}}$ | **Recurring Cost** | Wages during maintenance years, training, community mobilization. | **15–20%** |
| $C_{\text{MC}}$ | **Monitoring & Evaluation Cost** | Baseline surveys, M&E consultants, impact studies. | **5–7%** |
| $C_{\text{Misc}}$ | **Miscellaneous / Contingency** | Administrative overhead, unforeseen expenses. | **3–5%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Per Hectare Norm (NABARD / IWMP):** The standard benchmark project cost under integrated watershed programmes is approximately **₹12,000 to ₹15,000 per hectare** (as per recent Government of India guidelines). Always state the unit cost per hectare clearly in your proposal.

## 2.4 Economic Evaluation Indicators — The KTU Formula Sheet

These formulas are **exam-favourite** for numerical questions worth 7–14 marks.

### 2.4.1 Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)

$$BCR = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{B_t}{(1+i)^t}}{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}}$$

- $B_t$ = Benefits in year $t$ (₹)
- $C_t$ = Costs in year $t$ (₹)
- $i$ = Discount rate (typically **12%** for Indian rural projects)
- $n$ = Project life in years

**Decision Rule:** A watershed project is considered **economically viable only if BCR ≥ 1.0** (preferably $\geq 1.5$).

### 2.4.2 Net Present Value (NPV)

$$NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{B_t - C_t}{(1+i)^t} \; - \; C_0$$

- $C_0$ = Initial capital investment (₹)
- **Decision Rule:** Accept the project if **NPV > 0**.

### 2.4.3 Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

IRR is the discount rate ($i^*$) at which **NPV becomes zero**:

$$\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{B_t - C_t}{(1+i^*)^t} = 0$$

- **Decision Rule:** Accept the project if **IRR > cost of capital (MARR)**.

### 2.4.4 Payback Period

$$T_{\text{payback}} = \frac{\text{Total Investment}}{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}$$

### 2.4.5 Annual Equivalent Cost of Check Dam (Common Numerical)

For a small check dam with construction cost $C$, useful life $n$ years, salvage value $S$, and annual maintenance cost $M$, the annualized cost is:

$$\text{Annualized Cost} = C \cdot \text{CRF}(i, n) + M - S \cdot \text{SFF}(i, n)$$

Where the **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** and **Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)** are:

$$\text{CRF}(i, n) = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

$$\text{SFF}(i, n) = \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

## 2.5 Utility in Engineering Practice

These economic indicators are not merely academic — they are **legally mandated** by agencies like NABARD, MoRD, and the World Bank before any watershed project is sanctioned. A project with **BCR < 1.0** will be rejected outright during technical appraisal.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example — Watershed Project Economic Appraisal

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example Statement (14-Mark KTU Pattern):**
> A watershed development project covering **500 hectares** is proposed with a total capital investment of **₹75,00,000** and an annual recurring cost of **₹6,00,000**. The expected annual direct benefits are **₹18,00,000** and indirect benefits are **₹5,00,000**. The project life is **20 years**, the salvage value at end of life is **₹5,00,000**, and the discount rate is **12%** per annum. Evaluate the project using BCR and NPV. Comment on its viability.

### Step 1 — Identify and Tabulate Cash Flows

We list all cash flows clearly before substitution to avoid algebraic errors.

| Year ($t$) | Cost $C_t$ (₹) | Direct Benefit $B_{d,t}$ (₹) | Indirect Benefit $B_{i,t}$ (₹) | Net Benefit $B_t$ (₹) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0 | 75,00,000 | 0 | 0 | -75,00,000 |
| 1 to 20 | 6,00,000 | 18,00,000 | 5,00,000 | 17,00,000 |
| 20 (end) | -5,00,000 (salvage inflow) | — | — | +5,00,000 |

The **annual net benefit** during operation years is:

$$\text{Net Annual Benefit} = (18{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000) - 6{,}00{,}000 = 17{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹/year}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Discount Factor

The discount factor for each year $t$ at rate $i = 12\% = 0.12$ is:

$$DF_t = \frac{1}{(1 + 0.12)^t}$$

For a uniform series of $n = 20$ years, the **Present Worth Factor (PWF)** for an annuity is:

$$\text{PWF}(i, n) = \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$$

We now substitute $i = 0.12$ and $n = 20$:

$$\text{PWF} = \frac{(1.12)^{20} - 1}{0.12 \cdot (1.12)^{20}}$$

First, compute $(1.12)^{20}$. Using standard compound factor tables or a calculator:

$$(1.12)^{20} = 9.6463 \; (\text{rounded to 4 decimals})$$

Then:

$$\text{PWF} = \frac{9.6463 - 1}{0.12 \times 9.6463} = \frac{8.6463}{1.1576} = 7.4694$$

### Step 3 — Present Worth of All Benefits

The annual net benefit of ₹17,00,000 occurs uniformly for 20 years. Therefore:

$$\text{PW of Benefits} = 17{,}00{,}000 \times 7.4694$$

Performing the multiplication carefully:

$$\text{PW of Benefits} = 17{,}00{,}000 \times 7.4694 = 12{,}69{,}79{,}800 \; \text{(approximately ₹12.70 Crore)}$$

Now we add the **Present Worth of Salvage Value** in year 20:

$$\text{PW of Salvage} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{(1.12)^{20}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{9.6463} = 51{,}834 \; \text{₹}$$

Total PW of Benefits:

$$\text{PW}_{\text{Benefits, total}} = 12{,}69{,}79{,}800 + 51{,}834 \approx 12{,}70{,}31{,}634 \; \text{₹}$$

### Step 4 — Present Worth of All Costs

The initial investment of ₹75,00,000 occurs at $t = 0$, so its PW is the same value. The recurring cost of ₹6,00,000 occurs for 20 years:

$$\text{PW of Recurring Cost} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 7.4694 = 44{,}81{,}640 \; \text{₹}$$

Total PW of Costs:

$$\text{PW}_{\text{Costs, total}} = 75{,}00{,}000 + 44{,}81{,}640 = 1{,}19{,}81{,}640 \; \text{₹}$$

### Step 5 — Compute BCR

$$BCR = \frac{\text{PW of Benefits}}{\text{PW of Costs}} = \frac{12{,}70{,}31{,}634}{1{,}19{,}81{,}640}$$

$$BCR = 10.60 \; (\text{approximately})$$

**Interpretation:** Since **BCR = 10.60 >> 1.0**, the project is **highly economically viable**. Every rupee invested generates ₹10.60 in discounted benefits.

### Step 6 — Compute NPV

$$NPV = \text{PW of Benefits} - \text{PW of Costs} = 12{,}70{,}31{,}634 - 1{,}19{,}81{,}640$$

$$NPV = 11{,}50{,}49{,}994 \; \text{₹} \; \approx \; ₹11.50 \; \text{Crore}$$

**Interpretation:** Since **NPV is strongly positive**, the project should be **accepted**.

### Step 7 — Final Examiner-Style Conclusion

> The watershed project is **strongly recommended for sanction** because both BCR (=10.60) is far greater than unity and NPV (=₹11.50 Crore) is positive at the 12% discount rate. The project demonstrates excellent economic efficiency, indicating that watershed interventions can generate substantial long-term returns through soil conservation, augmented water availability, and enhanced agricultural productivity.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Cost Estimation Framework for Common Watershed Structures

| Structure | Unit | Quantity Estimation Basis | Unit Cost Reference (₹) | Useful Life (yrs) |
|:--|:-:|:--|:-:|:-:|
| **Contour Bund** | Running meter | Length × spacing depends on slope | 80–120 / m | 10–15 |
| **Terracing** | Hectare | Area × slope factor | 25,000–40,000 / ha | 15–20 |
| **Check Dam (Small)** | Number | Based on stream order & catchment | 1.5–3.0 lakh / no. | 15–25 |
| **Percolation Tank** | Number | Based on infiltration tests | 2.5–5.0 lakh / no. | 20–30 |
| **Farm Pond** | Number | Capacity × soil type | 50,000–1.5 lakh / no. | 15–20 |
| **Afforestation** | Hectare | Spacing 3 m × 3 m → ~1100 plants/ha | 20,000–30,000 / ha | Permanent |
| **Pasture Development** | Hectare | Seed rate + fencing | 15,000–22,000 / ha | 10 |

## 3.3 Logical Derivation of Per-Hectare Normative Cost

If a watershed area is $A$ hectares, the **per-hectare cost** ($C_{ha}$) is simply:

$$C_{ha} = \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{A} \; \; (\text{₹/ha})$$

Substituting $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{DC}} + C_{\text{RC}} + C_{\text{MC}} + C_{\text{Misc}}$:

$$C_{ha} = \frac{C_{\text{DC}} + C_{\text{RC}} + C_{\text{MC}} + C_{\text{Misc}}}{A}$$

**Example:** If a ₹75 lakh proposal covers 500 ha, then:

$$C_{ha} = \frac{75{,}00{,}000}{500} = 15{,}000 \; \text{₹/ha}$$

This matches the **NABARD / IWMP normative benchmark** — a strong validation point to include in your proposal and exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Flow of Watershed Project Proposal Formulation

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the end-to-end logical sequence of project proposal formulation — from field reconnaissance to economic appraisal.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Stage 1: Reconnaissance & Pre-Feasibility"] --> nodeB["Stage 2: Detailed Field Investigations"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Stage 3: Data Analysis & Baseline Report"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Stage 4: Problem Identification & SWOT Analysis"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Stage 5: Stakeholder Consultation and PRA"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Stage 6: Selection of Engineering and Biological Interventions"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Stage 7: Quantity Survey and Cost Estimation"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Stage 8: Financial Structuring and Funding Source Mapping"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Stage 9: Economic Appraisal BCR NPV IRR"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Stage 10: Preparation of DPR and Submission"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Stage 11: Technical and Financial Appraisal by Funding Agency"]
    nodeK --> nodeL{"Decision: Approved?"}
    nodeL -- Yes --> nodeM["Stage 12: Sanction Fund Release and Implementation"]
    nodeL -- No --> nodeN["Stage 13: Revise and Resubmit Proposal"]
    nodeM --> nodeO["Stage 14: Monitoring Evaluation and Impact Assessment"]
    nodeO --> nodeP["End: Project Closure and Documentation"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Components of Project Cost

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Total Project Cost C total"] --> nodeB["Direct Capital Cost C DC 60 to 70 percent"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Recurring Cost C RC 15 to 20 percent"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Monitoring Cost C MC 5 to 7 percent"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Contingency Cost C Misc 3 to 5 percent"]

    nodeB --> nodeF["Earthwork and Structures"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["Plantation and Seedlings"]
    nodeB --> nodeH["Machinery and Equipment"]

    nodeC --> nodeI["Wages for Maintenance Years"]
    nodeC --> nodeJ["Community Training and Mobilization"]

    nodeD --> nodeK["Baseline Survey"]
    nodeD --> nodeL["Mid Term Review"]

    nodeE --> nodeM["Administrative Overhead"]
    nodeE --> nodeN["Unforeseen Expenses"]
```

## 4.3 Logical Flow — Economic Appraisal Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Start: Cash Flow Data Available"] --> nodeB["Compute PW of Benefits"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Compute PW of Costs"]
    nodeB --> nodeD{"BCR >= 1.0 ?"}
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD -- No --> nodeE["Reject Project"]
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeF{"NPV > 0 ?"}
    nodeF -- No --> nodeG["Revise Cost Structure"]
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeH{"IRR > MARR ?"}
    nodeH -- No --> nodeI["Improve Benefit Streams"]
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeJ["Accept and Sanction Project"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define a *Watershed Management Project Proposal*. List any **four** mandatory components of such a proposal.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Watershed Management Project Proposal** is a comprehensive technical and financial document that presents the planning, design, cost, implementation strategy, and expected outcomes of interventions proposed within a defined hydrological unit, prepared to obtain administrative approval and financial sanction. *(1 mark)*

The four mandatory components are: *(1 mark for naming, 1 mark for brief explanation)*

1. **Baseline Profile** — Physical, hydrological, and socio-economic data of the watershed.
2. **Problem Definition and SWOT Analysis** — Identification of degradation issues and prioritization.
3. **Proposed Interventions** — Engineering (e.g., check dams, terraces) and biological (e.g., afforestation) measures.
4. **Cost Estimates and Economic Appraisal** — Item-wise budget and BCR / NPV analysis.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between *Direct Capital Cost* and *Recurring Cost* in a watershed project, giving **one example** of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Direct Capital Cost ($C_{\text{DC}}$) | Recurring Cost ($C_{\text{RC}}$) |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Definition** | One-time expenditure incurred during project construction. *(0.5)* | Expenditure incurred repeatedly during the project life. *(0.5)* |
| **Occurrence** | Years 1–3 (Entry Phase). *(0.5)* | Years 4–20 (Maintenance Phase). *(0.5)* |
| **Example** | Construction of a check dam / percolation tank. *(0.5)* | Wages paid for desilting the check dam annually. *(0.5)* |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Pattern, Module 4]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for the formulation of a watershed management project proposal**, including pre-feasibility, feasibility, and DPR stages. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A watershed of **800 hectares** is to be developed with a total budget of **₹96,00,000** over **5 years**. The expected annual benefits are **₹22,00,000** and annual recurring costs are **₹4,00,000**. Calculate the **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** at a **10% discount rate** over a **20-year project life**. Comment on the project's viability. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — Procedure for Formulation (7 Marks)

The formulation of a watershed project proposal proceeds through three well-defined stages:

**Stage 1 — Pre-Feasibility / Reconnaissance (2 marks)**

- Identification of watershed using Survey of India topo-sheets and remote sensing data.
- Preliminary walk-through surveys.
- Stakeholder identification.
- Rough cost estimates.

**Stage 2 — Detailed Feasibility (3 marks)**

- **Topographical survey** using DGPS / Total Station.
- **Soil survey** (texture, depth, erodibility).
- **Hydrological analysis** (rainfall, runoff, groundwater).
- **PRA / socio-economic baseline survey.**
- GIS-based thematic map preparation.

**Stage 3 — DPR Formulation & Submission (2 marks)**

- Consolidation of data into the 9-section proposal structure.
- Selection of engineering and biological interventions.
- Detailed cost estimation.
- Economic appraisal (BCR / NPV).
- Submission to funding agency for technical and financial appraisal.

##### Part (b) — BCR Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Watershed area $A = 800$ ha
- Total budget = ₹96,00,000 → Initial capital $C_0 = 96{,}00{,}000$ ₹
- Annual benefit $B_t = 22{,}00{,}000$ ₹
- Annual recurring cost $M = 4{,}00{,}000$ ₹
- Discount rate $i = 10\% = 0.10$
- Project life $n = 20$ years

**Step 1 — Net Annual Benefit**

$$\text{Net Annual Benefit} = B_t - M = 22{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = 18{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹}$$

**Step 2 — Present Worth Factor (PWF) for Annuity**

$$\text{PWF} = \frac{(1.10)^{20} - 1}{0.10 \cdot (1.10)^{20}}$$

Compute $(1.10)^{20} = 6.7275$.

$$\text{PWF} = \frac{6.7275 - 1}{0.10 \times 6.7275} = \frac{5.7275}{0.67275} = 8.5136$$

**Step 3 — PW of Benefits**

$$\text{PW}_{\text{Benefits}} = 18{,}00{,}000 \times 8.5136 = 15{,}32{,}44{,}800 \; \text{₹}$$

**Step 4 — PW of Costs**

Since the initial cost occurs at $t = 0$:

$$\text{PW}_{\text{Costs}} = 96{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹}$$

**Step 5 — BCR**

$$BCR = \frac{15{,}32{,}44{,}800}{96{,}00{,}000} = 15.96$$

**Step 6 — Verdict**

Since **BCR = 15.96 >> 1.0**, the project is **highly economically viable and strongly recommended for sanction**. The discounted return is approximately 16 times the discounted cost.

**Additional Insight (Bonus mark):** The per-hectare cost is:

$$C_{ha} = \frac{96{,}00{,}000}{800} = 12{,}000 \; \text{₹/ha}$$

which lies within the standard NABARD normative range of ₹12,000–15,000/ha — further validating the project's financial soundness.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a)** Describe the **structure and contents of a Detailed Project Report (DPR)** for a watershed management programme. Highlight the role of the **SWOT analysis** within the proposal. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **components of project cost** in a watershed programme with typical percentage allocations. A check dam costs **₹2,50,000** to construct, has a useful life of **20 years**, salvage value of **₹25,000**, and an annual maintenance cost of **₹8,000**. Compute its **annualized cost** at a **10% discount rate**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — DPR Structure and SWOT Role (7 Marks)

**Structure of DPR (5 marks — list and brief explanation of each):**

1. **Executive Summary** — Concise one-page overview.
2. **Introduction and Justification** — Need for the project.
3. **Baseline Profile** — Physical, biological, socio-economic data.
4. **Problem Statement and SWOT Analysis** — Strategic diagnosis.
5. **Objectives and Logical Framework** — SMART goals.
6. **Proposed Interventions** — Engineering and biological.
7. **Cost Estimates and Funding Pattern** — Item-wise budget.
8. **Implementation Mechanism** — Institutional framework, Gantt chart.
9. **Monitoring and Evaluation Plan** — Indicators, frequency, agencies.
10. **Expected Outcomes and Sustainability Plan.**

**Role of SWOT (2 marks):**

- **S**trengths — Internal positives (e.g., community participation, fertile soil).
- **W**eaknesses — Internal constraints (e.g., lack of technical staff).
- **O**pportunities — External enablers (e.g., convergence with MGNREGA, market access).
- **T**hreats — External risks (e.g., climate change, drought, market price volatility).

The SWOT analysis helps prioritize interventions, align resources, and propose mitigation strategies for identified risks.

##### Part (b) — Annualized Cost Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Construction cost $C = 2{,}50{,}000$ ₹
- Useful life $n = 20$ years
- Salvage value $S = 25{,}000$ ₹
- Annual maintenance cost $M = 8{,}000$ ₹
- Discount rate $i = 10\% = 0.10$

**Step 1 — Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**

$$\text{CRF} = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} = \frac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{20}}{(1.10)^{20} - 1}$$

Using $(1.10)^{20} = 6.7275$:

$$\text{CRF} = \frac{0.10 \times 6.7275}{6.7275 - 1} = \frac{0.67275}{5.7275} = 0.11746$$

**Step 2 — Sinking Fund Factor (SFF)**

$$\text{SFF} = \frac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1} = \frac{0.10}{5.7275} = 0.01746$$

**Step 3 — Annualized Cost**

$$\text{Annualized Cost} = C \cdot \text{CRF} + M - S \cdot \text{SFF}$$

Substituting values:

$$\text{Annualized Cost} = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.11746 + 8{,}000 - 25{,}000 \times 0.01746$$

Compute each term step-by-step:

$$= 29{,}365 + 8{,}000 - 436.50 = 36{,}928.50 \; \text{₹/year}$$

**Final Answer:** The annualized cost of the check dam is approximately **₹36,929 per year**.

**Engineering Interpretation (Bonus):** This annualized cost, when distributed over the irrigation benefit area or the cropped area served by the check dam, gives the per-hectare burden, which can be compared against the agricultural net income to verify economic sustainability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the discount rate** in BCR/NPV calculations — always use the **present worth** form, not undiscounted sums. *(–2 marks typical penalty)*
> 2. **Confusing "annual benefit" with "net annual benefit."** The recurring cost MUST be subtracted first.
> 3. **Omitting units** in the final answer (₹/year, ₹/ha) — KTU examiners deduct marks for this.
> 4. **Writing the formula for CRF or PWF incorrectly** — memorize and write the algebraic form cleanly, not in words.
> 5. **Ignoring the salvage value** in the annualized cost formula — this is a frequent omission.
> 6. **Listing components of a project proposal but not explaining their purpose** — simply naming sections fetches only partial marks; a one-line description of *why* each section is needed is required for full credit.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Project Proposal = administrative + technical + financial document** seeking approval for a watershed programme.
- A watershed proposal follows the **nine-section structure**: Executive Summary → Background → Baseline → SWOT → Objectives → Interventions → Cost → Implementation → M&E.
- **Three planning phases**: Pre-feasibility → Feasibility → DPR Formulation.
- **Cost components** ($C_{\text{total}}$): Direct Capital (60–70%) + Recurring (15–20%) + M&E (5–7%) + Contingency (3–5%).
- **Per-hectare normative cost** under IWMP / NABARD ≈ **₹12,000–15,000 / ha** (benchmark).
- **BCR formula**: ratio of discounted benefits to discounted costs — accept if **BCR ≥ 1.0**.
- **NPV formula**: difference between discounted benefits and discounted costs — accept if **NPV > 0**.
- **IRR**: discount rate at which NPV = 0 — accept if IRR > MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return).
- **Annualized cost** of a structure = $C \cdot \text{CRF} + M - S \cdot \text{SFF}$.
- **CRF** = $\dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$; **SFF** = $\dfrac{i}{(1+i)^n - 1}$.
- **Payback period** = Total Investment / Annual Net Cash Inflow.
- Always state the **discount rate** (typically 10–12% in Indian rural projects).
- **Participatory approach** (PRA, Gram Sabha, SHG involvement) is a **mandatory** component, not optional.
- **SWOT analysis** integrates internal strengths/weaknesses with external opportunities/threats — crucial for strategic intervention design.
- **Monitoring & Evaluation (M&E)** must include both **process indicators** (e.g., no. of check dams built) and **outcome indicators** (e.g., rise in groundwater table, % increase in crop yield).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benefit Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET741 - WATERSHED DEVELOPMENT AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Benefit Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Benefit Analysis** in watershed management is a systematic, quantitative, and qualitative appraisal methodology used to evaluate the total positive outcomes (benefits) generated from a watershed development project against the resources invested, considering the **time value of money** and the **project life cycle**.

According to the standard watershed economics framework, it is the process of identifying, classifying, measuring, and monetizing the stream of incremental benefits (agricultural, environmental, social, and ecological) accruing from interventions such as check dams, contour bunds, afforestation, terracing, and percolation tanks over the operational lifespan of the project.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Benefit Analysis forms the economic backbone of Module 4. It is essential for **project appraisal, feasibility reporting, and prioritization of watersheds** for funding by agencies such as the Ministry of Jal Shakti, NABARD, and watershed development boards.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a farmer, **Ravi**, who spends **₹1,00,000** to build a small check dam across a seasonal stream on his land. He doesn't get any return in Year 1 (construction year). But from Year 2 onwards, the well recharges, he gets a second crop, his soil retains moisture, and his cattle get water year-round.

**Question:** Was spending ₹1,00,000 worth it?

If Ravi simply adds up all the benefits (₹30,000/year for 10 years = ₹3,00,000) and compares with cost (₹1,00,000), he gets a profit of ₹2,00,000. But this is **wrong** — because money received in Year 5 is not worth the same as money today (inflation, opportunity cost).

**Benefit Analysis** is the formal financial tool that:
1. **Counts** all the benefits (yields, water saved, carbon sequestered, etc.).
2. **Discounts** them to a common point in time (Present Value).
3. **Compares** them with the cost stream using ratios (BCR), absolute differences (NPV), or break-even interest (IRR).

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Memory Hook:** *"Benefits earned tomorrow are smaller than benefits earned today — Benefit Analysis is the engine that shrinks future money and weighs it against today's cost."*

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Benefit** | Any positive, measurable output from the watershed project (yield, recharge, fuel, fodder, carbon credits). |
| **Cost** | Total expenditure on construction, maintenance, and operation over project life. |
| **Discount Rate (i)** | The opportunity cost of capital, expressed as a percentage (usually **8–12%** in KTU/government watershed projects). |
| **Project Life (n)** | Operational lifespan of the watershed intervention, typically **20–25 years** for soil and water conservation structures. |
| **Net Benefit** | Incremental benefit minus incremental cost in any given year. |
| **Sinking Fund Factor** | Used to convert annual maintenance cost to its equivalent annual value. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical/Economic Constants Used:** Standard discount rates adopted by the **Planning Commission of India** for watershed projects generally range between **8% and 12%**. A benchmark project life of **n = 25 years** is commonly adopted for major watershed schemes like MGNREGA-WDC, IWMP, and PMKSY.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Watershed Benefits

The benefits from a watershed development project are categorized into the following hierarchical levels:

1. **Tangible Benefits (Direct)** — Easily measurable in monetary terms.
   - Increased agricultural yield (kg/ha → ₹).
   - Additional area brought under cultivation.
   - Milk/dairy increments.
   - Fodder and fuel wood sold.
2. **Tangible Benefits (Indirect)** — Measurable but not directly produced.
   - Groundwater recharge (m³ saved).
   - Reduction in flood damage.
   - Reduced soil erosion (tonnes/ha/yr).
   - Drought mitigation savings.
3. **Intangible Benefits (Non-monetary)** — Difficult to assign a price.
   - Improved biodiversity.
   - Aesthetic/ecological upliftment.
   - Carbon sequestration.
   - Improved quality of life / social cohesion.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board examinations, students are often asked to **list and explain 5–6 categories of benefits**. Memorizing the table above is high-yield.

---

### 2.2 The Time Value of Money — Foundation of Benefit Analysis

A rupee earned today is worth more than a rupee earned 10 years from now, because today's rupee can be **invested** and earn interest, or because of **inflation**.

This principle is mathematically expressed through **discounting**. For an amount $B_t$ received at the end of year $t$, its Present Value (PV) at discount rate $i$ is:

$$PV = \frac{B_t}{(1+i)^t}$$

For a uniform annual benefit $B$ received for $n$ years, the **Present Worth of Benefits (PWB)** is computed using the **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF)**:

$$PWB = B \times \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$$

---

### 2.3 Steps of Conducting Benefit Analysis (Logical Sequence)

- **Step 1 — Baseline Survey:** Determine pre-project agricultural yield, water table, and income.
- **Step 2 — With-Project Scenario:** Estimate post-project outputs (using crop-cutting experiments or hydrological models).
- **Step 3 — Compute Incremental Benefits:** Subtract baseline from with-project yield for each year.
- **Step 4 — Project Life Estimation:** Adopt $n = 20$ to $25$ years for major structures.
- **Step 5 — Discounting:** Convert all future benefits and costs to Present Value using discount rate $i$.
- **Step 6 — Apply Decision Criteria:** Calculate BCR, NPV, and IRR.
- **Step 7 — Sensitivity Analysis:** Test the project for variations in discount rate, cost overrun, or benefit underestimation.
- **Step 8 — Recommendation:** Approve, revise, or reject the watershed intervention.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Present Worth of Benefits | $PWB = B \times \dfrac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$ | $B$ = Annual Benefit, $i$ = Discount rate, $n$ = Years | ₹ |
| 2 | Present Worth of Costs | $PWC = C_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{M_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | $C_0$ = Initial Cost, $M_t$ = Annual Maintenance | ₹ |
| 3 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = PWB - PWC$ | Difference of PW of benefits and costs | ₹ |
| 4 | **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** | $BCR = \dfrac{PWB}{PWC}$ | Ratio of PW of benefits to PW of costs | Dimensionless |
| 5 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | Set $NPV = 0$ and solve for $i$ | Discount rate where NPV = 0 | % |
| 6 | Annual Equivalent of Capital Cost | $A = C_0 \times \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Capital Recovery Factor (CRF) | ₹/year |
| 7 | Annualized Cost (incl. Maintenance) | $AC = A + M$ | $A$ from (6) + annual maintenance $M$ | ₹/year |
| 8 | Annual Benefit (Equivalent) | $AB = PWB \times \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ | Converts PWB back to uniform annual | ₹/year |
| 9 | Incremental Yield | $\Delta Y = Y_{with} - Y_{without}$ | Yield with project − Yield without | kg/ha |
| 10 | Incremental Income | $\Delta I = \Delta Y \times P_c$ | $P_c$ = Price of crop per kg | ₹/ha |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Memorize the **USPWF** numerator $(1+i)^n - 1$ and denominator $i(1+i)^n$ **in this exact order**. Examiners frequently award marks for correctly writing the formula even when the numerical substitution is incomplete.

---

### 2.5 Decision Criteria (KTU Board Valuation)

| Criterion | Accept Project? |
|---|---|
| **NPV > 0** | ✅ Yes — Project is economically viable. |
| **BCR ≥ 1** | ✅ Yes — Benefits exceed or equal costs. |
| **BCR > 1.5** | ✅ Highly desirable (often required by NABARD). |
| **IRR > MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return)** | ✅ Yes — Usually MARR = 12% for rural projects. |
| **NPV < 0 or BCR < 1** | ❌ Reject or redesign. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These same three metrics (NPV, BCR, IRR) are universally applied in **hydropower project appraisal**, **irrigation command area evaluation**, **afforestation carbon finance projects**, and **rural road PMGSY schemes**. Mastering Benefit Analysis in watershed context opens the door to all of these.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Present Worth of Benefits (PWB)

**Statement to Prove:**
For a uniform annual benefit $B$ received at the end of every year for $n$ years, the Present Worth at discount rate $i$ is given by the Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF).

**Derivation:**

Let $B$ be received at the end of years $1, 2, 3, \ldots, n$.

The Present Value of benefit received at the end of year $t$ is:

$$PV_t = \frac{B}{(1+i)^t}$$

Summing over $n$ years:

$$PWB = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{B}{(1+i)^t}$$

This is a geometric progression with first term $a = \dfrac{B}{1+i}$ and common ratio $r = \dfrac{1}{1+i}$.

The sum of a geometric series is:

$$S_n = a \cdot \frac{1 - r^n}{1 - r}$$

Substituting:

$$PWB = \frac{B}{1+i} \cdot \frac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+i}\right)^n}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i}}$$

Simplify the denominator:

$$1 - \frac{1}{1+i} = \frac{(1+i) - 1}{1+i} = \frac{i}{1+i}$$

So:

$$PWB = \frac{B}{1+i} \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{\dfrac{i}{1+i}}$$

The $(1+i)$ terms cancel:

$$PWB = B \cdot \frac{1 - (1+i)^{-n}}{i}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1+i)^n$:

$$PWB = B \cdot \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$$

$$\boxed{PWB = B \times \frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}}$$

This is the **Uniform Series Present Worth Factor (USPWF)** used universally in KTU board problems.

---

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem Statement:**

A watershed development project in Wayanad, Kerala involves the construction of **contour bunds, a percolation tank, and afforestation on 200 ha**. The estimated costs and benefits are:

- **Initial Capital Cost (Year 0):** ₹8,00,000
- **Annual Maintenance Cost:** ₹20,000 (from Year 1 to Year 25)
- **Annual Incremental Benefit:** ₹1,20,000 (assumed uniform from Year 1 to Year 25)
- **Discount Rate:** $i = 10\%$
- **Project Life:** $n = 25$ years

Compute the **NPV, BCR, and comment on project viability**.

---

**Step 1 — Compute USPWF for $i = 10\%$, $n = 25$**

$$USPWF = \frac{(1.10)^{25} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{25}}$$

Calculate $(1.10)^{25}$:

$$(1.10)^{25} \approx 10.8347$$

So:

$$USPWF = \frac{10.8347 - 1}{0.10 \times 10.8347} = \frac{9.8347}{1.08347}$$

$$USPWF \approx 9.0770$$

**Step 2 — Compute Present Worth of Benefits (PWB)**

$$PWB = B \times USPWF = 1,20,000 \times 9.0770$$

$$PWB \approx ₹10,89,240$$

**Step 3 — Compute Present Worth of Costs (PWC)**

Present Worth of annual maintenance = ₹20,000 × 9.0770 = ₹1,81,540.

Add initial capital cost:

$$PWC = 8,00,000 + 1,81,540 = ₹9,81,540$$

**Step 4 — Compute NPV**

$$NPV = PWB - PWC = 10,89,240 - 9,81,540$$

$$\boxed{NPV = ₹1,07,700}$$

Since **NPV > 0**, the project is **economically viable**.

**Step 5 — Compute BCR**

$$BCR = \frac{PWB}{PWC} = \frac{10,89,240}{9,81,540}$$

$$\boxed{BCR \approx 1.11}$$

Since **BCR > 1**, the project generates more benefits than costs and is **recommended for approval**.

---

### 3.3 Computation of Annualized Benefit & Annualized Cost (Alternative Approach)

Sometimes the KTU examiner asks for the **Annual Equivalent** analysis. Let us redo the same example.

**Capital Recovery Factor (CRF):**

$$CRF = \frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1} = \frac{0.10 \times 10.8347}{10.8347 - 1} = \frac{1.08347}{9.8347}$$

$$CRF \approx 0.11017$$

**Annual Equivalent of Capital Cost:**

$$A = 8,00,000 \times 0.11017 = ₹88,136$$

**Total Annual Cost:**

$$AC = A + M = 88,136 + 20,000 = ₹1,08,136$$

**Annual Benefit** = ₹1,20,000 (already uniform).

**Annual BCR:**

$$BCR_{annual} = \frac{1,20,000}{1,08,136} = 1.110$$

This matches our earlier BCR of 1.11 — confirming the answer.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Useful for Board Answers)

| Method | What it Asks | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NPV** | Is the project profitable in absolute ₹? | Best indicator of wealth creation. | Sensitive to choice of discount rate. |
| **BCR** | Is every ₹1 of cost worth it? | Easy to interpret, scales to project size. | Can mislead when comparing projects of different scale. |
| **IRR** | What rate of return does the project earn? | Communicates efficiency in % terms. | Multiple IRRs possible for non-conventional cash flows. |
| **Payback Period** | How long to recover the cost? | Very simple. | Ignores time value of money (unless discounted). |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Benefit Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Proposal: Watershed Intervention] --> B[Baseline Survey: Pre-Project Conditions]
    B --> C[Identification of Benefit Categories]
    C --> D[Tangible Direct Benefits]
    C --> E[Tangible Indirect Benefits]
    C --> F[Intangible Benefits]
    D --> G[Quantification in Monetary Units]
    E --> G
    F --> H[Qualitative Scoring Matrix]
    G --> I[Discounting at Rate i over n years]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Present Worth of Benefits PWB]
    K[Cost Stream: Capital + Maintenance] --> L[Present Worth of Costs PWC]
    J --> M[Compute NPV]
    L --> M
    J --> N[Compute BCR]
    L --> N
    M --> O[Decision: NPV greater than 0]
    N --> P[Decision: BCR greater than 1]
    O --> Q[Project Approved]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Sensitivity Analysis: Vary i and n]
    R --> S[Final Recommendation to Funding Agency]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Map: Decision Logic of Benefit-Cost Evaluation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Benefit Analysis] --> B[Compute PWB using USPWF]
    A --> C[Compute PWC including initial and annual cost]
    B --> D{Calculate NPV}
    C --> D
    D --> E{NPV greater than 0?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Project is Viable]
    E -->|No| G[Project is Rejected]
    B --> H{Calculate BCR}
    C --> H
    H --> I{BCR greater than 1?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Benefits Exceed Costs]
    I -->|No| K[Costs Exceed Benefits]
    F --> L[Proceed to IRR Computation]
    J --> L
    L --> M{IRR greater than MARR?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Recommend for Funding]
    M -->|No| O[Redesign or Reject]
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic: Time-Value-of-Money Discounting in Watershed Projects

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Y0[Year 0: Capital Outflow] -->|High weight| PV[Present Value Anchor]
    Y1[Year 1 Benefit B1] -->|Discount factor 1.10| PV
    Y2[Year 2 Benefit B2] -->|Discount factor 1.21| PV
    Y3[Year 3 Benefit B3] -->|Discount factor 1.331| PV
    Yn[Year n Benefit Bn] -->|Discount factor 1.10 power n| PV
    PV --> Decision{Compare PWB vs PWC}
    Decision -->|PWB greater| Approve[Project Approved]
    Decision -->|PWB smaller| Reject[Project Rejected]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define Benefit Analysis in the context of watershed development. List any four categories of benefits.*

**Model Answer:**

Benefit Analysis is the systematic economic evaluation of all positive outcomes — both tangible and intangible — accruing from a watershed development project, discounted to present value and compared with project costs.

Four categories of benefits:

1. **Direct Tangible Benefits:** Increased crop yield, additional cropping area, higher milk yield.
2. **Indirect Tangible Benefits:** Groundwater recharge, reduced soil erosion, flood damage reduction.
3. **Intangible Benefits:** Biodiversity enhancement, carbon sequestration, aesthetic value.
4. **Social Benefits:** Improved rural employment, reduced migration, better health and sanitation.

*(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Distinguish between Net Present Value (NPV) and Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) as decision criteria in watershed project appraisal.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | NPV | BCR |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Absolute difference between Present Worth of Benefits and Present Worth of Costs. | Ratio of Present Worth of Benefits to Present Worth of Costs. |
| Unit | Monetary (₹) | Dimensionless number. |
| Interpretation | Measures net wealth created. | Measures efficiency per rupee invested. |
| Decision Rule | Accept if NPV > 0. | Accept if BCR > 1. |
| Limitation | Sensitive to discount rate selection. | May mislead when comparing projects of different scale. |

*(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Stream A**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*A watershed development project in Idukki district involves construction of a percolation tank, 5 check dams, and afforestation over 150 ha. The estimated initial capital cost is ₹6,00,000. The annual maintenance cost is ₹15,000 and the uniform annual incremental benefit is ₹95,000. The project life is 25 years and the discount rate is 10%.*

**(a)** Compute the Net Present Value (NPV) of the project. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Compute the Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) and comment on the economic viability of the project. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — USPWF for i = 10%, n = 25**

$$USPWF = \frac{(1.10)^{25} - 1}{0.10 \times (1.10)^{25}} = \frac{10.8347 - 1}{1.08347} = 9.0770$$

**Step 2 — Present Worth of Benefits (PWB)**

$$PWB = 95,000 \times 9.0770 = ₹8,62,315$$

**Step 3 — Present Worth of Costs (PWC)**

Present Worth of annual maintenance:

$$PW_M = 15,000 \times 9.0770 = ₹1,36,155$$

Total PWC:

$$PWC = 6,00,000 + 1,36,155 = ₹7,36,155$$

**Step 4 — NPV**

$$NPV = PWB - PWC = 8,62,315 - 7,36,155 = \boxed{₹1,26,160}$$

Since **NPV > 0**, the project is **economically viable**.

---

**Part (b) — Benefit-Cost Ratio**

$$BCR = \frac{PWB}{PWC} = \frac{8,62,315}{7,36,155} = \boxed{1.17}$$

**Comment:** Since **BCR = 1.17 > 1**, every rupee invested in the watershed project returns ₹1.17 in present-valued benefits. The project is recommended for implementation.

*(14 marks total: 7 + 7)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Stream B**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**
*A rainwater harvesting structure is proposed for a 100 ha microwatershed in Palakkad. The capital cost is ₹4,50,000, the annual maintenance is ₹12,000, and the annual incremental benefit is ₹80,000. Take the project life as 20 years and the discount rate as 12%.*

**(a)** Compute the Present Worth of Benefits (PWB) and Present Worth of Costs (PWC). *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Determine the NPV and BCR. State whether the project should be accepted. *(7 marks)*

---

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — USPWF for i = 12%, n = 20**

$$USPWF = \frac{(1.12)^{20} - 1}{0.12 \times (1.12)^{20}}$$

Compute $(1.12)^{20}$:

$$(1.12)^{20} \approx 9.6463$$

Therefore:

$$USPWF = \frac{9.6463 - 1}{0.12 \times 9.6463} = \frac{8.6463}{1.1576} = 7.4694$$

**Step 2 — Present Worth of Benefits (PWB)**

$$PWB = 80,000 \times 7.4694 = ₹5,97,552$$

**Step 3 — Present Worth of Costs (PWC)**

Present Worth of maintenance:

$$PW_M = 12,000 \times 7.4694 = ₹89,633$$

Total PWC:

$$PWC = 4,50,000 + 89,633 = ₹5,39,633$$

**Step 4 — NPV**

$$NPV = PWB - PWC = 5,97,552 - 5,39,633 = \boxed{₹57,919}$$

Since **NPV > 0**, project is viable.

**Step 5 — BCR**

$$BCR = \frac{PWB}{PWC} = \frac{5,97,552}{5,39,633} = \boxed{1.11}$$

Since **BCR > 1**, project is **accepted**.

*(14 marks total: 7 + 7)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the initial capital cost** when computing PWC — examiners specifically look for $C_0 + PW_M$. Omitting $C_0$ loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Reversing the USPWF formula:** Writing $\dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$ when PWB is required. This is the Capital Recovery Factor (CRF), used for converting cost to annual equivalent — NOT for PWB.
> 3. **Wrong decimal placement** in $(1+i)^n$ — at $i = 10\%, n = 25$, the value is $10.8347$, NOT $1.8347$ or $9.8347$.
> 4. **Not stating the decision** (whether to accept/reject) — board examiners allocate **1–2 marks** explicitly for the recommendation line. Always end with *"Since BCR > 1, the project is recommended."*
> 5. **Mixing up years** — if construction takes 2 years, the benefit stream starts only from Year 3. Always re-check the timeline.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Benefit Analysis** = Quantification + Discounting + Comparison of benefits vs. costs of a watershed project.
- ✅ **Time Value of Money** is the foundation: a rupee today > a rupee tomorrow.
- ✅ **Three Golden Decision Criteria:** NPV > 0, BCR > 1, IRR > MARR.
- ✅ **USPWF formula (must memorize):**

$$\frac{(1+i)^n - 1}{i(1+i)^n}$$

- ✅ **CRF formula (capital recovery, reciprocal of USPWF):**

$$\frac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n - 1}$$

- ✅ **Standard Discount Rate** for watershed projects in India: **8–12%**.
- ✅ **Standard Project Life:** **20–25 years** for major soil/water conservation structures.
- ✅ **Benefit Categories:** Tangible Direct, Tangible Indirect, Intangible, Social.
- ✅ **Annual Equivalent Method** is an alternative way to compare benefits and costs uniformly each year.
- ✅ **Always state your recommendation** explicitly — NPV > 0 AND BCR > 1 → *"Project is recommended for implementation."*
- ✅ **Sensitivity Analysis** (varying $i$ or $n$) is a bonus mark-earning discussion point.
- ✅ **Incremental Benefit** = Yield/Income *with* project minus yield/income *without* project — this is the only benefit that should be counted.
- ✅ **Intangible benefits** (biodiversity, carbon) may be included via **willingness-to-pay** or **hedonic pricing**, but KTU board questions usually restrict you to monetary (tangible) benefits.

---

*End of Module 4 — Benefit Analysis | KTU 2024 Scheme PGAET741*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PGAET741 - WATERSHED DEVELOPMENT AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Preparation of Detailed Project Report (DPR) for Watershed Projects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — Preparation of Detailed Project Report (DPR) for Watershed Projects

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Detailed Project Report (DPR)** for a watershed project is a comprehensive, scientifically engineered, and financially justified document that consolidates **baseline surveys, resource inventories, hydrological assessments, engineering designs, treatment plans, cost estimates, institutional arrangements, and socio-economic impact analysis** into a single, technically vetted proposal. As per the **Department of Land Resources (DoLR), Ministry of Rural Development, Government of India** and the **Integrated Watershed Management Programme (IWMP) / PMKSY-WDC** guidelines, a DPR is the **mandatory technical, administrative, and financial blueprint** required before any watershed development intervention is sanctioned, funded, or executed.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a DPR is the **linking document** that converts a *Watershed Development Plan* (scientific/technical) into an *executable, time-bound, budgeted project proposal* that satisfies the **Course Outcomes (COs)** related to planning, design, and management.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Blueprint of a House"

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a DPR as the **architectural blueprint, cost estimate, contractor agreement, and 5-year maintenance plan for a house** — all rolled into one document.
>
> Just as a builder cannot lay a single brick without a sanctioned plan, an implementing agency (DRDA / State Watershed Cell / NGO) cannot spend a single rupee of public money without a sanctioned DPR.

| Element in House Construction | Equivalent in Watershed DPR |
|---|---|
| Soil testing of plot | Baseline survey (land use, soil, slope, hydrology) |
| Architectural drawings | Watershed treatment plan (contour bunds, check dams, plantations) |
| Bill of Quantities (BOQ) | Abstract estimate & detailed cost sheet |
| Structural design | Engineering design of structures (gabion, weir, percolation tank) |
| Sanction by Municipality | Sanction by District Watershed Committee / SLNA |
| Loan approval by Bank | Funding by NABARD / MoRD / State Budget |

---

## 1.3 Why a DPR is the Heart of Watershed Projects

1. **Statutory Requirement** — A DPR is mandatory under **PMKSY-WDC 2015 guidelines** and **DoLR operational guidelines**.
2. **Funding Trigger** — No Central / State release under **WDC-PMKSY** without an approved DPR.
3. **Technical Legitimacy** — Ensures the project is based on **scientific watershed characterization** using remote sensing and GIS.
4. **Accountability** — Establishes clear **M&E (Monitoring & Evaluation)** indicators and exit protocol.

> [!TIP]
> KTU Examiner Insight: When a 14-mark question asks *"Discuss the components of a DPR"* — students are expected to recall the **standard 13-chapter format** prescribed by DoLR for IWMP/PMKSY projects. Memorize the chapter sequence.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Structure, Philosophy & Logic Behind DPR

## 2.1 Hierarchical Logic of DPR Preparation

The DPR is **not a single document but a 13-chapter compendium** arranged in a strict logical cascade. Each chapter feeds the next:

- **Layer 1 — Diagnostic:** Chapters 1–4 (basic data, baseline)
- **Layer 2 — Analytical:** Chapters 5–7 (problem identification)
- **Layer 3 — Planning:** Chapters 8–10 (treatment plan, cost)
- **Layer 4 — Executive:** Chapters 11–13 (institutional, O&M, M&E)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Skipping any layer breaks the **logical chain of evidence** required by appraisal agencies (NABARD, NITI Aayog, Ministry).

---

## 2.2 Standard 13-Chapter DPR Format (DoLR / PMKSY-WDC Compliant)

| Ch. No. | Chapter Title | Purpose / Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Executive Summary** | One-page snapshot for the Minister / Secretary |
| 2 | **Introduction & Background** | Genesis, location, geo-coordinates, accessibility |
| 3 | **Basic Data of the Watershed** | Area, villages, households, demographic profile |
| 4 | **Baseline Survey & Resource Inventory** | Land use, soil, slope, geology, hydrogeology, vegetation |
| 5 | **Analysis of Existing Systems** | Gap analysis of existing water/land/forest resources |
| 6 | **Problem Identification** | SWOT, PRA, focused group discussions |
| 7 | **Treatment Plan / Watershed Treatment Schedule** | Activity-wise physical & financial targets |
| 8 | **Engineering Design & Cost Estimation** | Hydraulic structures, BOQ, rate analysis |
| 9 | **Financial Plan & Funding Pattern** | Central:State:Beneficiary share |
| 10 | **Economic & Social Analysis** | BCR, NPV, IRR, employment generation |
| 11 | **Institutional Arrangement & Capacity Building** | WC, WCDC, PIA, SHGs |
| 12 | **Operation, Maintenance & Sustainability** | O&M fund, community ownership |
| 13 | **Monitoring, Evaluation & Learning** | M&E indicators, MIS, third-party evaluation |

---

## 2.3 Stepwise DPR Preparation Workflow

1. **Reconnaissance survey** — Preliminary site visit, stakeholder consultation.
2. **Data collection** — Toposheets (1:50,000), satellite imagery (LISS-III / Sentinel-2), rainfall data (IMD), soil maps (NBSS&LUP).
3. **GIS & Remote Sensing mapping** — Delineation of watershed boundary, drainage network, slope classes, land-use classification.
4. **Detailed Project Report (DPR) preparation** — Using the 13-chapter format above.
5. **Appraisal by District Watershed Development Agency (DWDA)** → Approval by **State Level Nodal Agency (SLNA)**.
6. **Sanction & fund release.**
7. **Implementation, M&E, and completion report.**

> [!NOTE]
> The **appraisal hierarchy** is: **Gram Sabha → Watershed Committee → WCDC → DWDA → SLNA → DoLR (Gol)**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Financial & Cost Estimation)

> [!TIP]
> Financial formulas are the **most commonly tested 3–5 mark sub-questions** in KTU exams. Master every equation below.

### 2.4.1 Cost Escalation Formula

$$
C_f = C_p \times \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n
$$

Where:
- $C_f$ = Future cost (₹)
- $C_p$ = Present cost (₹)
- $r$ = Annual rate of escalation (%)
- $n$ = Number of years

### 2.4.2 Net Present Value (NPV)

$$
NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{B_t}{(1+i)^t} \;-\; \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}
$$

### 2.4.3 Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)

$$
BCR = \dfrac{\sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{B_t}{(1+i)^t}}{\sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+i)^t}}
$$

Decision rule: **Accept project if BCR > 1**.

### 2.4.4 Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

IRR is the discount rate $i^*$ at which $NPV = 0$:

$$
\sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(B_t - C_t)}{(1 + i^*)^t} = 0
$$

### 2.4.5 Quantities of Earthwork (for bunds/terraces)

$$
V = A \times L
$$

Where $V$ = Volume of earthwork (m³), $A$ = Cross-sectional area (m²), $L$ = Length (m).

### 2.4.6 Capacity of a Check Dam / Percolation Tank

$$
Q = A \times R
$$

Where $Q$ = Volume impounded (m³), $A$ = Catchment area (m²), $R$ = Runoff depth (m).

### 2.4.7 Area Treatment Cost (Standard Metric)

$$
\text{Unit Cost} = \dfrac{\text{Total Project Cost}}{\text{Total Watershed Area}}
$$

The **DoLR benchmark** is approximately **₹12,000 to ₹15,000 per hectare** (as per PMKSY-WDC 2015, revised periodically).

---

### 2.4.8 Consolidated KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Unit | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cost Escalation | $C_f = C_p (1 + r/100)^n$ | ₹ | Future value of cost |
| 2 | NPV | $\sum B_t/(1+i)^t - \sum C_t/(1+i)^t$ | ₹ | Project viability |
| 3 | BCR | $\Sigma PV(B) / \Sigma PV(C)$ | ratio | Benefit-cost analysis |
| 4 | IRR | $\Sigma (B_t - C_t)/(1+i^*)^t = 0$ | % | Discount rate at NPV=0 |
| 5 | Earthwork Volume | $V = A \times L$ | m³ | Bund / trench design |
| 6 | Storage Capacity | $Q = A \times R$ | m³ | Check dam sizing |
| 7 | Unit Cost | Total Cost / Area | ₹/ha | Budget norm check |
| 8 | Cut-off Ratio | Earthwork : Cost ratio | — | Sanity check |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- For **NABARD-funded watershed projects**, the DPR must demonstrate **BCR > 1.0** and **IRR > 10%** (banking norms).
- For **PMKSY-WDC**, the DPR must follow the **Common Guidelines for Watershed Development Projects 2008 / 2011 / 2015** (revised).
- For **MGNREGS convergence**, the DPR provides the shelf of labour-intensive works.

---

# 📘 3. Step-by-Step Analysis — Worked Numerical & DPR Cost Estimation Walkthrough

## 3.1 Sample Problem — Cost Estimation of an Earthen Bund

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Design a contour earthen bund on a 15% slope for a watershed of 250 ha. The bund cross-section is trapezoidal with top width **0.5 m**, bottom width **2.5 m**, and height **0.75 m**. The horizontal spacing (vertical interval × cot α) gives a bund length of **1,200 m**. Calculate **(a) earthwork volume, (b) cost @ ₹350 per m³, (c) cost per hectare, (d) cost escalated 3 years later at 6% p.a.**

### Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of trapezoidal bund

$$
A = \dfrac{(a + b)}{2} \times h
$$

Substituting values:

$$
A = \dfrac{(0.5 + 2.5)}{2} \times 0.75
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{3.0}{2} \times 0.75
$$

$$
A = 1.5 \times 0.75 = 1.125 \; \text{m}^2
$$

### Step 2 — Total volume of earthwork

$$
V = A \times L
$$

$$
V = 1.125 \times 1200 = 1350 \; \text{m}^3
$$

### Step 3 — Present cost @ ₹350 / m³

$$
C_p = V \times \text{Rate}
$$

$$
C_p = 1350 \times 350 = \text{₹} \; 4{,}72{,}500
$$

### Step 4 — Cost per hectare

$$
\text{Unit Cost} = \dfrac{C_p}{\text{Area}} = \dfrac{4{,}72{,}500}{250}
$$

$$
\text{Unit Cost} = \text{₹} \; 1{,}890 \; \text{per hectare}
$$

### Step 5 — Escalated cost after 3 years @ 6% p.a.

$$
C_f = C_p \left(1 + \dfrac{r}{100}\right)^n
$$

$$
C_f = 4{,}72{,}500 \times \left(1 + 0.06\right)^3
$$

$$
C_f = 4{,}72{,}500 \times (1.191016)
$$

$$
C_f = \text{₹} \; 5{,}62{,}696 \; (\text{approximately})
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked NPV / BCR — A Mini Watershed Project

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A watershed treatment project requires an investment of **₹5 lakh in Year 0**. Estimated benefits (yield increase, milk, fodder, groundwater recharge valuation) for 5 years are: Year 1: ₹80,000, Year 2: ₹1.2 lakh, Year 3: ₹1.5 lakh, Year 4: ₹2.0 lakh, Year 5: ₹2.0 lakh. Discount rate **i = 10%**. Compute **(a) NPV** and **(b) BCR**.

### Step 1 — Discount factors

$$
DF_t = \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^t}
$$

| Year (t) | Cash Flow B_t (₹) | Discount Factor | PV (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | -5,00,000 (cost) | 1.0000 | -5,00,000 |
| 1 | 80,000 | 0.9091 | 72,728 |
| 2 | 1,20,000 | 0.8264 | 99,168 |
| 3 | 1,50,000 | 0.7513 | 1,12,695 |
| 4 | 2,00,000 | 0.6830 | 1,36,600 |
| 5 | 2,00,000 | 0.6209 | 1,24,180 |

### Step 2 — Total PV of Benefits

$$
\sum PV(B) = 72{,}728 + 99{,}168 + 1{,}12{,}695 + 1{,}36{,}600 + 1{,}24{,}180
$$

$$
\sum PV(B) = \text{₹} \; 5{,}45{,}371
$$

### Step 3 — Total PV of Costs

$$
\sum PV(C) = \text{₹} \; 5{,}00{,}000
$$

### Step 4 — Net Present Value

$$
NPV = \sum PV(B) - \sum PV(C)
$$

$$
NPV = 5{,}45{,}371 - 5{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
NPV = \text{₹} \; 45{,}371
$$

**Decision:** Since **NPV > 0**, the project is **financially viable**.

### Step 5 — Benefit-Cost Ratio

$$
BCR = \dfrac{\sum PV(B)}{\sum PV(C)}
$$

$$
BCR = \dfrac{5{,}45{,}371}{5{,}00{,}000} = 1.09
$$

**Decision:** Since **BCR > 1**, accept the project.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note: In real DoLR/NABARD projects, **BCR > 1.5** is preferred for priority funding under PMKSY-WDC.

---

## 3.3 DPR Content Writing Template — Chapter-by-Chapter Logic

| Chapter | Logic Flow | Key Data Inputs |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Executive Summary | What + Why + How much | 1-page abstract |
| 2. Introduction | Geography + jurisdiction | Lat/long, district, block |
| 3. Basic Data | Demographic baseline | Census, village profile |
| 4. Resource Inventory | Scientific baseline | Soil, slope, drainage |
| 5. Existing Systems | Gap analysis | Cropping pattern, runoff |
| 6. Problem Identification | PRA / SWOT | Stakeholder issues |
| 7. Treatment Plan | Solution design | Bunds, plantations, tanks |
| 8. Engineering Design | Hydraulic/structural | Sizes, drawings, BOQ |
| 9. Financial Plan | Funding pattern | Central : State ratio |
| 10. Economic Analysis | BCR / NPV / IRR | Discounted cash flow |
| 11. Institutional Plan | Governance | WC, SHG, PIA |
| 12. O&M Plan | Sustainability | O&M fund corpus |
| 13. M&E Plan | Learning & accountability | Indicators, frequency |

---

## 3.4 Funding Pattern (PMKSY-WDC 2015 Norms)

> [!TIP]
> A frequently asked 3-mark question: *"What is the funding pattern under PMKSY-WDC?"*

| Component | Centre : State | Beneficiary |
|---|---|---|
| Non-tribal plains | 60 : 40 | Nil (component-wise) |
| Tribal / Hilly / NE | 90 : 10 | Nil |
| Convergence (MGNREGA) | — | Labour component |

**Project life:** 4–7 years (depending on watershed size and complexity).
